# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2.1 Essential Radial Cam Terminology (KTU Board-Exam-Grade)

Below is the **complete, exam-ready vocabulary** of a radial cam. Each term is defined with a clear *physical meaning* and a *typical symbol*.

### 2.1.1 Geometry of the Cam

**1. Axis of the Cam**
- The geometric centre line about which the cam rotates. All radial measurements originate from the cam's **centre point** (foot of the axis on the disc plane).

**2. Centre of the Cam ($O$)**
- The point where the cam's axis of rotation intersects the disc plane. Denoted as $O$ on all cam drawings.

**3. Trace Point**
- A *reference* point on the follower (often the **centre of the roller** for a roller follower, or a sharp point on a knife-edge follower) whose path traces the cam profile.

> [!NOTE]
> The trace point is a **mathematical reference**; the actual contact point is at the roller periphery (different by a fixed distance = roller radius).

**4. Base Circle**
- The **smallest circle** drawn tangent to the cam profile, centred at $O$.
- **Diameter / Radius** denoted as $D_b$ / $r_b$.
- Represents the cam surface when the follower is at its **lowest position (zero lift)**.
- Determines the *minimum size* of the cam and governs the curvature at the start of lift.

**5. Pitch Circle**
- A **reference circle** drawn through the **pitch point** (see below) — i.e., the circle on which the pitch point lies.
- **Diameter / Radius** = $D_p$ / $r_p$.
- Used as the **reference** for measuring the follower lift in graphical construction.

**6. Prime Circle**
- The **smallest circle** drawn tangent to the cam profile when the follower is at its **maximum lift** position.
- **Diameter / Radius** = $D_p'$ / $r_p'$.
- Important for clearance considerations in the mechanism housing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For an **on-centre (radial) follower**, the pitch circle and the base circle are often **identical** when no dwell-with-lift is specified. The distinction is critical when the follower begins to lift from a non-zero radius.

**7. Pitch Point ($P$)**
- The point on the pitch circle at which the follower motion is **pure radial** (i.e., the pressure angle is theoretically **zero**). Used as the **reference for tracing the profile**.

**8. Pitch Curve (or Pitch Profile)**
- The locus of the **trace point** as the cam rotates one full revolution with the follower in perfect contact.
- For a knife-edge follower, the pitch curve **=** the cam profile.
- For a roller follower, the pitch curve is **smaller than the cam profile by the roller radius**.

**9. Working Curve / Cam Profile**
- The actual physical surface of the cam which contacts the follower.
- For a knife-edge follower, working curve = pitch curve.
- For a roller follower, the working curve is the **offset curve** of the pitch curve by the roller radius (offset outward).

### 2.1.2 Motion Terminology

**10. Follower Lift (or Stroke, $S$)**
- The **maximum displacement** of the follower from its lowest to its highest position.
- Symbol: $S$ or $h_{\max}$, unit: mm.
- Equals the **radial distance between the base circle and the prime circle** for an on-centre follower:
  $S = r_p' - r_b$.

**11. Follower Dwell**
- A period during which the follower remains **stationary** (no lift) while the cam continues to rotate.
- **Outer Dwell** (or *top dwell*): follower at maximum lift, $S$ = constant.
- **Inner Dwell** (or *bottom dwell*): follower at zero lift, $S = 0$.

**12. Follower Rise (or Outstroke)**
- The portion of the cam rotation during which the follower moves from the **lowest to the highest** position.
- Cam angle subtended: $\theta_o$ (for outstroke).

**13. Follower Return (or Return Stroke / Instroke)**
- The portion during which the follower returns from the **highest to the lowest** position.
- Cam angle subtended: $\theta_r$ (for return).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One complete cycle of a typical cam = Outstroke + Outer Dwell + Return + Inner Dwell**
> Cam angle sum: $\theta_o + \theta_d + \theta_r + \theta_i = 360°$ (for a full one-revolution cam).

### 2.1.3 Force / Direction Terms

**14. Pressure Angle ($\alpha$ or $\phi$)**
- The angle between the **direction of motion of the follower** and the **normal to the pitch curve at the point of contact**.
- Governs the side-thrust on the follower and hence the magnitude of the driving force.
- Smaller $\alpha$ → smoother force transmission. Practically limited to $\alpha \le 30°$ for translating followers.

**15. Eccentricity ($e$)**
- The perpendicular distance between the **cam axis** and the **line of motion of an offset follower**.
- $e = 0$ → on-centre (radial) follower; $e > 0$ → off-set follower.

**16. Line of Stroke (or Axis of Follower)**
- The straight line along which the follower translates (for a translating follower).

## 2.2 Follower Types — Quick Reference

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 emphasis:** Examiners expect you to draw the follower shape and label *all* the terms on a labelled diagram.

| Follower Type | Contact Geometry | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Knife-edge follower** | Point contact at a sharp edge | Simplest, ideal for theory | High wear, no practical use |
| **Roller follower** | Line contact between roller & cam | Reduced wear, common in practice | Profile must be offset, more complex |
| **Flat-faced follower** | Surface contact | No side thrust, durable | High contact stress |
| **Spherical-faced follower** | Point contact (3-D) | Tolerates misalignment | Complex profile |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Base circle radius | $r_b$ | Smallest radius tangent to cam profile | mm |
| 2 | Pitch circle radius | $r_p$ | Radius through pitch point | mm |
| 3 | Prime circle radius | $r_p'$ | Smallest circle tangent to cam profile at max lift | mm |
| 4 | Maximum lift / Stroke | $S$ | $S = r_p' - r_b$ | mm |
| 5 | Total cam angle | $\theta_{tot}$ | $\theta_o + \theta_d + \theta_r + \theta_i = 360°$ | deg |
| 6 | Follower displacement (SHM) | $y(\theta)$ | $y = \dfrac{S}{2}\Big[1 - \cos\Big(\dfrac{\pi \theta}{\theta_o}\Big)\Big]$ | mm |
| 7 | Follower velocity (SHM) | $v$ | $v = \dfrac{\pi S \omega}{2 \theta_o} \sin\Big(\dfrac{\pi \theta}{\theta_o}\Big)$ | mm/s |
| 8 | Follower acceleration (SHM) | $a$ | $a = \dfrac{\pi^{2} S \omega^{2}}{2 \theta_o^{2}} \cos\Big(\dfrac{\pi \theta}{\theta_o}\Big)$ | mm/s² |
| 9 | Uniform velocity displacement | $y(\theta)$ | $y = \dfrac{S}{\theta_o}\theta$ | mm |
| 10 | Uniform accel/decel | $y(\theta)$ | $y = 2S(\theta/\theta_o)^2$ (accel. half) | mm |
| 11 | Cycloidal displacement | $y(\theta)$ | $y = S\Big[\dfrac{\theta}{\theta_o} - \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sin\Big(\dfrac{2\pi \theta}{\theta_o}\Big)\Big]$ | mm |
| 12 | Eccentricity | $e$ | Perp. distance cam axis to follower axis | mm |
| 13 | Pressure angle | $\alpha$ | $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{dy/d\theta \pm e}{r_b + y}$ | rad/deg |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Tip:** In the SHM displacement formula, *forget the factor ½* and you'll lose 2 marks immediately. Memorise: $y = \tfrac{S}{2}[1 - \cos(\pi\theta/\theta_o)]$, *not* $S[1-\cos(\pi\theta/\theta_o)]$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive valve camshafts** — radial cam with translating roller follower.
- **IC engine fuel injection pumps** — radial cam with flat-faced follower.
- **Textile machinery** — automatic cam controls loom shuttle / needle motion.
- **Printing presses** — synchronised cam profiles for paper feed.
- **Automatic copying machines** — multi-cam control systems.
- **Watch escapements** (older mechanical watches) — precision cam profiles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The radial cam with a translating roller follower is the **single most important machine element** taught in Module 2 — exam questions are guaranteed. Learn the terminology, the motion profile equations, and the offset construction.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3.1 Derivation of the Pressure-Angle Equation (Translating Offset Roller Follower)

This derivation is *the* cornerstone formula for any radial-cam analysis problem.

### 3.1.1 Setup and Geometry

Let:
- $O$ = centre of cam
- $A$ = instantaneous contact point of roller with cam
- $A'$ = centre of roller (the trace point)
- $N$ = common normal at the point of contact $A$ (perpendicular to the cam surface)
- $\overline{AA'} = r_r$ (roller radius)
- $e$ = eccentricity (perpendicular distance from $O$ to the follower line of stroke)
- $\phi$ = angle subtended by the cam during follower rise (i.e., $\theta_o$ for outstroke)
- $y$ = follower lift at the current cam angle
- $\omega$ = angular velocity of cam (rad/s)
- $v$ = velocity of follower (mm/s) = $dy/dt = (dy/d\theta)\cdot \omega$

### 3.1.2 Velocity Decomposition

The velocity $v$ of the trace point $A'$ is directed **along the line of stroke** (vertical, in standard diagrams). At the point of contact, the velocity of the contact point on the **cam** is perpendicular to $OA$ and equal in magnitude (rolling-without-slipping idealisation).

Resolving $v$ into components along and perpendicular to the **normal** $N$:

$$
v_N \;=\; v \cos\alpha \quad \text{(component along normal)}
$$

where $\alpha$ is the angle between the direction of follower motion and the normal $N$ — *this is the pressure angle*.

### 3.1.3 Geometric Relation from Triangle $OAA'$

Draw the perpendicular from $A'$ to the normal $NA$. The roller centre $A'$ lies at distance $r_r$ from $A$, perpendicular to the tangent at $A$ (i.e., along the normal $N$). Hence the distance from $O$ to the line $NA$ is $(r_b + y)$, where $r_b$ is the base-circle radius and $y$ is the lift.

Projecting the roller-radius vector $\overline{AA'}$ onto the direction perpendicular to the line of stroke:

$$
\text{(perpendicular component of } r_r \text{)} \;=\; r_r \sin\alpha
$$

This must equal the eccentricity shift visible in the velocity triangle:

$$
r_r \sin\alpha \;=\; \frac{dy}{d\theta} \pm e
$$

The sign convention:
- Use **(+)** when the **normal tilts towards the cam centre** as the follower rises.
- Use **(−)** when the normal tilts **away**.

### 3.1.4 Final Pressure-Angle Expression

Dividing both sides by the term representing the geometry along the line of stroke, $(r_b + y)$:

$$
\tan\alpha \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{dy}{d\theta} \pm e}{r_b + y}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For an ON-CENTRE follower ($e = 0$):**
> $$\tan\alpha \;=\; \frac{dy/d\theta}{r_b + y}$$

This is the formula KTU examiners expect in every part-(a) of a Module-2 cam problem.

## 3.2 Derivation of the SHM Displacement Equation

The follower displacement in **Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)** must satisfy:

- $y = 0$ at $\theta = 0$
- $y = S$ at $\theta = \theta_o$
- Velocity $v = 0$ at both ends of the stroke (start and end of lift)

**Step 1: General SHM equation** (sinusoidal in time)

$$
y \;=\; A_0 - A\cos(\omega_0 t)
$$

**Step 2: Substitute** $t = \theta/\omega$ and rename constants

$$
y \;=\; A_0 - A\cos\!\left(\frac{\omega_0}{\omega}\theta\right)
$$

**Step 3: Apply boundary conditions**

- At $\theta = 0$, $y = 0$:
  $$0 = A_0 - A\cos(0) = A_0 - A \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_0 = A$$

- At $\theta = \theta_o$, $y = S$:
  $$S = A - A\cos\!\left(\frac{\omega_0 \theta_o}{\omega}\right) = A\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{\omega_0 \theta_o}{\omega}\right)\right]$$

For $S$ to be a finite value with $A_0 = A$, the cosine term must equal $-1$, i.e.:

$$
\frac{\omega_0 \theta_o}{\omega} \;=\; \pi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_0 \;=\; \frac{\pi \omega}{\theta_o}
$$

**Step 4: Back-substitute**

$$
y \;=\; A - A\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\theta_o}\right) = A\!\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\theta_o}\right)\right]
$$

**Step 5: Determine $A$**

Since $S = A[1 - \cos(\pi)] = A[1 - (-1)] = 2A$:

$$
A \;=\; \frac{S}{2}
$$

**Step 6: Final SHM displacement equation**

$$
\boxed{\;y(\theta) \;=\; \frac{S}{2}\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\theta_o}\right)\right]\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is **standard for KTU Part-B questions** — examiners often give 4–6 marks for a clean derivation.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — SHM Cam Profile (KTU-Style)

**Given Data:**
- Base-circle radius $r_b = 40$ mm
- Maximum lift $S = 25$ mm
- Outstroke angle $\theta_o = 90°$ (cam rotates at uniform $\omega = 20$ rad/s)
- Dwell for $60°$ at top
- Return angle $\theta_r = 120°$ (Uniform Velocity)
- Dwell for $90°$ at bottom
- Follower: Translating roller follower, roller radius $r_r = 8$ mm
- Follower is **on-centre** ($e = 0$)

**Step 1: Verify angle sum**
$$\theta_o + \theta_d + \theta_r + \theta_i = 90° + 60° + 120° + 90° = 360° \;\;\checkmark$$

**Step 2: Prime circle radius**
$$r_p' = r_b + S = 40 + 25 = 65 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: SHM displacement table** for outstroke ($\theta = 0°$ to $90°$ in steps of $15°$)

The formula is $y = \dfrac{S}{2}\Big[1 - \cos\!\big(\dfrac{\pi \theta}{\theta_o}\big)\Big] = 12.5\big[1 - \cos(2\theta°)\big]$ (since $\pi/90° = 2°$ in radian conversion with $\theta$ in degrees)

| $\theta$ (°) | $\pi\theta/\theta_o$ (rad) | $\cos(\pi\theta/\theta_o)$ | $y$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 1.0000 | 0.00 |
| 15 | 0.5236 | 0.8660 | 1.67 |
| 30 | 1.0472 | 0.5000 | 6.25 |
| 45 | 1.5708 | 0.0000 | 12.50 |
| 60 | 2.0944 | −0.5000 | 18.75 |
| 75 | 2.6180 | −0.8660 | 23.33 |
| 90 | 3.1416 | −1.0000 | 25.00 |

**Step 4: Compute derivative $dy/d\theta$ at each outstroke angle**

The derivative:
$$\frac{dy}{d\theta} = \frac{S}{2}\cdot \frac{\pi}{\theta_o}\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\theta_o}\right) = 12.5 \cdot \frac{\pi}{90°}\sin(2\theta°)$$

For $\theta$ in radians, $\theta_o = \pi/2$ rad, so:
$$\frac{dy}{d\theta} = 12.5 \cdot 2 \cdot \sin(2\theta) = 25\sin(2\theta) \quad \text{(in mm/rad)}$$

| $\theta$ (°) | $\sin(\pi\theta/\theta_o)$ | $dy/d\theta$ (mm/rad) | $\tan\alpha$ | $\alpha$ (°) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0000 | 0.00 | 0 | 0.00 |
| 15 | 0.5000 | 12.50 | 12.50/41.67 = 0.300 | 16.70 |
| 30 | 0.8660 | 21.65 | 21.65/46.25 = 0.468 | 25.10 |
| 45 | 1.0000 | 25.00 | 25.00/52.50 = 0.476 | 25.46 |
| 60 | 0.8660 | 21.65 | 21.65/58.75 = 0.369 | 20.24 |
| 75 | 0.5000 | 12.50 | 12.50/63.33 = 0.197 | 11.16 |
| 90 | 0.0000 | 0.00 | 0 | 0.00 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Maximum pressure angle** for SHM outstroke occurs at $\theta = \theta_o/2 = 45°$. This is a key conceptual point — the **mid-stroke position is the worst-case** for force transmission, not the start or end.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Follower Motion Types

| Feature | Uniform Velocity | SHM | Uniform Accel/Decel | Cycloidal |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Displacement eq.** | $y = (S/\theta_o)\theta$ | $\tfrac{S}{2}[1-\cos(\pi\theta/\theta_o)]$ | $2S(\theta/\theta_o)^2$ (1st half) | $S[\theta/\theta_o - \tfrac{1}{2\pi}\sin(2\pi\theta/\theta_o)]$ |
| **Max velocity** | $S\omega/\theta_o$ | $\tfrac{\pi S \omega}{2\theta_o} \approx 1.571 S\omega/\theta_o$ | $2S\omega/\theta_o$ | $2S\omega/\theta_o$ |
| **Max acceleration** | $\infty$ (sudden reversal) | $\tfrac{\pi^2 S \omega^2}{2\theta_o^2} \approx 4.93 S\omega^2/\theta_o^2$ | $4S\omega^2/\theta_o^2$ | $6.28 S\omega^2/\theta_o^2$ |
| **Velocity continuity at start/end** | Discontinuous → infinite accel | Continuous, $v=0$ at both ends | Continuous | Continuous |
| **Acceleration continuity** | Discontinuous | Continuous, but changes sign abruptly | Discontinuous at midpoint | Continuous everywhere |
| **Suitability** | Low-speed only | Medium-speed (most common) | Moderate-speed | High-speed, smoothest |
| **Jerk (rate of change of accel)** | Discontinuous (shock) | Discontinuous at mid-stroke | Discontinuous at mid-stroke | Continuous (zero jerk!) |
| **KTU board pick-frequency** | Common | Very common | Common | Common (especially in Module 2) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cycloidal motion is the "gold standard"** for high-speed cams (like racing engine camshafts) because its **jerk is zero throughout the entire stroke** — giving the smoothest possible force transmission.

## 4.2 Concept Flow — One Cam Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2

    InnerDwell --> Outstroke: At theta_i
    Outstroke --> OuterDwell: At theta_i + theta_o
    OuterDwell --> ReturnStroke: At theta_i + theta_o + theta_d

    note right of Outstroke
        y increases from 0 to S
        SHM / Cycloidal / etc.
    end note

    note right of OuterDwell
        y = S (constant)
        follower stationary
    end note

    note right of ReturnStroke
        y decreases from S to 0
        motion profile defined
    end note
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model QP]**
**Define the following terms with a neat sketch of a radial cam with a roller follower:**
**(a) Base circle  (b) Pitch curve  (c) Pressure angle**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Base Circle:** The smallest circle drawn tangent to the cam profile, centred at the cam's rotation centre $O$. Radius $r_b$. It defines the cam's *minimum size* and represents the follower position at zero lift.

**(b) Pitch Curve:** The path traced by a chosen *trace point* on the follower (typically the roller centre) as the cam completes one full revolution with the follower in perfect contact. For a roller follower, the pitch curve lies **inside** the cam profile by exactly the roller radius $r_r$.

**(c) Pressure Angle ($\alpha$):** The angle between the **direction of motion of the follower** and the **normal to the pitch curve at the instantaneous point of contact**. It governs the side-thrust on the follower and the magnitude of the driving force. Practically, $\alpha \le 30°$.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model QP]**
**Differentiate between:**
**(a) Base circle and Prime circle**
**(b) Outstroke and Return stroke**
**(c) Radial follower and Offset follower**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Base Circle vs Prime Circle:**

| Base Circle | Prime Circle |
|---|---|
| Smallest circle tangent to cam profile at **minimum lift** ($y = 0$) | Smallest circle tangent to cam profile at **maximum lift** ($y = S$) |
| Radius $r_b$ | Radius $r_p' = r_b + S$ |
| Defines cam's minimum size | Defines cam's overall size at top position |

**(b) Outstroke vs Return Stroke:**

- **Outstroke**: Cam rotation during which the follower moves from the **lowest to the highest** position. Cam angle = $\theta_o$.
- **Return stroke**: Cam rotation during which the follower moves from the **highest back to the lowest** position. Cam angle = $\theta_r$.

**(c) Radial (On-centre) vs Offset Follower:**

- **Radial follower**: Line of motion of follower passes through the cam's rotation centre $O$ (eccentricity $e = 0$).
- **Offset follower**: Line of motion of follower is at a perpendicular distance $e > 0$ from the cam's centre. Reduces pressure angle in the rising portion — useful in high-speed cams.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part-B question offers an internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Both are compulsory-style (students choose one). Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2, Model QP]**

A radial cam operating an IC engine exhaust valve is driven at **1800 rpm**. The cam rotates clockwise and operates a **translating roller follower** through a single lift per revolution. The following motion specifications apply:

- **Outstroke (SHM):** $\theta_o = 60°$, lift $S = 20$ mm
- **Dwell at top:** $\theta_d = 30°$
- **Return stroke (Uniform Acceleration & Deceleration):** $\theta_r = 90°$
- **Dwell at bottom:** $\theta_i = 180°$
- **Roller radius:** $r_r = 12$ mm
- **Base circle radius:** $r_b = 50$ mm
- **Follower is on-centre** ($e = 0$)

**(a)** Draw a neat labelled sketch of the cam with all terminology marked. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Determine the **maximum velocity and maximum acceleration** of the follower during the outstroke and return stroke. Also calculate the **maximum pressure angle** during the outstroke. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $N = 1800$ rpm $\Rightarrow \omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1800}{60} = 188.5$ rad/s
- $S = 20$ mm, $r_b = 50$ mm, $r_r = 12$ mm, $e = 0$

**Part (a) — Labelled Sketch (7 marks)**

*Refer to the Mermaid Block Diagram in Section 4.3.* The student must mark on a clean 2-D drawing:

- Cam centre $O$
- Base circle of radius $r_b = 50$ mm
- Prime circle of radius $r_p' = r_b + S = 70$ mm
- Pitch point $P$ on pitch circle (coincident with base circle for on-centre follower)
- Roller of radius $r_r = 12$ mm in contact with cam profile
- Follower line of stroke passing through $O$
- Normal $N$ at a contact point
- Pressure angle $\alpha$ between $N$ and the line of stroke
- All angles $\theta_o = 60°$, $\theta_d = 30°$, $\theta_r = 90°$, $\theta_i = 180°$ marked on a small reference circle.

**Part (b) — Kinematic Analysis (7 marks)**

**Step 1: SHM outstroke — maximum velocity**

For SHM: $v_{\max} = \dfrac{\pi S \omega}{2 \theta_o}$ (with $\theta_o$ in radians)

Convert $\theta_o = 60° = \pi/3$ rad:

$$
v_{\max, \text{out}} \;=\; \frac{\pi \times 20 \times 188.5}{2 \times (\pi/3)} \;=\; \frac{20 \times 188.5 \times 3}{2} \;=\; 5655 \text{ mm/s}
$$

**Step 2: SHM outstroke — maximum acceleration**

For SHM: $a_{\max} = \dfrac{\pi^2 S \omega^2}{2 \theta_o^2}$

$$
a_{\max, \text{out}} \;=\; \frac{\pi^2 \times 20 \times (188.5)^2}{2 \times (\pi/3)^2} \;=\; \frac{9 \times 20 \times (188.5)^2}{2}
$$

$$
a_{\max, \text{out}} \;=\; \frac{180 \times 35532.25}{2} \;=\; 3{,}197{,}902 \text{ mm/s}^2 \approx 3.20 \text{ km/s}^2
$$

**Step 3: Uniform Accel/Decel return — maximum velocity**

For UAD: $v_{\max} = \dfrac{2 S \omega}{\theta_r}$ (with $\theta_r$ in radians)

Convert $\theta_r = 90° = \pi/2$ rad:

$$
v_{\max, \text{ret}} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 20 \times 188.5}{\pi/2} \;=\; \frac{4 \times 20 \times 188.5}{\pi} \;=\; \frac{15080}{\pi}
$$

$$
v_{\max, \text{ret}} \;=\; 4800 \text{ mm/s}
$$

**Step 4: Uniform Accel/Decel return — maximum acceleration**

For UAD: $a_{\max} = \dfrac{4 S \omega^2}{\theta_r^2}$

$$
a_{\max, \text{ret}} \;=\; \frac{4 \times 20 \times (188.5)^2}{(\pi/2)^2} \;=\; \frac{80 \times 35532.25 \times 4}{\pi^2}
$$

$$
a_{\max, \text{ret}} \;=\; \frac{11{,}370{,}320}{9.8696} \;\approx\; 1{,}152{,}000 \text{ mm/s}^2 \approx 1.15 \text{ km/s}^2
$$

**Step 5: Maximum pressure angle during SHM outstroke**

For an on-centre follower, the maximum pressure angle for SHM occurs at $\theta = \theta_o/2 = 30°$:

$$
\left.\frac{dy}{d\theta}\right|_{\max} \;=\; \frac{S\pi}{2\theta_o} \;=\; \frac{20 \times \pi}{2 \times \pi/3} \;=\; 30 \text{ mm/rad}
$$

Lift at mid-stroke: $y = S/2 = 10$ mm. Hence:

$$
\tan\alpha_{\max} \;=\; \frac{30}{r_b + 10} \;=\; \frac{30}{50 + 10} \;=\; \frac{30}{60} \;=\; 0.5
$$

$$
\alpha_{\max} \;=\; \tan^{-1}(0.5) \;\approx\; 26.57°
$$

Since $26.57° < 30°$ — **the design is acceptable** for a translating roller follower.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Model QP]**

A radial cam with an **oscillating roller follower** is to be designed. The cam rotates at **300 rpm** counter-clockwise. Specifications:

- **Follower swings** through a total angle $\psi_{\max} = 30°$ about its pivot
- **Outstroke (SHM):** $\theta_o = 120°$
- **Dwell at top:** $\theta_d = 30°$
- **Return stroke (Cycloidal):** $\theta_r = 150°$
- **Dwell at bottom:** $\theta_i = 60°$
- **Distance from cam centre to follower pivot:** $L = 120$ mm
- **Roller radius:** $r_r = 10$ mm
- **Base circle radius:** $r_b = 40$ mm

**(a)** Sketch the radial cam with the oscillating roller follower, marking all terminology. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Derive the **SHM displacement equation** for the follower and compute the **maximum angular velocity of the follower** during outstroke. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**Given:** $\omega = 2\pi \times 300 / 60 = 31.416$ rad/s; $\psi_{\max} = 30° = \pi/6$ rad.

**Part (a) — Sketch (7 marks):**

- Cam centre $O$
- Follower pivot $P_f$ at distance $L = 120$ mm from $O$
- Base circle radius $r_b = 40$ mm centred at $O$
- The follower's line of motion is along the arc of radius $L$, with the roller tracing a circular arc.
- Mark the roller radius $r_r = 10$ mm
- The angle of swing $\psi$ is measured from the line $OP_f$
- Show the cam profile enveloping the roller positions

**Part (b) — SHM Displacement Derivation (7 marks):**

For SHM, the angular displacement of the follower $\psi$ as a function of cam angle $\theta$ during outstroke:

**Step 1:** The general SHM form is
$$\psi(\theta) = A_0 - A\cos(\omega_0 t) \quad \text{where } t = \theta/\omega$$

**Step 2:** Boundary conditions:
- $\psi(0) = 0$ ⟹ $A_0 = A$
- $\psi(\theta_o) = \psi_{\max}$ ⟹ $\psi_{\max} = A(1 - \cos(\pi)) = 2A$ ⟹ $A = \psi_{\max}/2$

**Step 3:** Substitute back:
$$
\boxed{\;\psi(\theta) = \frac{\psi_{\max}}{2}\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi \theta}{\theta_o}\right)\right]\;}
$$

**Step 4:** Maximum angular velocity occurs at $\theta = \theta_o/2$:

$$
\left.\frac{d\psi}{d\theta}\right|_{\max} = \frac{\psi_{\max}}{2} \cdot \frac{\pi}{\theta_o} = \frac{\pi \psi_{\max}}{2\theta_o}
$$

With $\theta_o = 120° = 2\pi/3$ rad:

$$
\left.\frac{d\psi}{d\theta}\right|_{\max} = \frac{\pi \times (\pi/6)}{2 \times (2\pi/3)} = \frac{\pi^2/6}{4\pi/3} = \frac{\pi}{8}
$$

Angular velocity:
$$
\dot{\psi}_{\max} = \frac{d\psi}{d\theta}\cdot \omega = \frac{\pi}{8}\times 31.416 = 12.34 \text{ rad/s}
$$

Convert to rpm: $12.34 \times 60/(2\pi) = 117.8$ rpm.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Unit consistency for $\theta_o$:** When plugging into $v_{\max} = \pi S\omega/(2\theta_o)$, **convert $\theta_o$ to radians** first. Mixing degrees and radians in the same equation is the #1 mark-loss reason in cam problems.
> 2. **Sign of the lift $y$:** Always treat $y$ as a *positive* number measured from the base circle outward. The pressure-angle equation $\tan\alpha = (dy/d\theta \pm e)/(r_b+y)$ uses the absolute value of $y$ in the denominator.
> 3. **Confusing pitch curve with cam profile:** In roller followers, the **pitch curve is offset inward** by the roller radius. Forgetting this distinction in profile-construction sub-parts costs 2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the dwell angles:** A "single-lift-per-revolution" cam with $\theta_o + \theta_d + \theta_r + \theta_i$ must sum to $360°$. Cross-verify before starting any calculation.
> 5. **Maximum pressure angle location:** Students often compute $\alpha$ at the **start** or **end** of the stroke, but for SHM, $\alpha_{\max}$ occurs at the **mid-stroke** ($\theta = \theta_o/2$). Marks lost: 1–2 per part.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Radial Cam Terminology (Module 2)**

- [ ] **Cam** = driver link with irregular profile; **follower** = driven link.
- [ ] **Radial cam**: follower motion is *radial* (in plane perpendicular to cam axis).
- [ ] **Centre of cam $O$**: point of intersection of axis of rotation with the disc plane.
- [ ] **Base circle** radius $r_b$: smallest circle tangent to the cam profile.
- [ ] **Pitch circle** radius $r_p$: circle passing through the pitch point.
- [ ] **Prime circle** radius $r_p'$: smallest circle tangent to profile at **maximum lift**.
- [ ] **Maximum lift** $S = r_p' - r_b$.
- [ ] **Trace point**: reference point on follower (typically roller centre).
- [ ] **Pitch curve**: locus of trace point during one revolution.
- [ ] **Cam profile** = offset of pitch curve by roller radius (roller follower) or = pitch curve (knife-edge follower).
- [ ] **Pitch point $P$**: point on pitch circle where follower motion is purely radial ($\alpha = 0$).
- [ ] **Outstroke** $\theta_o$: follower rises; **Return stroke** $\theta_r$: follower returns.
- [ ] **Outer dwell** $\theta_d$ (top), **Inner dwell** $\theta_i$ (bottom).
- [ ] **Total: $\theta_o + \theta_d + \theta_r + \theta_i = 360°$**.
- [ ] **Pressure angle** $\alpha$: angle between follower direction and normal at contact.
- [ ] **For on-centre follower:** $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{dy/d\theta}{r_b + y}$.
- [ ] **For offset follower:** $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{dy/d\theta \pm e}{r_b + y}$ (sign depends on normal tilt).
- [ ] **Limit:** $\alpha \le 30°$ for translating followers (practical design rule).
- [ ] **Eccentricity $e$**: perpendicular distance from $O$ to the line of stroke (zero for on-centre).
- [ ] **SHM displacement:** $y = \tfrac{S}{2}\big[1 - \cos(\pi\theta/\theta_o)\big]$ — always check the factor ½.
- [ ] **SHM max velocity:** $v_{\max} = \pi S\omega / (2\theta_o)$ at $\theta = \theta_o/2$.
- [ ] **SHM max acceleration:** $a_{\max} = \pi^2 S \omega^2 / (2\theta_o^2)$ at $\theta = 0$ and $\theta = \theta_o$.
- [ ] **Uniform velocity:** $y = (S/\theta_o)\theta$ — infinite accel at stroke ends → use only for slow cams.
- [ ] **Uniform Accel/Decel:** $v_{\max} = 2S\omega/\theta_r$; $a_{\max} = 4S\omega^2/\theta_r^2$.
- [ ] **Cycloidal:** smoothest, zero jerk throughout stroke — $y = S[\theta/\theta_o - \tfrac{1}{2\pi}\sin(2\pi\theta/\theta_o)]$.
- [ ] **Roller follower** → profile = pitch curve offset by $r_r$ outward.
- [ ] **Knife-edge follower** → profile = pitch curve.
- [ ] **Flat-faced follower** → no side thrust, surface contact, good for high contact loads.
- [ ] **Real-world uses:** IC engine valves, fuel pumps, textile machines, printing presses.
- [ ] **Convert all angles to radians** before plugging into kinematic formulas — common pitfall.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Displacement, Velocity, Acceleration Diagrams for Uniform Velocity, Uniform Acceleration and SHM. Cam Profile Synthesis (Knife Edge Follower)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET404 - THEORY OF MACHINES AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Displacement, Velocity, Acceleration Diagrams for Uniform Velocity, Uniform Acceleration and SHM. Cam Profile Synthesis (Knife Edge Follower)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of a Cam and Follower

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CAM:** A cam is a rotating or sliding mechanical element (typically with an irregular shape) that imparts a prescribed motion to another element called the **follower**, by means of direct contact. The shape of the cam profile directly dictates the motion characteristics of the follower.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FOLLOWER:** The follower is the driven member that makes contact with the cam and executes the desired motion (translation or oscillation) as dictated by the cam profile.

A **Knife-Edge Follower** is the simplest type of translating follower in which the contacting surface is a sharp edge (theoretical point contact). It is used purely for kinematic analysis since real-world applications would experience severe wear and stress concentration at the edge.

### 1.2 Displacement, Velocity, and Acceleration Diagrams

These three diagrams are the graphical representation of the follower's kinematic behavior as a function of the cam's angular rotation ($\theta$ or $\phi$):

- **Displacement Diagram (s vs $\theta$):** The fundamental input. Shows the position of the follower at every angular position of the cam during one complete cycle (rise, dwell, return, dwell).
- **Velocity Diagram (v vs $\theta$):** The first derivative of displacement, $v = \frac{ds}{dt} = \omega \cdot \frac{ds}{d\theta}$, showing how fast the follower moves.
- **Acceleration Diagram (a vs $\theta$):** The second derivative, $a = \frac{d^2s}{dt^2} = \omega^2 \cdot \frac{d^2s}{d\theta^2}$, showing the rate of change of velocity.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **sewing machine needle** rising and falling in a precise pattern. The cam is the oval-shaped wheel driven by the motor's main shaft, and the needle-rod is the follower. As the wheel spins, the needle doesn't move at a constant speed — it accelerates, dwells at the top, decelerates, and dwells at the bottom. 

The **displacement diagram** is the *blueprint* of the needle's motion path over time. The **velocity diagram** tells you *how fast* the needle is moving at each instant. The **acceleration diagram** tells you *how jerky* the motion is — high acceleration means the needle (and the mechanism attached to it) is being stressed, which causes vibration, noise, wear, and fatigue in real machines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams matter in engineering:** In high-speed machinery (automotive engines, textile machines, printing presses), poorly designed cam profiles cause **inertia forces** that can shake the entire machine apart. The acceleration diagram is the designer's "stress map" — flatter acceleration curves = smoother operation = longer machine life.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Base Circle (r$_b$):** The smallest circle that can be drawn tangent to the cam profile. The follower's initial position rests on this circle.
- **Prime Circle (r$_p$):** The circle of radius equal to the minimum distance from the cam center to the roller/knife edge at maximum lift position.
- **Pitch Curve:** The path traced by the follower tip relative to the cam.
- **Pressure Angle ($\alpha$):** The angle between the direction of follower motion and the normal to the cam profile at the point of contact. KTU benchmark: $\alpha \le 30°$ for translating followers.
- **Cam Speed:** $\omega$ (rad/s) — assumed constant for kinematic analysis.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Standard Motions Used in KTU Examinations

When designing a cam profile, the engineer must choose the **type of motion** the follower executes during the rise and return strokes. KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests the three motions below.

#### 2.1.1 Uniform Velocity (UV) Motion

**Operational Concept:** The follower moves with a **constant velocity** during the rise (or return) stroke.

- **Why use it:** Simple to manufacture, requires the smallest cam size for a given stroke.
- **How it works:** Displacement increases linearly with cam angle. Velocity is constant. Acceleration is theoretically **zero** during motion, but **infinite impulses** occur at the start and end (sudden changes in velocity direction).

**Step-by-step Logic:**

1. Displacement $s$ varies linearly with $\theta$.
2. Velocity $v = \frac{ds}{dt}$ is constant, equal to $\frac{h}{t_r}$, where $h$ is the stroke and $t_r$ is the rise time.
3. Acceleration $a = \frac{dv}{dt} = 0$ during the stroke, but instantaneous finite jump at boundaries (impulse).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Uniform velocity is rarely used in practice because the infinite acceleration impulses cause severe impact, noise, and vibration. It is the simplest case for examinations, however, and students must know its construction perfectly.

#### 2.1.2 Uniform Acceleration and Retardation (UAR) Motion

**Operational Concept:** The follower accelerates uniformly for the first half of the stroke and retards (decelerates) uniformly for the second half.

- **Why use it:** Provides finite acceleration values (no infinite impulses), more practical than UV.
- **How it works:** During acceleration, $a = +a_0$ (constant). During retardation, $a = -a_0$ (constant). At the midpoint, velocity is maximum and acceleration changes sign.

**Step-by-step Logic:**

1. Acceleration is constant in magnitude (with sign change at mid-stroke).
2. Velocity increases linearly during acceleration, then decreases linearly during retardation.
3. Displacement is parabolic — concave up during acceleration, concave down during retardation.
4. The displacement curve has a **kink (cusp)** at the midpoint where acceleration changes sign.

#### 2.1.3 Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)

**Operational Concept:** The follower executes motion identical to a point on a Simple Harmonic Oscillator (like a piston in an ideal spring-mass system).

- **Why use it:** Produces the smoothest, most vibration-free motion. The acceleration varies sinusoidally.
- **How it works:** The displacement is sinusoidal. Velocity is cosinusoidal. Acceleration is the negative sinusoidal.

**Step-by-step Logic:**

1. The displacement curve is a **sine wave** segment.
2. The velocity curve is a **cosine wave** segment.
3. The acceleration curve is the **negative sine wave** segment.
4. The acceleration is **zero at the start, mid, and end** of the stroke (smooth transitions).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation used:** $h$ = total lift/stroke, $t_r$ = rise time, $\theta_r$ = rise angle, $\omega$ = cam angular velocity, $\phi$ = cam angle from start of stroke.

| Motion Type | Displacement $s(\phi)$ | Velocity $v = \omega \frac{ds}{d\phi}$ | Acceleration $a = \omega^2 \frac{d^2s}{d\phi^2}$ | Max Acceleration |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Uniform Velocity** | $s = \frac{h}{\theta_r} \phi$ | $v = \omega \cdot \frac{h}{\theta_r}$ (constant) | $a = 0$ during stroke, $\infty$ at start/end | Infinite (impulse) |
| **Uniform Accel. & Retardation** | For $0 \le \phi \le \theta_r/2$: $s = \frac{2h}{\theta_r^2} \phi^2$ | For $0 \le \phi \le \theta_r/2$: $v = \omega \cdot \frac{4h}{\theta_r^2} \phi$ | $a = +\frac{4h\omega^2}{\theta_r^2}$ (constant) | $a_{max} = \frac{4h\omega^2}{\theta_r^2}$ |
| **Uniform Accel. & Retardation** | For $\theta_r/2 \le \phi \le \theta_r$: $s = h - \frac{2h}{\theta_r^2}(\theta_r - \phi)^2$ | For $\theta_r/2 \le \phi \le \theta_r$: $v = \omega \cdot \frac{4h}{\theta_r^2}(\theta_r - \phi)$ | $a = -\frac{4h\omega^2}{\theta_r^2}$ (constant) | Same magnitude, negative |
| **Simple Harmonic Motion** | $s = \frac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\left(\frac{\pi \phi}{\theta_r}\right)\right]$ | $v = \omega \cdot \frac{h\pi}{2\theta_r} \sin\left(\frac{\pi \phi}{\theta_r}\right)$ | $a = \omega^2 \cdot \frac{h\pi^2}{2\theta_r^2} \cos\left(\frac{\pi \phi}{\theta_r}\right)$ | $a_{max} = \frac{h\pi^2\omega^2}{2\theta_r^2}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Comparison for KTU Board Exams:** For the same stroke $h$, rise angle $\theta_r$, and cam speed $\omega$, the maximum acceleration ranks as: **SHM < UAR < Uniform Velocity (theoretically infinite)**. This is why SHM is the smoothest, and UV is rarely used in real high-speed machines.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Engine Valves:** Use a **polynomial (modified sine)** or **cycloidal** motion — variants of SHM — to balance smooth operation with adequate dwell time for gas flow.
- **Automatic Toy Mechanisms:** Frequently use **UAR** as a compromise between simplicity and smoothness.
- **Precision Printing Presses:** Use **SHM or higher-order polynomials** to minimize vibration that would blur print quality.
- **Projectile Launchers (Toy Guns, Clocks):** Simple **uniform velocity** is acceptable at low speeds where vibration is negligible.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Uniform Velocity Motion

Starting with the linear displacement assumption over the rise angle $\theta_r$:

$$s = \frac{h}{\theta_r} \phi$$

Differentiating once with respect to time using the chain rule $\frac{ds}{dt} = \frac{ds}{d\phi} \cdot \frac{d\phi}{dt} = \omega \frac{ds}{d\phi}$:

$$v = \frac{ds}{dt} = \omega \cdot \frac{d}{d\phi}\left(\frac{h \phi}{\theta_r}\right) = \omega \cdot \frac{h}{\theta_r}$$

Differentiating again:

$$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left(\omega \cdot \frac{h}{\theta_r}\right) = 0$$

**Interpretation:** The velocity is a constant horizontal line. The acceleration is zero everywhere except at the boundaries ($\phi = 0$ and $\phi = \theta_r$), where it takes the form of a Dirac delta impulse of finite magnitude. This is the root cause of the vibration problems associated with UV motion.

### 3.2 Derivation: Uniform Acceleration and Retardation (UAR) Motion

**Phase 1 (Acceleration, $0 \le \phi \le \theta_r/2$):** Assume constant acceleration $+a_0$:

$$a = a_0 \quad \text{(constant)}$$

Integrating once with respect to time, and using $v(0) = 0$:

$$v = a_0 t = a_0 \cdot \frac{\phi}{\omega}$$

Integrating again with $s(0) = 0$:

$$s = \frac{1}{2} a_0 t^2 = \frac{1}{2} a_0 \cdot \frac{\phi^2}{\omega^2} = \frac{a_0}{2\omega^2} \phi^2$$

Applying the boundary condition at $\phi = \theta_r/2$ that $s = h/2$:

$$\frac{h}{2} = \frac{a_0}{2\omega^2} \cdot \frac{\theta_r^2}{4}$$

Solving for $a_0$:

$$a_0 = \frac{4h\omega^2}{\theta_r^2}$$

Substituting back:

$$s = \frac{2h}{\theta_r^2} \phi^2 \quad \text{for} \quad 0 \le \phi \le \frac{\theta_r}{2}$$

$$v = \omega \cdot \frac{4h\phi}{\theta_r^2} \quad \text{for} \quad 0 \le \phi \le \frac{\theta_r}{2}$$

**Phase 2 (Retardation, $\theta_r/2 \le \phi \le \theta_r$):** By symmetry, the velocity is mirrored and the acceleration is $-a_0$:

$$s = h - \frac{2h}{\theta_r^2}(\theta_r - \phi)^2$$

$$v = \omega \cdot \frac{4h}{\theta_r^2}(\theta_r - \phi)$$

$$a = -\frac{4h\omega^2}{\theta_r^2}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)

We model the follower's motion as the projection of a point P moving uniformly around a circle of radius $h/2$ onto a vertical diameter.

The angular velocity of point P on the auxiliary circle is $\Omega = \frac{\pi}{\theta_r}$ rad per unit cam angle.

The vertical position of P (i.e., the follower displacement) is:

$$s = \frac{h}{2}(1 - \cos(\Omega \phi)) = \frac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\left(\frac{\pi \phi}{\theta_r}\right)\right]$$

Taking the first derivative with respect to time:

$$\frac{ds}{d\phi} = \frac{h}{2} \cdot \frac{\pi}{\theta_r} \sin\left(\frac{\pi \phi}{\theta_r}\right)$$

$$\therefore \quad v = \omega \cdot \frac{h\pi}{2\theta_r} \sin\left(\frac{\pi \phi}{\theta_r}\right)$$

Taking the second derivative:

$$\frac{d^2s}{d\phi^2} = \frac{h}{2} \cdot \frac{\pi^2}{\theta_r^2} \cos\left(\frac{\pi \phi}{\theta_r}\right)$$

$$\therefore \quad a = \omega^2 \cdot \frac{h\pi^2}{2\theta_r^2} \cos\left(\frac{\pi \phi}{\theta_r}\right)$$

**Maximum acceleration** occurs at $\phi = 0$ (and $\phi = \theta_r$), where $\cos(0) = 1$:

$$a_{max} = \frac{h\pi^2 \omega^2}{2\theta_r^2} \approx \frac{4.93 \, h\omega^2}{\theta_r^2}$$

This is approximately **23% higher** than the UAR maximum ($\frac{4h\omega^2}{\theta_r^2}$), but the SHM acceleration begins and ends at **zero**, eliminating the impulse spikes that plague UV motion.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Key Comparison Numbers

For the same values of $h = 40$ mm, $\omega = 10$ rad/s, $\theta_r = \pi/2$ rad (90°):

| Motion Type | Max Velocity $v_{max}$ (mm/s) | Max Acceleration $a_{max}$ (mm/s²) | Smoothness |
|---|---|---|---|
| Uniform Velocity | $v = 10 \cdot \frac{40}{(\pi/2)} \approx 254.6$ | Infinite (impulse) | Poor (vibration) |
| Uniform Accel. & Retardation | $v_{max} = 10 \cdot \frac{4 \cdot 40}{(\pi/2)^2} \approx 647.0$ | $\frac{4 \cdot 40 \cdot 100}{(\pi/2)^2} \approx 6472$ | Moderate |
| Simple Harmonic Motion | $v_{max} = 10 \cdot \frac{40\pi}{\pi} = 400$ | $\frac{40 \cdot \pi^2 \cdot 100}{2 \cdot (\pi/2)^2} \approx 8000$ | Excellent |

### 3.5 Step-by-Step Procedure: Cam Profile Synthesis for a Knife-Edge Follower

This is the **most important KTU practical question** on this topic. The procedure is exhaustive:

**Step 1 — Draw the Displacement Diagram:**
- Take the cam angle on the x-axis and the follower lift on the y-axis.
- Mark the rise angle $\theta_r$, dwell, return angle $\theta_f$, and final dwell.
- Plot the displacement curve for the chosen motion type (UV, UAR, or SHM).

**Step 2 — Divide into Equal Angular Intervals:**
- Divide the rise and return strokes into an equal number of equal angular segments (KTU standard: 8, 10, or 12 parts).
- At each division, mark the displacement value $s_1, s_2, s_3, \ldots, s_n$ from the displacement diagram.

**Step 3 — Draw the Base Circle:**
- Draw the base circle of the cam with radius $r_b$ centered at O.
- Draw a vertical line from O representing the initial follower position (knife edge touching the base circle at point A).

**Step 4 — Mark Angular Divisions on the Base Circle:**
- With O as center, draw radial lines OA, OB, OC, ... corresponding to the same angular divisions used in Step 2.
- The number of radial lines = number of divisions in Step 2.

**Step 5 — Project the Displacement Values:**
- On each radial line, starting from the base circle, mark a distance equal to the corresponding displacement $s_1, s_2, s_3, \ldots, s_n$ in the direction of follower motion (radially outward for a knife-edge follower).
- For example, on radial line OB, mark $OB' = s_1$. On OC, mark $OC' = s_2$, and so on.

**Step 6 — Trace the Cam Profile:**
- Join the points $A, B', C', D', \ldots$ with a smooth curve.
- This smooth curve is the **pitch curve** of the cam. For a knife-edge follower, the pitch curve **is** the cam profile.

**Step 7 — Add the Dwell Portions:**
- For dwell periods, the cam profile coincides with a circle of constant radius (the dwell circle).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Check for Knife-Edge Follower:** Since the knife edge has zero radius, no offset is needed. The pitch curve directly equals the profile. For roller followers, an equivalent roller of identical radius is used, and the profile is offset inward by the roller radius.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cam-Follower Kinematic System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Input1[Cam Shaft Input: Constant Angular Velocity omega] --> CamBlock[Cam Profile Geometry]
    CamBlock --> ContactZone[Contact Zone Between Cam and Knife Edge]
    ContactZone --> FollowerBlock[Knife Edge Follower: Translating in Y direction]
    
    FollowerBlock --> DisplOut[Displacement s of Follower]
    FollowerBlock --> VelOut[Velocity v of Follower]
    FollowerBlock --> AccelOut[Acceleration a of Follower]
    
    subgraph MotionTypes[Follower Motion Selection]
        direction LR
        M1[Uniform Velocity]
        M2[Uniform Acceleration and Retardation]
        M3[Simple Harmonic Motion SHM]
    end
    
    MotionTypes --> DisplOut
    DisplOut --> VelOut
    VelOut --> AccelOut
    AccelOut --> DesignCheck{Design Validation}
    DesignCheck -->|Max Accel too high| ModifyCam[Modify Cam Profile Geometry]
    DesignCheck -->|Acceptable| FinalProfile[Finalize Cam Profile for Manufacture]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cam Profile Synthesis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start([Start: Given Stroke h, Rise Angle theta_r, Return Angle theta_f, Base Circle Radius r_b]) --> StepA[Step 1: Draw Displacement Diagram s vs phi]
    
    StepA --> StepB[Step 2: Divide Rise and Return into N equal parts]
    StepB --> StepC[Step 3: Mark Displacement Values s1, s2, ..., sN on Diagram]
    
    StepC --> StepD[Step 4: Draw Base Circle of Cam with center O and radius r_b]
    StepD --> StepE[Step 5: Draw N radial lines from O at equal angular spacing]
    
    StepE --> StepF[Step 6: On Each Radial Line, Measure Displacement si from Base Circle Outward]
    StepF --> StepG[Step 7: Mark Points P1, P2, ..., PN at the Tip of Each Measurement]
    
    StepG --> StepH[Step 8: Join All Points Pi with Smooth Curve]
    StepH --> StepI[Step 9: Identify Dwell Sections as Circular Arcs]
    
    StepI --> StepJ[Step 10: Final Cam Profile Complete]
    StepJ --> End([End: Profile Ready for Manufacturing])
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Comparative Motion Characteristics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Displacement s] -->|First Derivative| B[Velocity v]
    B -->|Second Derivative| C[Acceleration a]
    
    subgraph S1[Uniform Velocity]
        A1[s: Linear] --> B1[v: Constant] --> C1[a: Zero with Impulses]
    end
    
    subgraph S2[Uniform Accel Retard]
        A2[s: Parabolic] --> B2[v: Triangular] --> C2[a: Step Function]
    end
    
    subgraph S3[Simple Harmonic]
        A3[s: Sinusoidal] --> B3[v: Cosinusoidal] --> C3[a: Negative Sinusoidal]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Interpretation:** This block diagram demonstrates the progression of differentiation across all three motion types. Notice how SHM produces the **smoothest acceleration curve** (a continuous cosine wave with no discontinuities), making it the most desirable in high-speed machinery.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define the following terms with neat sketches: (a) Base circle, (b) Pitch curve, (c) Pressure angle.**

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Base Circle:** It is the smallest circle that can be drawn tangent to the cam profile. It is the reference circle from which all radial distances of the cam profile are measured. The follower rests on the base circle during the dwell period.

(b) **Pitch Curve:** It is the path traced by the follower (knife edge or roller center) relative to the cam as the cam rotates through one complete cycle. For a knife-edge follower, the pitch curve coincides with the cam profile.

(c) **Pressure Angle ($\alpha$):** It is the angle between the direction of motion of the follower and the normal to the cam profile at the point of contact. A larger pressure angle causes more side thrust and wear. KTU design limit: $\alpha \le 30°$ for translating followers.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Differentiate between uniform velocity motion and simple harmonic motion of a follower with respect to acceleration characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Uniform Velocity Motion | Simple Harmonic Motion |
|---|---|---|
| Acceleration during stroke | Zero throughout | Cosinusoidal, varies continuously |
| Acceleration at start/end | Infinite (impulse spikes) | Zero (smooth start and stop) |
| Velocity | Constant | Cosinusoidal, peaks at mid-stroke |
| Displacement | Linear | Sinusoidal |
| Smoothness | Poor, causes vibration | Excellent, smooth operation |
| Practical use | Rarely used in high-speed machines | Common in high-speed valve mechanisms |

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model Paper]*

**(a)** A cam rotating at **300 rpm** is to give the following motion to a knife-edge follower:
- **Outstroke (Rise):** Uniform acceleration and retardation over **120°**
- **Dwell:** **30°**
- **Return stroke:** Simple Harmonic Motion over **90°**
- **Dwell:** **Remaining angle** to complete the cycle

The stroke of the follower is **40 mm**, and the base circle diameter is **50 mm**. Draw the displacement, velocity, and acceleration diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

**Step 1 — Identify the given parameters:**

- Cam speed $N = 300$ rpm $\Rightarrow \omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 300}{60} = 31.416$ rad/s
- Stroke $h = 40$ mm
- Base circle radius $r_b = 25$ mm
- Rise angle $\theta_r = 120° = \frac{2\pi}{3}$ rad $= 2.094$ rad
- Dwell angle $\theta_d = 30° = \frac{\pi}{6}$ rad
- Return angle $\theta_f = 90° = \frac{\pi}{2}$ rad $= 1.571$ rad
- Final dwell angle $= 360° - (120° + 30° + 90°) = 120°$

**Step 2 — Draw the displacement diagram:**

- Plot x-axis for cam angle (0° to 360°), y-axis for displacement (0 to 40 mm).
- **Rise (0° to 120°):** UAR motion — displacement follows two parabolic arcs meeting at the midpoint (60°) at $h/2 = 20$ mm.
- **Dwell (120° to 150°):** Constant at 40 mm.
- **Return (150° to 240°):** SHM — sinusoidal descent from 40 mm to 0 mm.
- **Dwell (240° to 360°):** Constant at 0 mm.

**Step 3 — Calculate key values for UAR Rise:**

Maximum acceleration: 
$$a_{max, UAR} = \frac{4h\omega^2}{\theta_r^2} = \frac{4 \times 0.04 \times (31.416)^2}{(2.094)^2} = \frac{4 \times 0.04 \times 987.0}{4.385} \approx 36.0 \, \text{m/s}^2$$

Maximum velocity at mid-stroke:
$$v_{max, UAR} = \omega \cdot \frac{2h}{\theta_r} = 31.416 \times \frac{2 \times 0.04}{2.094} = 31.416 \times 0.0382 \approx 1.20 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate key values for SHM Return:**

Maximum acceleration at start of return:
$$a_{max, SHM} = \frac{h\pi^2\omega^2}{2\theta_f^2} = \frac{0.04 \times \pi^2 \times (31.416)^2}{2 \times (1.571)^2} = \frac{0.04 \times 9.87 \times 987.0}{4.935} \approx 78.9 \, \text{m/s}^2$$

Maximum velocity at mid-return:
$$v_{max, SHM} = \omega \cdot \frac{h\pi}{2\theta_f} = 31.416 \times \frac{0.04 \times \pi}{2 \times 1.571} = 31.416 \times 0.04 = 1.257 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Sketch the diagrams** with these magnitudes properly scaled.

**(b) Construct the cam profile for the knife-edge follower described in part (a). State any assumptions made. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**Step 1 — Choose the number of divisions:** $N = 12$ equal parts (so each division = $120°/12 = 10°$ for rise).

**Step 2 — Mark displacement values on the diagram:**
For UAR rise, displacement at angle $\phi$ (in degrees) from 0° to 60°:
$$s = \frac{2h}{\theta_r^2} \phi^2 = \frac{2 \times 40}{(120°)^2} \phi^2 = \frac{80}{14400} \phi^2 = 0.00556 \phi^2 \text{ mm}$$

At $\phi = 10°$: $s = 0.556$ mm  
At $\phi = 20°$: $s = 2.222$ mm  
At $\phi = 30°$: $s = 5.000$ mm  
At $\phi = 40°$: $s = 8.889$ mm  
At $\phi = 50°$: $s = 13.889$ mm  
At $\phi = 60°$: $s = 20.000$ mm

From 60° to 120°, use mirror symmetry: $s(\phi) = 40 - s(120° - \phi)$.

**Step 3 — Draw the base circle** with center O, radius $r_b = 25$ mm. Mark the initial contact point A on the vertical line above O.

**Step 4 — Draw 12 radial lines** from O at $10°$ intervals, starting from the vertical reference.

**Step 5 — On each radial line**, measure outward from the base circle a distance equal to the corresponding $s_i$:
- At radial line 1 (10°): mark $25 + 0.556 = 25.556$ mm
- At radial line 2 (20°): mark $25 + 2.222 = 27.222$ mm
- ... and so on.

**Step 6 — Repeat for the SHM return portion** with 9 divisions of 10° each. SHM displacement from start of return ($\phi' = 0$ to 90°):
$$s = \frac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\left(\frac{\pi \phi'}{90°}\right)\right] = 20\left[1 - \cos\left(\frac{\pi \phi'}{90°}\right)\right]$$

At $\phi' = 0°$: $s = 0$; at $\phi' = 90°$: $s = 40$ mm; etc.

**Step 7 — Join the points** smoothly to obtain the cam profile. The dwell portions are concentric circular arcs at radii 65 mm (outer dwell) and 25 mm (base circle dwell).

**Assumptions:**
1. Cam shaft is vertical, follower moves vertically (in line with cam center).
2. Knife edge has negligible radius and no wear.
3. Cam rotates at constant angular velocity.
4. No flexible components; rigid body kinematics only.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Model Paper]*

**(a)** A cam rotating uniformly at **200 rpm** drives a knife-edge follower through a stroke of **30 mm**. The follower moves with **uniform velocity** during the rise of **$\pi/2$ rad**, dwells for **$\pi/6$ rad**, and returns with **simple harmonic motion** during **$\pi/2$ rad**. The remaining period is dwell. Calculate: (i) the maximum velocity during the rise stroke, (ii) the maximum acceleration during the return stroke, and (iii) the time required for the complete cycle. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**Given:**
- $N = 200$ rpm $\Rightarrow \omega = \frac{2\pi \times 200}{60} = 20.944$ rad/s
- Stroke $h = 30$ mm $= 0.030$ m
- Rise angle $\theta_r = \pi/2$ rad (UV motion)
- Dwell after rise $= \pi/6$ rad
- Return angle $\theta_f = \pi/2$ rad (SHM)
- Final dwell $= 2\pi - (\pi/2 + \pi/6 + \pi/2) = 2\pi - 5\pi/3 = \pi/3$ rad

**Part (i): Maximum velocity during UV rise:**

For uniform velocity motion:
$$v_{max} = \omega \cdot \frac{h}{\theta_r} = 20.944 \times \frac{0.030}{\pi/2} = 20.944 \times 0.01910 \approx 0.400 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Part (ii): Maximum acceleration during SHM return:**

$$a_{max} = \frac{h \pi^2 \omega^2}{2 \theta_f^2} = \frac{0.030 \times \pi^2 \times (20.944)^2}{2 \times (\pi/2)^2}$$

$$a_{max} = \frac{0.030 \times 9.8696 \times 438.65}{2 \times 2.4674} = \frac{129.86}{4.935} \approx 26.31 \, \text{m/s}^2$$

**Part (iii): Time for complete cycle:**

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega} = \frac{2\pi}{20.944} = 0.300 \, \text{s} = 300 \, \text{ms}$$

This is the time for one full revolution of the cam, encompassing rise, dwells, and return.

**(b) Draw the velocity and acceleration diagrams for the follower motion described in part (a) and explain why SHM is preferred over uniform velocity in high-speed machinery. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Velocity Diagram:**
- **UV Rise (0 to $\pi/2$ rad):** Horizontal straight line at $v = 0.400$ m/s, with two vertical impulses of magnitude $\pm 0.400$ m/s at the start and end (Dirac delta spikes).
- **Dwell ($\pi/2$ to $2\pi/3$ rad):** Zero velocity.
- **SHM Return ($2\pi/3$ to $7\pi/6$ rad):** Cosine wave starting from 0, peaking at the mid-return point at $v_{max,SHM} = \frac{\omega h \pi}{2\theta_f} = \frac{20.944 \times 0.030 \times \pi}{2 \times \pi/2} = 0.628$ m/s, returning to zero.
- **Final Dwell ($7\pi/6$ to $2\pi$ rad):** Zero velocity.

**Acceleration Diagram:**
- **UV Rise:** Two infinite impulse spikes (positive at start, negative at end), zero in between.
- **Dwell:** Zero everywhere.
- **SHM Return:** Negative cosine curve — starts at $-26.31$ m/s², rises to zero at mid-return, peaks at $+26.31$ m/s² at end of return. (Note: For SHM starting from rest, the initial acceleration magnitude is the maximum.)

**Why SHM is preferred in high-speed machinery (Reasoning):**

1. **Continuous acceleration:** SHM has a continuous, smooth acceleration profile. UV has infinite acceleration impulses.
2. **No jerk spikes:** A "jerk" is the rate of change of acceleration ($da/dt$). In UV motion, jerk is undefined at the boundaries (impulses). In SHM, jerk is finite and continuous.
3. **Reduced vibration:** High acceleration spikes excite natural frequencies of the machine structure, causing resonance, noise, and fatigue failure. SHM minimizes this.
4. **Lower peak forces on bearings and guides:** The reaction forces on the follower stay bounded in SHM, reducing wear on sliding surfaces.
5. **Better dynamic balancing:** In multi-cylinder engines using cam-driven valves, SHM-based cam profiles produce more uniform force transmission to the crankshaft.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Most common mark-loss area: Angular unit consistency.** Students frequently mix degrees and radians. $\omega$ is in **rad/s**, so $\theta_r$ and $\theta_f$ **must be in radians** in all formulas. Always convert degrees to radians: multiply degrees by $\pi/180$.
> 2. **Second common error: Forgetting to square the cam speed.** Acceleration formulas have $\omega^2$, not $\omega$. A single missing square can cascade into a 3-mark deduction.
> 3. **Third common error: Wrong direction in cam profile construction.** For a translating knife-edge follower, the displacement must be measured **radially outward** from the base circle. Students sometimes measure inward, producing an inverted cam profile.
> 4. **Fourth common error: Confusing maximum velocity location.** For UAR motion, $v_{max}$ is at the **mid-stroke**. For SHM, $v_{max}$ is also at the **mid-stroke**, but the value differs. For UV, $v_{max}$ is the constant value during the entire stroke.
> 5. **Fifth common error: Forgetting units in the final answer.** Always state whether the answer is in m/s, m/s², mm/s, etc. Unit omission is a guaranteed 0.5-mark loss.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 2 (Cam Kinematics & Knife-Edge Cam Synthesis)**

- [ ] **Base Circle:** Smallest circle tangent to cam profile. Always the starting reference.
- [ ] **Knife-Edge Follower:** Zero-radius contact point. Pitch curve **equals** cam profile.
- [ ] **Uniform Velocity:** $s = (h/\theta_r)\phi$, $v = \omega h/\theta_r$ (constant), $a = 0$ (with infinite impulses at boundaries).
- [ ] **Uniform Accel & Retard:** Displacement is parabolic (two arcs), $a_{max} = 4h\omega^2/\theta_r^2$, kink at mid-stroke.
- [ ] **Simple Harmonic Motion:** $s = (h/2)[1 - \cos(\pi\phi/\theta_r)]$, smoothest of the three, $a_{max} = h\pi^2\omega^2/(2\theta_r^2)$.
- [ ] **Comparative Max Accel (same h, $\omega$, $\theta$):** UV $\to \infty$ > SHM ($\approx 4.93 h\omega^2/\theta^2$) > UAR ($= 4h\omega^2/\theta^2$).
- [ ] **Cam Profile Synthesis Steps (12 steps for full marks):** Displacement diagram $\to$ divide into N equal parts $\to$ mark $s_i$ values $\to$ draw base circle $\to$ mark N radial lines $\to$ project $s_i$ outward $\to$ join smoothly $\to$ add dwell arcs.
- [ ] **Pressure Angle:** KTU design limit $\alpha \le 30°$ for translating knife-edge followers to avoid jamming.
- [ ] **Critical Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $v = \omega \cdot ds/d\phi$
  - $a = \omega^2 \cdot d^2s/d\phi^2$
  - SHM: $s = (h/2)[1 - \cos(\pi\phi/\theta_r)]$
  - UAR accel: $a_{max} = 4h\omega^2/\theta_r^2$
  - SHM accel: $a_{max} = h\pi^2\omega^2/(2\theta_r^2)$
- [ ] **Common Pitfalls to Avoid:** Mixed angular units, missing $\omega^2$, wrong measurement direction, unit omission.
- [ ] **High-Yield Exam Tip:** When asked to "construct the cam profile," always state the number of divisions used (8, 10, or 12) and **list the displacement values** in a table for examiner convenience.
- [ ] **Practical Insight:** In real engines, a **polynomial** (modified trapezoidal) cam profile is used — not pure SHM, not pure UAR. It combines the dwell advantage of UAR with the smoothness of SHM. This is a favorite **2-mark short note** topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Friction Clutch - Design of Plate Friction Clutch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET404 - THEORY OF MACHINES AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Friction Clutch - Design of Plate Friction Clutch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Friction Clutch (As per KTU PBIET404 Syllabus):**
> A *friction clutch* is a machine element designed to transmit rotational torque from a driving shaft to a driven shaft through **frictional contact** between its members, with the ability to engage, disengage, or slip progressively while in operation, without the use of teeth, keys, or positive mechanical interlock.

A **Plate Friction Clutch** is a specific subclass in which the torque-transmitting frictional surfaces are essentially **flat annular discs (plates)** pressed axially against one another by an axial force. The torque capacity is governed by the **axial thrust**, the **coefficient of friction** between the contacting surfaces, and the **geometry of the friction surface** (inner and outer radii).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Classification — Plate Friction Clutch Family**
> 1. **Single Plate Clutch** — One driving and one driven plate; used in automobiles, machine tools.
> 2. **Multi-Plate Clutch** — Multiple discs stacked alternately; used where high torque-to-size ratio is required (motorcycles, racing cars, industrial gearboxes).
> 3. **Cone Clutch** — Tapered surfaces (special case; discussed separately).
> 4. **Disc / Centrifugal Clutch** — Self-engaging through centrifugal force (scooters, chainsaws).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Press-and-Twist" Principle

Imagine you are **pressing a wet plate against a spinning potter's wheel with one hand** while holding the plate still with the other. The harder you press (more axial force), the more torque you can transfer from the wheel to the plate. If your hand slips slightly (sliding friction), the wheel keeps spinning while torque is transmitted.

| Real-Life System | Driving Member | Driven Member | Axial Force Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bicycle Hand Brake on Rim** | Rim (rotating) | Brake Pad (stationary) | Hand Squeezing Lever |
| **Car Clutch Pedal** | Engine Flywheel | Clutch Disc (to gearbox) | Diaphragm Spring |
| **Drill Press Chuck** | Spindle (rotating) | Drill Bit (stationary workpiece) | Hand-Tightened Jaws |
| **Motorcycle Wet Clutch** | Engine Crank | Gearbox Input Shaft | Coil Springs (5–6 discs) |

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** The **friction force (F = μN)** acts *tangentially* on the contact surface and the moment arm (the radius) at which this force acts determines the torque. The clutch designer controls **N (axial force)**, **μ (material choice)**, and **r (geometry)** to achieve a target torque **T**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Material Reference Values

| Parameter | Typical Range | Engineering Material / Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Coefficient of friction (μ) for **ferodo on steel** | **0.25 – 0.40** | Sintered bronze / ferodo lining |
| Coefficient of friction (μ) for **cast iron on cast iron** | **0.10 – 0.15** | Bare metal, dry contact |
| Coefficient of friction (μ) for **steel on steel (oil-lubricated)** | **0.05 – 0.10** | Multi-plate wet clutches |
| Allowable pressure (p) — ferodo | **0.35 – 0.70 MPa** | IS 6603, Shigley/Dimtri/Robert Norton's design data |
| Allowable pressure (p) — cast iron | **0.25 – 0.50 MPa** | Empirical industrial range |
| Allowable pressure (p) — sintered bronze | **0.80 – 1.20 MPa** | High-duty industrial clutches |
| Slip speed limit | **≤ 10 m/s** (single plate), **≤ 25 m/s** (multi-plate wet) | Heat dissipation constraint |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Governing Pressure Distribution Theories

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** The design of any plate friction clutch fundamentally requires choosing **ONE** of two opposing assumptions. Examiners explicitly test your ability to **state, derive, and apply** both.

### Theory A — Uniform Pressure Theory
**Assumption:** The pressure **p** is **constant** across the entire contact annulus (from inner radius $r_1$ to outer radius $r_2$).

**Why this assumption?** In **new clutches** with rigid axial loading (e.g., a flat machined mating plate pressed by a flat thrust collar), the contact pressure naturally equalises if the surfaces are perfectly flat and rigid.

### Theory B — Uniform Wear Theory
**Assumption:** The product **p × r = constant** across the contact annulus (pressure is **inversely proportional to radius**).

**Why this assumption?** In clutches that have been **run-in** (worn), the higher linear velocity at the outer edge wears the lining faster. The system reaches a steady state where wear rate is uniform radially, meaning the *intensity* of wear is the same at every radius. Since wear ∝ (p × sliding distance) and sliding distance ∝ r, we get p × r = constant.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (Shigley & Norton):** Always design using **Uniform Wear Theory** for clutches that will experience long service life. Use **Uniform Pressure** only for short-life, single-engagement applications (e.g., press tools, stamping clutches).

## 2.2 Operational Logic Flow

1. **Axial thrust W** is applied through springs, levers, or hydraulic actuators.
2. This thrust generates a **contact pressure p(r)** over the annular contact zone between $r_1$ and $r_2$.
3. The frictional shear stress at radius r is $\tau(r) = \mu \cdot p(r)$.
4. The elementary torque contribution is $dT = \mu \cdot p(r) \cdot (2\pi r \, dr) \cdot r = 2\pi\mu \, p(r) \, r^2 \, dr$.
5. **Total torque T** is obtained by integrating from $r_1$ to $r_2$ and (for multi-plate) multiplying by the **number of contact surfaces n**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **must-know for KTU 2024 ESE**. Variables: $r_1$ = inner radius, $r_2$ = outer radius, $R$ = mean radius, $W$ = axial thrust, $p$ = pressure, $n$ = number of contact surfaces, $T$ = torque, $\mu$ = coefficient of friction.

| # | Quantity | Uniform Pressure | Uniform Wear | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pressure distribution | $p = \text{const}$ | $p \cdot r = C$ (constant) | Pa or N/m² |
| 2 | Axial thrust **W** | $W = \pi p (r_2^2 - r_1^2)$ | $W = 2\pi C (r_2 - r_1)$ | N |
| 3 | Constant **C** (wear) | — | $C = \dfrac{W}{2\pi(r_2 - r_1)}$ | N/m |
| 4 | Torque **T** (single surface) | $T = \dfrac{2}{3}\pi\mu p (r_2^3 - r_1^3)$ | $T = \dfrac{1}{2}\pi\mu C (r_2^2 - r_1^2)$ | N·m |
| 5 | **Torque in terms of W** | $T = \dfrac{2\mu W}{3}\left(\dfrac{r_2^3 - r_1^3}{r_2^2 - r_1^2}\right)$ | $T = \dfrac{\mu W}{2}(r_1 + r_2)$ | N·m |
| 6 | **Effective mean radius R** | $R_{eq} = \dfrac{2}{3}\left(\dfrac{r_2^3 - r_1^3}{r_2^2 - r_1^2}\right)$ | $R_{eq} = \dfrac{r_1 + r_2}{2}$ | m |
| 7 | Max pressure $p_{max}$ | $p_{max} = p$ (at all r) | $p_{max} = p$ at $r_1$ | Pa |
| 8 | Torque per surface (multi-plate) | $T_{total} = n \cdot T_{single}$ | $T_{total} = n \cdot T_{single}$ | N·m |
| 9 | Friction power loss | $P = T \cdot \omega_{slip}$ | Same | W |
| 10 | Energy of friction per engagement | $E = T \cdot \theta_{slip}$ (radians) | Same | J |
| 11 | Inner-to-outer radius ratio (optimum) | $r_1/r_2 = \sqrt{2} - 1 \approx 0.707$ (max torque) | $r_1/r_2 = 0.578$ (max torque) | — |
| 12 | Max torque (optimum ratio) | $T_{max} = 0.2687\,\mu W r_2$ | $T_{max} = 0.1875\,\mu W r_2$ | N·m |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** The **clutch pedal feel** is engineered by selecting the W–μ–r combination to match engine torque output. A uniform-wear design ensures the same pedal force works effectively throughout clutch life.
- **Heavy Earthmoving Equipment:** Multi-plate wet clutches in automatic transmissions (e.g., torque converters with lock-up clutches) are designed using uniform-wear theory with sintered bronze discs to handle continuous slip during gear shifts.
- **Machine Tools & Presses:** Single-shot, high-impact clutches use uniform-pressure theory because the lining wears out in one or two engagements anyway.
- **Wind Turbine Pitch Drives:** Centrifugal plate clutches act as **mechanical fuses**, slipping when wind torque exceeds a calibrated threshold to protect the gearbox.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Torque under Uniform Pressure Theory

### Setup
Consider an annular friction surface with inner radius $r_1$, outer radius $r_2$, axial thrust $W$, and uniform pressure $p$ everywhere on the surface. There is **one** contact pair.

### Step 1 — Axial Equilibrium (Find p in terms of W)
The total axial force equals pressure times projected area:

$$W = p \cdot A = p \cdot \pi(r_2^2 - r_1^2)$$

Solving for pressure:

$$p = \dfrac{W}{\pi(r_2^2 - r_1^2)}$$

### Step 2 — Elementary Friction Force on a Ring Element
Consider a thin annular ring at radius $r$ with width $dr$. The area of this ring is:

$$dA = 2\pi r \, dr$$

The normal force on this ring (axial) is:

$$dN = p \cdot dA = 2\pi p r \, dr$$

The **frictional (tangential) force** on this ring, using Coulomb's law $F = \mu N$:

$$dF_f = \mu \cdot dN = 2\pi \mu p r \, dr$$

### Step 3 — Elementary Torque Contribution
The elementary torque is force times radius (moment arm):

$$dT = dF_f \cdot r = 2\pi \mu p r^2 \, dr$$

### Step 4 — Integrate Over the Full Annulus
$$T = \int_{r_1}^{r_2} 2\pi \mu p r^2 \, dr = 2\pi \mu p \int_{r_1}^{r_2} r^2 \, dr$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$T = 2\pi \mu p \left[ \dfrac{r^3}{3} \right]_{r_1}^{r_2} = \dfrac{2}{3}\pi \mu p (r_2^3 - r_1^3)$$

### Step 5 — Substitute p from Step 1 to Get Torque in Terms of W
$$T = \dfrac{2}{3}\pi \mu \left[\dfrac{W}{\pi(r_2^2 - r_1^2)}\right](r_2^3 - r_1^3)$$

After cancellation of $\pi$:

$$\boxed{T = \dfrac{2\mu W}{3}\left(\dfrac{r_2^3 - r_1^3}{r_2^2 - r_1^2}\right)}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Torque under Uniform Wear Theory

### Setup
Assume $p \cdot r = C$ (constant). The pressure is highest at the inner radius and lowest at the outer radius.

### Step 1 — Axial Equilibrium
$$W = \int_{r_1}^{r_2} p(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \dfrac{C}{r} \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

The $r$ cancels inside the integral:

$$W = 2\pi C \int_{r_1}^{r_2} dr = 2\pi C (r_2 - r_1)$$

Therefore:

$$C = \dfrac{W}{2\pi(r_2 - r_1)}$$

### Step 2 — Elementary Friction Force
$$dF_f = \mu \cdot p(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \mu \cdot \dfrac{C}{r} \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = 2\pi \mu C \, dr$$

> **Note:** The $r$ cancels, so the tangential force per unit radial length is **constant** — this is a direct consequence of uniform wear.

### Step 3 — Elementary Torque
$$dT = dF_f \cdot r = 2\pi \mu C \cdot r \, dr$$

### Step 4 — Integrate from r₁ to r₂
$$T = 2\pi \mu C \int_{r_1}^{r_2} r \, dr = 2\pi \mu C \left[ \dfrac{r^2}{2} \right]_{r_1}^{r_2} = \pi \mu C (r_2^2 - r_1^2)$$

### Step 5 — Substitute C from Step 1
$$T = \pi \mu \left[\dfrac{W}{2\pi(r_2 - r_1)}\right](r_2^2 - r_1^2)$$

Using the identity $r_2^2 - r_1^2 = (r_2 - r_1)(r_2 + r_1)$:

$$T = \pi \mu \cdot \dfrac{W}{2\pi(r_2 - r_1)} \cdot (r_2 - r_1)(r_2 + r_1)$$

The $(r_2 - r_1)$ cancels cleanly:

$$\boxed{T = \dfrac{1}{2}\mu W (r_1 + r_2)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Elegant Engineering Insight:** Under uniform wear, the torque formula depends *only* on the **arithmetic mean** of the two radii, $R_m = (r_1 + r_2)/2$. This is why it's often called the **"mean radius"** or **"uniform-wear mean radius"** formula.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Optimum Radius Ratio for Maximum Torque (Uniform Wear)

### Setup
For a given axial thrust $W$ and outer radius $r_2$, we want to find the ratio $k = r_1/r_2$ that maximises torque. We have:

$$T = \dfrac{\mu W}{2}(r_1 + r_2) = \dfrac{\mu W r_2}{2}(k + 1)$$

### Constraint (Fixed Outer Radius)
Differentiate T with respect to k, treating $W$ and $r_2$ as constants:

$$\dfrac{dT}{dk} = \dfrac{\mu W r_2}{2}$$

This is a constant, so T increases linearly with k — meaning **larger $r_1$ gives more torque**.

### Engineering Constraint (Fixed Mass of Lining)
If we fix the **mass of friction material**, the area is fixed:

$$A = \pi(r_2^2 - r_1^2) = \text{const}$$

Let $A_0 = \pi r_2^2(1 - k^2)$ be the constant. Solving for $r_2$:

$$r_2^2 = \dfrac{A_0}{\pi(1 - k^2)}$$

Substituting into T:

$$T = \dfrac{\mu W}{2}\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{A_0}{\pi(1 - k^2)}} \cdot k + \sqrt{\dfrac{A_0}{\pi(1 - k^2)}}\right)$$

$$T = \dfrac{\mu W}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{A_0}{\pi}} \cdot \dfrac{1+k}{\sqrt{1-k^2}}$$

To maximise, differentiate with respect to k and set to zero. Let $f(k) = (1+k)/\sqrt{1-k^2}$.

$$\dfrac{df}{dk} = \dfrac{\sqrt{1-k^2} - (1+k)\cdot\frac{-k}{\sqrt{1-k^2}}}{1-k^2} = \dfrac{(1-k^2) + k(1+k)}{(1-k^2)^{3/2}} = \dfrac{1 + k}{(1-k^2)^{3/2}}$$

Setting $df/dk = 0$ gives $1 + k = 0$, which is not physical. We need a different approach.

### Standard Result
Using Lagrange multipliers or direct differentiation of the proper form, the **optimum** is:

$$\boxed{k_{opt} = r_1/r_2 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.577}$$

This yields maximum torque $T_{max} = (3\sqrt{3}/8)\,\mu W r_2 \approx 0.6495\,\mu W r_2 / 2 = 0.1875\,\mu W r_2$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Multi-Plate Clutch Torque

### Logic
A multi-plate clutch has **n** pairs of contact surfaces. The same axial thrust $W$ acts on each surface (assuming even pressure distribution via thrust plates). Therefore:

$$T_{multi} = n \cdot T_{single}$$

For uniform wear:

$$\boxed{T_{multi} = \dfrac{n \mu W (r_1 + r_2)}{2}}$$

For uniform pressure:

$$T_{multi} = \dfrac{2n\mu W}{3}\left(\dfrac{r_2^3 - r_1^3}{r_2^2 - r_1^2}\right)$$

### Number of Contact Surfaces
If a clutch has $N$ driving discs and $M$ driven discs interleaved:

$$n = N + M - 1$$

(For a typical automotive single-plate clutch: $N=1$ driving plate, $M=1$ driven plate → $n = 1$ contact pair.)
(For a 5-disc motorcycle wet clutch: $N=3$ and $M=2$ → $n = 4$ contact pairs.)

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Design Procedure Summary

| Step | Design Action | Governing Equation | Decision Variable |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identify torque to transmit | $T = (P/\omega) \times SF$ | T (N·m) |
| 2 | Choose materials → get μ, $p_{allow}$ | Material tables | μ, p_max |
| 3 | Choose radius ratio $k = r_1/r_2$ | 0.55 to 0.75 (typical) | k |
| 4 | Calculate mean radius $R_m = (r_1+r_2)/2$ | $T = n\mu W R_m$ | R_m |
| 5 | Check max pressure at $r_1$ | $p_{max} = 2W/[\pi r_1 (r_2-r_1)]$ | $p_{max} \le p_{allow}$ |
| 6 | Check slip velocity | $V = \omega r_2 \le 10$ m/s (dry) | V |
| 7 | Check heat dissipation | $E = T\theta \le m C_p \Delta T$ | ΔT |
| 8 | Iterate until all checks pass | — | Final W, n, r_1, r_2 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 5 — Pressure Check (Uniform Wear)**
> $$p_{max} = \dfrac{C}{r_1} = \dfrac{W}{2\pi r_1 (r_2 - r_1)}$$
> This must not exceed $p_{allow}$ for the lining material.

## 4.4 Schematic Description — Annular Friction Surface Geometry

> [!NOTE]
> The friction surface of a plate clutch is an **annulus** (ring-shaped region). Drawing it as a rectangle with arrows makes the math visible:

```
        OUTER RADIUS r_2
        <------------------>
       +==================+
       ||  ............  ||   ↑
       ||  :  p(r) :    ||   |  ANNULAR
       ||  :      :     ||   |  CONTACT
       ||  : r_1  :     ||   |  WIDTH
       ||  :      :     ||   |
       ||  :......:     ||   ↓
       +==================+
        INNER RADIUS r_1
```

**Key geometric parameters:**
- **Mean radius:** $R_m = (r_1 + r_2)/2$
- **Annular width:** $b = r_2 - r_1$
- **Area:** $A = \pi(r_2^2 - r_1^2)$
- **Average radius (uniform pressure):** $R_{av} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{r_2^3 - r_1^3}{r_2^2 - r_1^2}$

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

### **Q1. (a) State the assumptions of the Uniform Pressure Theory used in the design of plate friction clutches.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Uniform Pressure Theory is based on the following assumptions:
1. The **pressure distribution** is **uniform (constant)** over the entire contact surface of the friction disc.
2. The contact surfaces are **rigid, flat, and perfectly parallel** to each other.
3. The **coefficient of friction μ is constant** over the entire contact area.
4. The wear of the friction lining is **not considered** (theory is valid for new, unused clutches).
5. Heat generated is dissipated uniformly and does not affect the friction coefficient.

> *(Award 1 mark for each clearly stated assumption; full marks for any 3 well-explained assumptions.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

### **Q1. (b) Distinguish between Uniform Pressure and Uniform Wear theories in friction clutches.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No. | Aspect | Uniform Pressure | Uniform Wear |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pressure variation | p = constant | p ∝ 1/r (p·r = const) |
| 2 | Maximum pressure | At all radii | At inner radius r₁ |
| 3 | Torque formula | $T = \frac{2\mu W}{3}\cdot\frac{r_2^3 - r_1^3}{r_2^2 - r_1^2}$ | $T = \frac{\mu W}{2}(r_1 + r_2)$ |
| 4 | Application | New, rigid, short-life clutches | Long-life, run-in clutches |
| 5 | Design margin | Lower torque for same W | More realistic, recommended |

*(1 mark for stating each theory; 1 mark for formulas; 1 mark for application/distinction.)*
