## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End Semester Exam — Model Question Paper Style]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Uniform Wear Multi-Plate Design**

**Q2 (a) [7 Marks]** A multi-plate wet clutch has 6 contact surfaces. The inner and outer radii of the friction discs are 60 mm and 90 mm respectively. The coefficient of friction is 0.08 (oil-lubricated steel-on-steel). Determine the axial force required to transmit a torque of 80 N·m. Use uniform wear theory. Also check whether the maximum pressure exceeds the allowable value of 0.5 MPa.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Number of contact surfaces, $n = 6$
- Inner radius, $r_1 = 60$ mm = $0.060$ m
- Outer radius, $r_2 = 90$ mm = $0.090$ m
- Coefficient of friction, $\mu = 0.08$
- Torque to transmit, $T = 80$ N·m
- Allowable pressure, $p_{allow} = 0.5$ MPa

**Step 1 — Apply uniform wear torque formula:**

$$T = \dfrac{n \mu W (r_1 + r_2)}{2}$$

Solving for W:

$$W = \dfrac{2T}{n \mu (r_1 + r_2)} = \dfrac{2 \times 80}{6 \times 0.08 \times (0.060 + 0.090)}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate denominator:**

$$n \mu (r_1 + r_2) = 6 \times 0.08 \times 0.150 = 0.0720$$

**Step 3 — Compute W:**

$$W = \dfrac{160}{0.0720} = 2222.22 \text{ N} \approx 2.22 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Check maximum pressure (uniform wear):**

$$p_{max} = \dfrac{W}{2\pi r_1 (r_2 - r_1)} = \dfrac{2222.22}{2\pi \times 0.060 \times (0.090 - 0.060)}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate denominator:**

$$2\pi r_1 (r_2 - r_1) = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 0.060 \times 0.030 = 0.01131 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 6 — Compute $p_{max}$:**

$$p_{max} = \dfrac{2222.22}{0.01131} = 196{,}484 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.196 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 7 — Compare with allowable:**

$$p_{max} = 0.196 \text{ MPa} < p_{allow} = 0.5 \text{ MPa} \quad \textbf{SAFE}$$

**Final Answer:** $W = 2.22$ kN; pressure is within allowable limits. ✓

---

**Q2 (b) [7 Marks]** A single plate clutch is to be designed with uniform pressure theory. The friction lining material has $\mu = 0.35$ and allowable pressure $p = 0.5$ MPa. The clutch must transmit 1.5 kW at 1500 rpm with a safety factor of 1.5. If the outer diameter is 1.4 times the inner diameter, determine the inner and outer radii and the axial thrust.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate the design torque:**

$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$T_{mean} = \dfrac{P}{\omega} = \dfrac{1500}{157.08} = 9.549 \text{ N·m}$$

$$T_{design} = T_{mean} \times SF = 9.549 \times 1.5 = 14.324 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2 — Use the given ratio:**

$$d_2 = 1.4 \, d_1 \implies r_2 = 1.4 \, r_1$$

**Step 3 — Apply uniform pressure torque formula (single surface, n=1):**

$$T = \dfrac{2\mu W}{3}\left(\dfrac{r_2^3 - r_1^3}{r_2^2 - r_1^2}\right)$$

But we can also use $W = \pi p (r_2^2 - r_1^2)$ to eliminate W:

$$T = \dfrac{2\mu}{3} \cdot \pi p (r_2^3 - r_1^3)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $r_2 = 1.4 r_1$:**

$$r_2^3 - r_1^3 = r_1^3(1.4^3 - 1) = r_1^3(2.744 - 1) = 1.744 \, r_1^3$$

$$14.324 = \dfrac{2 \times 0.35}{3} \times \pi \times 0.5 \times 10^6 \times 1.744 \, r_1^3$$

**Step 5 — Compute the constant:**

$$\dfrac{2 \times 0.35}{3} \times \pi \times 0.5 \times 10^6 \times 1.744 = 0.6390 \times 10^6$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $r_1$:**

$$r_1^3 = \dfrac{14.324}{0.6390 \times 10^6} = 22.41 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$$

$$r_1 = (22.41 \times 10^{-6})^{1/3} = 0.02819 \text{ m} \approx 28.2 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 7 — Calculate $r_2$:**

$$r_2 = 1.4 \times 28.2 = 39.48 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 8 — Calculate axial thrust W:**

$$W = \pi p (r_2^2 - r_1^2) = \pi \times 0.5 \times 10^6 \times (0.03948^2 - 0.02819^2)$$

$$r_2^2 - r_1^2 = 0.0015586 - 0.0007947 = 0.0007639 \text{ m}^2$$

$$W = \pi \times 0.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.0007639 = 1199.7 \text{ N} \approx 1.2 \text{ kN}$$

**Final Answer:** $r_1 = 28.2$ mm, $r_2 = 39.5$ mm, $W = 1.2$ kN ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (Heat Dissipation & Slip Energy)**

**Q3 (a) [7 Marks]** A single plate clutch with uniform wear theory has $r_1 = 75$ mm, $r_2 = 125$ mm, and an axial thrust of 5 kN. The coefficient of friction is 0.3. The driving shaft runs at 1440 rpm while the driven shaft comes to rest from the same speed in 4 seconds during engagement. Calculate: (i) the torque transmitted, and (ii) the total energy dissipated as heat.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate the torque (uniform wear, n=1):**

$$T = \dfrac{\mu W (r_1 + r_2)}{2} = \dfrac{0.3 \times 5000 \times (0.075 + 0.125)}{2}$$

$$T = \dfrac{0.3 \times 5000 \times 0.200}{2} = \dfrac{300}{2} = 150 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate angular velocity:**

$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1440}{60} = 150.8 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the angular slip during engagement:**

The driven shaft decelerates from $\omega$ to 0 in 4 s (assuming uniform deceleration):

$$\theta_{slip} = \omega t - \dfrac{1}{2}\alpha t^2 = \omega t - \dfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{\omega}{t} t^2 = \dfrac{1}{2}\omega t$$

$$\theta_{slip} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 150.8 \times 4 = 301.6 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate total energy dissipated as heat:**

$$E = T \cdot \theta_{slip} = 150 \times 301.6 = 45{,}240 \text{ J} \approx 45.24 \text{ kJ}$$

**Final Answer:** (i) T = 150 N·m; (ii) E = 45.24 kJ ✓

---

**Q3 (b) [7 Marks]** If the cast iron clutch plate in Q3(a) above has a mass of 4 kg and specific heat of 500 J/(kg·K), and 30% of the heat is lost by radiation, find the temperature rise of the plate. Assume no conduction losses to the shaft.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Heat absorbed by the plate (70% of total):**

$$E_{absorbed} = 0.70 \times 45{,}240 = 31{,}668 \text{ J}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the heat equation:**

$$E_{absorbed} = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$$

$$\Delta T = \dfrac{E_{absorbed}}{m \cdot C_p} = \dfrac{31{,}668}{4 \times 500}$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$\Delta T = \dfrac{31{,}668}{2000} = 15.83 \text{ K} \approx 15.83 \text{ °C}$$

**Final Answer:** $\Delta T \approx 15.83$ °C ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by 'n' (number of contact surfaces) in multi-plate clutches.** A 5-disc wet clutch has $n = N + M - 1$, not 5!
> 2. **Mixing up theories in the same problem.** Once you assume uniform wear, use $p_{max} = W/[2\pi r_1 (r_2 - r_1)]$. Once you assume uniform pressure, $p_{max} = p$ everywhere.
> 3. **Unit mismatch.** Radii MUST be in **metres** when torque is in N·m and pressure in Pa (N/m²). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for unit inconsistency.
> 4. **Not converting rpm to rad/s.** Use $\omega = 2\pi N/60$, NOT $\omega = N$.
> 5. **Neglecting the safety factor** in design problems. Always state the SF used.
> 6. **Energy and power confusion.** Energy E = Tθ (J); Power P = Tω (W). They look similar but use **angular slip θ (radians)** vs **angular velocity ω (rad/s)**.
> 7. **Heat dissipation check is mandatory.** A clutch can transmit torque correctly but melt from frictional heat. Always verify $\Delta T \le 50$°C for ferodo, $\le 80$°C for sintered bronze.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Must Memorise**

- [x] **Two theories** — Uniform Pressure (new/rigid) and Uniform Wear (run-in/service). Default for design = **Uniform Wear**.
- [x] **Uniform Pressure Torque:** $T = \frac{2\mu W}{3}\cdot\frac{r_2^3 - r_1^3}{r_2^2 - r_1^2}$
- [x] **Uniform Wear Torque:** $T = \frac{\mu W}{2}(r_1 + r_2) = \mu W R_m$
- [x] **Multi-plate factor:** $T_{multi} = n \times T_{single}$ where $n = N + M - 1$
- [x] **Axial thrust under uniform pressure:** $W = \pi p (r_2^2 - r_1^2)$
- [x] **Axial thrust under uniform wear:** $W = 2\pi C (r_2 - r_1)$, where $C = Wr_1$ type constant.
- [x] **Max pressure check (uniform wear):** $p_{max} = W / [2\pi r_1 (r_2 - r_1)] \le p_{allow}$
- [x] **Slip velocity check:** $V = \omega r_2 \le 10$ m/s (dry), 25 m/s (wet).
- [x] **Optimum radius ratio:** $r_1/r_2 = 1/\sqrt{3} \approx 0.577$ for max torque (uniform wear, fixed outer radius).
- [x] **Practical radius ratio range:** $0.5 \le r_1/r_2 \le 0.75$ (industry standard).
- [x] **Energy of slip:** $E = T\theta$ (J); **Power loss:** $P = T\omega$ (W).
- [x] **Heat rise:** $\Delta T = E_{abs} / (m C_p)$; check $\Delta T \le 50$ °C for organic linings.
- [x] **Material pairs to remember:** Ferodo-Steel (μ ≈ 0.3–0.4, p ≈ 0.35–0.7 MPa); Cast Iron-Cast Iron (μ ≈ 0.1, p ≈ 0.25–0.5 MPa); Steel-Steel wet (μ ≈ 0.06–0.08, p ≈ 0.5–1.0 MPa); Sintered Bronze-Steel (μ ≈ 0.1, p ≈ 0.8–1.2 MPa).
- [x] **Number of contact surfaces in single-plate:** n = 1. In multi-plate: n = (driving discs) + (driven discs) − 1.
- [x] **Default n (motorcycle wet clutch):** 4–6 surfaces typical.
- [x] **Key synonyms in exam papers:** "disc clutch" = "plate clutch"; "axial force" = "thrust" = "clamping force" = W; "torque capacity" = "moment transmitting capacity" = T.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Belt Drive Power Transmission - Open and Crossed Belt. Brakes and Dynamometers (No Derivations): Simple Band Brake, Block Brake, Disk Brake
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET404 - THEORY OF MACHINES AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Belt Drive Power Transmission - Open and Crossed Belt. Brakes and Dynamometers (No Derivations): Simple Band Brake, Block Brake, Disk Brake

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Belt Drive — The Foundation of Flexible Power Transmission

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** A *belt drive* is a flexible power transmission system in which a flexible belt connects two pulleys mounted on parallel shafts to transmit power from one shaft to another by means of friction (or teeth in the case of synchronous/toothed belts). The pulleys may be arranged in an **open** or **crossed** configuration depending on the desired direction of rotation.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Towel & Two Roller Analogy

Imagine taking a long towel and looping it around two rotating rollers (like the rollers of an old washing machine). If both ends of the towel run straight down without twisting, both rollers spin in the **same direction**. If you cross the towel into a figure-of-eight (like tying your shoelaces), the two rollers now spin in **opposite directions**. This is precisely the difference between an **open belt** and a **crossed belt** drive. The towel is the *belt*, the rollers are the *pulleys*, and the friction between towel and roller is what transmits power.

### 1.2 Open Belt Drive

> [!NOTE]
> **Open Belt Drive:** The belt is looped around two pulleys such that the upper and lower straight portions of the belt run parallel without crossing. This causes both pulleys to rotate in the **same direction**.

**Key characteristics:**
- Used when shafts are **parallel and rotate in the same sense**.
- Rotation direction: **Same** for driver and driven.
- Side of the belt that approaches the driven pulley is called the **tight side**; the side leaving the driven pulley is the **slack side**.
- Standard drive for most industrial machinery (fans, pumps, conveyors).

### 1.3 Crossed Belt Drive

> [!NOTE]
> **Crossed Belt Drive:** The belt is intentionally twisted into a figure-of-eight configuration, causing the two pulleys to rotate in **opposite directions**.

**Key characteristics:**
- Used when shafts are **parallel but must rotate in opposite directions**.
- Rotation direction: **Opposite** for driver and driven.
- The belt crosses at the midpoint between the two pulleys.
- Wear is higher than open belt due to the rubbing/scraping action at the crossover point.

### 1.4 Brakes — The Stopping Force in Machines

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU):** A *brake* is a machine element (or mechanism) used to **absorb kinetic energy** of a moving body by means of friction, thereby reducing or completely arresting its motion. Brakes convert mechanical energy of motion into **heat energy** which is dissipated to the surroundings.

**Engineering Analogy — The Bicycle Hand-Brake:** When you squeeze the lever on a bicycle, rubber blocks (or pads) clamp down on the metal rim of the wheel. The friction between pads and rim opposes the wheel's rotation and dissipates the bicycle's kinetic energy as heat. Industrial brakes operate on exactly the same principle — at a much higher scale of force, speed, and power.

### 1.5 Dynamometers — The Power Measurers

> [!NOTE]
> **Dynamometer:** A device used to **measure the torque, speed, and power** transmitted by a rotating shaft, without extracting the net energy from the system (transmission dynamometer) or by absorbing and dissipating it as heat (absorption dynamometer).

**Analogy — The Bathroom Weighing Scale:** A dynamometer is to a rotating shaft what a weighing scale is to a body — it tells you "how much" (in this case, how much torque/power) is being transmitted. Just as the scale measures weight by balancing against a known spring force, a dynamometer measures torque by balancing against a known friction force.

### 1.6 Brakes vs. Dynamometers — The Subtle Distinction

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse brakes and dynamometers. The difference is **purpose**, not construction.
> - A **brake** is used to **slow down or stop** a moving body.
> - A **dynamometer** is used to **measure** the torque/power of a rotating shaft.
>
> However, the **friction principle** is identical — many dynamometers (e.g., rope brake dynamometer) are essentially brakes used in a measurement role.

### 1.7 Classification Snapshot

| Category | Sub-Types | KTU Focus |
|----------|-----------|-----------|
| **Belt Drives** | Open Belt, Crossed Belt, Belt with idler, Compound belt | Open & Crossed (this module) |
| **Brakes** | Band Brake, Block/Shoe Brake, Disk Brake, Internal Expanding Brake | Simple Band, Block, Disk (this module) |
| **Dynamometers** | Absorption (Rope Brake, Prony Brake, Hydraulic), Transmission (Epicyclic, Torsion) | Overview only (this module) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Belt Drive — Operational Theory

### 2.1.1 Velocity Ratio (Speed Ratio)

The velocity ratio is the ratio of the speed of the driver pulley to the speed of the driven pulley.

For both open and crossed belts, assuming **no slip** between the belt and pulleys:

$$N_1 \cdot D_1 = N_2 \cdot D_2$$

Therefore, the **velocity ratio** $i$ is:

$$i = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{D_2}{D_1}$$

where:
- $N_1$ = speed of driver pulley (rpm)
- $N_2$ = speed of driven pulley (rpm)
- $D_1$ = diameter of driver pulley (m)
- $D_2$ = diameter of driven pulley (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Belt velocity** $v$ at any point on the belt is the same as the linear velocity at the rim of either pulley:
> $$v = \frac{\pi D_1 N_1}{60} = \frac{\pi D_2 N_2}{60} \quad \text{(m/s)}$$

### 2.1.2 Angle of Contact (Wrap Angle)

The angle of contact is the angle (in radians) over which the belt wraps around each pulley. This is **critical** because the larger the wrap angle, the more friction grip the belt has.

**For Open Belt Drive:**

$$\theta_{\text{large}} = \pi + 2\alpha \quad \text{(on the larger pulley)}$$

$$\theta_{\text{small}} = \pi - 2\alpha \quad \text{(on the smaller pulley)}$$

where $\alpha$ is the **angle of inclination** of the belt run, given by:

$$\sin \alpha = \frac{R - r}{C} = \frac{D_2 - D_1}{2C}$$

with $C$ = center distance between the two pulleys.

**For Crossed Belt Drive:**

$$\theta_1 = \theta_2 = \pi + 2\alpha \quad \text{(wrap angle is the same on both pulleys)}$$

This means the crossed belt has a **larger wrap angle** on both pulleys — a key advantage (more friction grip) at the cost of higher belt wear.

### 2.1.3 Length of Belt

**For Open Belt Drive:**

$$L_{\text{open}} = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D_1 + D_2) + \frac{(D_2 - D_1)^2}{4C}$$

**For Crossed Belt Drive:**

$$L_{\text{cross}} = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D_1 + D_2) + \frac{(D_1 + D_2)^2}{4C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **difference** $L_{\text{cross}} - L_{\text{open}} = \dfrac{D_1 \cdot D_2}{C}$ — this shows that for small pulleys close together, crossed belts require significantly more length.

### 2.1.4 Belt Tensions and Power Transmission

When the belt is in motion and transmitting power, the belt has **two tensions**:
- **Tight side tension** $T_1$ (on the side approaching the driven pulley)
- **Slack side tension** $T_2$ (on the side leaving the driven pulley)

The **effective driving force** is:

$$P_{\text{eff}} = T_1 - T_2$$

The **Euler's Belt Friction Equation** (capstan equation) gives the relationship:

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$$

where:
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction between belt and pulley
- $\theta$ = angle of wrap (in **radians**)

**Centrifugal Tension** $T_c$ acts on the belt when it moves at velocity $v$ and must be considered for high-speed drives:

$$T_c = m v^2 = \frac{w \cdot v^2}{g}$$

where:
- $m$ = mass of belt per unit length (kg/m)
- $w$ = weight of belt per unit length (N/m)
- $v$ = belt velocity (m/s)
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Maximum Power Transmitted:**

$$P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v = (T - T_c)\left(1 - \frac{1}{e^{\mu \theta}}\right) \cdot v$$

where $T$ is the maximum allowable tension in the belt.

### 2.1.5 Condition for Maximum Power Transmission

The belt velocity for maximum power transmission (taking centrifugal tension into account) is:

$$v_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{T \cdot g}{3 w}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** Belt drives are ubiquitous — from washing machines, ceiling fans, and car alternators to industrial conveyor systems and agricultural machinery. Understanding the wrap angle, friction, and tension ratio is essential to design drives that don't slip and don't break.

## 2.2 Brakes — Operational Theory

### 2.2.1 Simple Band Brake

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A *simple band brake* consists of a flexible band (steel or fabric) wrapped around a rotating drum. One end of the band is fixed to a lever; the other end is attached to a fulcrum. When a force is applied at the lever end, the band tightens around the drum, creating friction that opposes rotation.

**Operating principle:** The friction between the band and the drum generates a braking torque:

$$M_t = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot R = \mu \cdot T_n \cdot R$$

**Direction of rotation effect (KTU high-yield):**
- If the drum rotates in the **direction that tightens the band** (band rotates with drum onto the fixed end first), $T_1$ is on the tight side as expected.
- If the drum rotates in the **direction that slackens the band** (self-energizing becomes self-de-energizing), the brake is **differentially acting** and the required actuating force is much higher.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The "self-energizing" and "self-de-energizing" direction of a band brake is a favourite exam question. Always draw the FBD and identify which end of the band is fixed.

### 2.2.2 Block (Shoe) Brake

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A *block brake* uses one or two rigid blocks (shoes) lined with friction material (e.g., asbestos, ceramic composite) that press against a rotating drum. The blocks are actuated by a lever and a pivot.

**Two main sub-types:**
1. **Single Block Brake** — only one block, used for light-duty applications (e.g., hand brake on small machinery).
2. **Double Block (or Two-Shoe) Brake** — two blocks symmetrically placed at $\pm 90°$ (or other angles) to provide balanced braking.

**Operating principle:** The normal reaction $\mu N$ at the friction surface creates a braking torque:

$$M_t = \mu \cdot N \cdot R$$

**KTU Note:** When two blocks are used, the total braking torque is the **sum** of torques from both blocks (assuming equal normal reactions):

$$M_{t,\text{total}} = \mu (N_1 + N_2) R$$

### 2.2.3 Disk Brake

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A *disk brake* consists of a flat (or ventilated) rotating disk attached to the shaft, and one or more friction pads (calipers) that press on **both faces** of the disk. Disk brakes are the modern standard for automobiles, two-wheelers, and high-speed machinery.

**Operating principle:** The braking torque for a single-sided disk brake with $n$ friction surfaces (faces) is:

$$M_t = \mu \cdot N \cdot n \cdot R_{\text{mean}}$$

For a **double-sided disk brake** (pads on both sides):

$$M_t = 2 \mu \cdot N \cdot R_{\text{mean}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why disk brakes replaced drum brakes in automobiles:**
> - Better heat dissipation (disk is exposed to air).
> - Self-cleaning action (water and debris fling off by centrifugal force).
> - More uniform pressure distribution.
> - Less prone to **fading** at high temperatures.
> - Easier to modulate (good for anti-lock braking systems / ABS).

### 2.2.4 Braking Efficiency

The **braking efficiency** $\eta_b$ of a single-block brake with pivoted block is:

$$\eta_b = \frac{\mu \cdot (h + \mu \cdot a)}{h - \mu \cdot a}$$

where:
- $h$ = vertical distance from pivot to line of action of braking force
- $a$ = horizontal distance from pivot to line of action of braking force
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Self-energizing condition:** If $\eta_b > 1$, the brake is **self-energizing** (rotation assists braking, reducing required effort). If $\eta_b < 1$, it is **self-de-energizing**.

## 2.3 Dynamometers — Operational Theory

### 2.3.1 Types of Dynamometers

> [!NOTE]
> **1. Absorption Dynamometers** — absorb and dissipate the energy as heat (e.g., rope brake, Prony brake, hydraulic dynamometer).
> **2. Transmission Dynamometers** — measure transmitted power without absorbing net energy (e.g., torsion, epicyclic, belt transmission dynamometers).

### 2.3.2 Rope Brake Dynamometer

A rope wound several turns around a rotating drum (mounted on the shaft whose power is to be measured) with weights $W$ and $S$ at the two ends. The net tangential force is $(W - S)$, and the torque measured is:

$$M_t = (W - S) \cdot R \quad \text{(N·m)}$$

The **power** transmitted is:

$$P = \frac{2 \pi N \cdot M_t}{60} = \frac{2 \pi N (W - S) R}{60} \quad \text{(W)}$$

### 2.3.3 Prony Brake Dynamometer

A wooden block (or friction pad) is pressed against a rotating pulley. The friction force creates a torque that is balanced by a lever arm and a known dead weight. The torque equation and power equation are the same in form as the rope brake, but with the difference that the friction coefficient is provided by the block-pulley interface.

### 2.3.4 Epicyclic (Transmission) Dynamometer

Uses an epicyclic gear train to measure torque. The relative motion between the gear and the planet carrier is proportional to the transmitted torque. By holding one component fixed and allowing another to rotate against a spring, the torque can be directly read on a calibrated scale.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is your one-stop cheat sheet** for all possible KTU exam questions on this module. Memorize it.

| **#** | **Quantity / Concept** | **Formula** | **Units / Remarks** |
|:-----:|------------------------|-------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Velocity ratio (open & crossed) | $i = N_1 / N_2 = D_2 / D_1$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Belt linear velocity | $v = \pi D_1 N_1 / 60$ | m/s |
| 3 | Wrap angle, open (large) | $\theta = \pi + 2\alpha$ | radians |
| 4 | Wrap angle, open (small) | $\theta = \pi - 2\alpha$ | radians |
| 5 | Wrap angle, crossed (both) | $\theta = \pi + 2\alpha$ | radians |
| 6 | $\sin \alpha$ for open belt | $\sin \alpha = (D_2 - D_1) / (2C)$ | $C$ = center distance |
| 7 | $\sin \alpha$ for crossed belt | $\sin \alpha = (D_1 + D_2) / (2C)$ | — |
| 8 | Belt length, open | $L = 2C + (\pi/2)(D_1 + D_2) + (D_2 - D_1)^2 / (4C)$ | meters |
| 9 | Belt length, crossed | $L = 2C + (\pi/2)(D_1 + D_2) + (D_1 + D_2)^2 / (4C)$ | meters |
| 10 | Tension ratio (Euler) | $T_1 / T_2 = e^{\mu \theta}$ | $\theta$ in rad |
| 11 | Centrifugal tension | $T_c = w v^2 / g$ | Newtons |
| 12 | Max power condition velocity | $v_{\max} = \sqrt{T g / (3w)}$ | m/s |
| 13 | Effective driving force | $P_{\text{eff}} = T_1 - T_2$ | Newtons |
| 14 | Power transmitted | $P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v$ | Watts |
| 15 | Band brake torque | $M_t = (T_1 - T_2) R = \mu T_n R$ | N·m |
| 16 | Block brake torque (single) | $M_t = \mu N R$ | N·m |
| 17 | Block brake torque (double) | $M_t = \mu (N_1 + N_2) R$ | N·m |
| 18 | Disk brake torque | $M_t = \mu N n R_{\text{mean}}$ | N·m |
| 19 | Braking efficiency | $\eta_b = \mu (h + \mu a) / (h - \mu a)$ | Dimensionless |
| 20 | Rope brake torque | $M_t = (W - S) R$ | N·m |
| 21 | Power measured (rotating shaft) | $P = 2 \pi N M_t / 60$ | Watts |
| 22 | Self-energizing condition | $\mu a \ge h$ (for block brake) | $\eta_b \to \infty$ |
| 23 | Slip condition | $T_1 / T_2 < e^{\mu \theta}$ | Belt slips |
| 24 | No-slip (design) | $T_1 / T_2 \le e^{\mu \theta}$ | Used for design margin |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** Every formula in this table has a real industrial counterpart. From the design of a simple fan belt to the caliper-and-disk system in your car to the dynamometer that certifies an aircraft engine's power output — these are the workhorse equations of power transmission and machine design.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Note (KTU 2024):** The topic "Brakes and Dynamometers" is **explicitly marked "No Derivations"**. Therefore, only the **belt drive derivations** are shown in this section. The brake and dynamometer material is summarized as an **analytical matrix** with final results and qualitative reasoning only.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Length of an Open Belt Drive

### Objective

To derive the closed-form expression for the length $L$ of an open belt passing over two pulleys of diameters $D_1$ and $D_2$ with center distance $C$.

### Setup

Let:
- Pulley 1 (driver) — radius $R_1 = D_1 / 2$
- Pulley 2 (driven) — radius $R_2 = D_2 / 2$
- Center distance $C$ (assumed $D_2 > D_1$ without loss of generality)
- $O_1$ and $O_2$ = centers of the two pulleys
- The belt touches pulley 1 along an arc of wrap $\pi - 2\alpha$ and pulley 2 along an arc of wrap $\pi + 2\alpha$.
- The two straight runs of the belt are inclined at angle $\alpha$ to the line of centers.

### Step 1 — Geometry of the straight portions

The straight portions of the belt, when extended, would meet the line $O_1O_2$ at points that are offset by $R_1$ and $R_2$ perpendicular to the belt direction. The geometry of the small triangle formed by these offsets and the center distance gives:

$$\sin \alpha = \frac{R_2 - R_1}{C} = \frac{D_2 - D_1}{2C}$$

This equation **defines** the angle $\alpha$ in terms of the known geometry.

### Step 2 — Length of straight portions

The straight portion of the belt on each side has a projected length of $\sqrt{C^2 - (R_2 - R_1)^2}$ along the line of centers. There are **two** such straight portions, so:

$$L_{\text{straight}} = 2 \sqrt{C^2 - (R_2 - R_1)^2}$$

This can be expanded using the binomial approximation (for $C \gg R_2 - R_1$):

$$L_{\text{straight}} = 2C \sqrt{1 - \frac{(R_2 - R_1)^2}{C^2}} \approx 2C \left[1 - \frac{(R_2 - R_1)^2}{2 C^2} \right] = 2C - \frac{(R_2 - R_1)^2}{C}$$

### Step 3 — Length of arc portions

The belt wraps around the smaller pulley through an angle $(\pi - 2\alpha)$ and around the larger pulley through an angle $(\pi + 2\alpha)$. Arc length on a circle of radius $R$ subtended by angle $\theta$ is $R \theta$.

$$L_{\text{arc}} = R_1 (\pi - 2\alpha) + R_2 (\pi + 2\alpha)$$

$$L_{\text{arc}} = \pi (R_1 + R_2) + 2\alpha (R_2 - R_1)$$

For small $\alpha$ (typical engineering case where $C \gg D$), the term $2\alpha (R_2 - R_1)$ is very small. We can expand $\alpha$ for small arguments:

$$\alpha \approx \frac{R_2 - R_1}{C} = \frac{D_2 - D_1}{2C}$$

Therefore:

$$2\alpha (R_2 - R_1) \approx \frac{2(R_2 - R_1)^2}{C} = \frac{(D_2 - D_1)^2}{2C}$$

### Step 4 — Total belt length

Summing straight and arc contributions:

$$L = L_{\text{straight}} + L_{\text{arc}}$$

$$L \approx \left[2C - \frac{(R_2 - R_1)^2}{C}\right] + \left[\pi (R_1 + R_2) + \frac{(R_2 - R_1)^2}{C} \cdot 2 \right]$$

Wait — the sign of the arc term needs careful tracking. The corrected classical result, retaining only first-order terms in $(R_2-R_1)/C$, is the well-known KTU formula:

$$L_{\text{open}} = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D_1 + D_2) + \frac{(D_2 - D_1)^2}{4C}$$

### Step 5 — Final compact form

$$\boxed{L_{\text{open}} = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D_1 + D_2) + \frac{(D_2 - D_1)^2}{4C}}$$

**Remarks:**
- The first term $2C$ is the dominant contribution (twice the center distance).
- The second term $\frac{\pi}{2}(D_1 + D_2)$ accounts for the half-circumferences around the two pulleys.
- The third term $\frac{(D_2 - D_1)^2}{4C}$ is a **correction** that accounts for the belt being inclined (it goes to zero when the pulleys are equal in diameter).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Length of a Crossed Belt Drive

### Objective

To derive the length of a crossed belt connecting two pulleys of diameters $D_1$ and $D_2$ with center distance $C$.

### Step 1 — Geometry of straight portions

For a crossed belt, the two straight runs cross each other at the midpoint. Each straight run is inclined at angle $\alpha$ to the line of centers, but now the geometry gives:

$$\sin \alpha = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{C} = \frac{D_1 + D_2}{2C}$$

Note the **+ sign** in the numerator (versus − for the open belt) — the radii add because the belt is crossing.

### Step 2 — Length of straight portions

Projecting the straight run onto the line of centers, the projected length is $\sqrt{C^2 - (R_1 + R_2)^2}$. There are two such straight portions, so:

$$L_{\text{straight}} = 2 \sqrt{C^2 - (R_1 + R_2)^2}$$

Using the same binomial approximation (valid for $C \gg R_1 + R_2$):

$$L_{\text{straight}} \approx 2C - \frac{(R_1 + R_2)^2}{C}$$

### Step 3 — Length of arc portions

For the crossed belt, the wrap angle is the **same on both pulleys**:

$$\theta_1 = \theta_2 = \pi + 2\alpha$$

Therefore the total arc length is:

$$L_{\text{arc}} = R_1 (\pi + 2\alpha) + R_2 (\pi + 2\alpha) = \pi(R_1 + R_2) + 2\alpha(R_1 + R_2)$$

Expanding $2\alpha \approx 2(R_1 + R_2)/C$:

$$L_{\text{arc}} \approx \pi (R_1 + R_2) + \frac{2(R_1 + R_2)^2}{C}$$

### Step 4 — Total length

Summing:

$$L_{\text{cross}} \approx 2C - \frac{(R_1 + R_2)^2}{C} + \pi(R_1 + R_2) + \frac{2(R_1 + R_2)^2}{C}$$

$$L_{\text{cross}} \approx 2C + \pi(R_1 + R_2) + \frac{(R_1 + R_2)^2}{C}$$

Converting to diameters and writing in the standard KTU form:

$$\boxed{L_{\text{cross}} = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D_1 + D_2) + \frac{(D_1 + D_2)^2}{4C}}$$

### Step 5 — Comparison with Open Belt

The **difference in length** between crossed and open belts is:

$$L_{\text{cross}} - L_{\text{open}} = \frac{(D_1 + D_2)^2 - (D_2 - D_1)^2}{4C} = \frac{4 D_1 D_2}{4C} = \frac{D_1 D_2}{C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A crossed belt is **always longer** than an open belt of the same geometry, by an amount $D_1 D_2 / C$. The shorter the center distance and the larger the pulleys, the bigger the difference. This is why crossed belts are **not** preferred for very small pulleys close together.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Centrifugal Tension in a Belt

### Step 1 — Set up the free body

Consider an elemental arc $ds = R \, d\theta$ of the belt wrapped around a pulley of radius $R$ and rotating at angular speed $\omega$ (linear belt speed $v = \omega R$). Let $m$ be the mass per unit length of the belt.

The centrifugal force on this element is:

$$dF_c = (m \, ds) \cdot \frac{v^2}{R} = m \cdot R \, d\theta \cdot \frac{v^2}{R} = m v^2 \, d\theta$$

### Step 2 — Resolve the components

The centrifugal force acts **radially outward**. The component of $dF_c$ along the belt (tangential) at the element creates the centrifugal tension $T_c$.

For small $d\theta$:

$$dT_c = dF_c \cdot \cos \phi$$

where $\phi$ is the angle between the radius and the belt tangent at the element. In the limit $d\theta \to 0$, the integration gives:

$$T_c = m v^2 \quad \text{(per side)}$$

### Step 3 — Final expression

Converting to weight per unit length $w = mg$:

$$\boxed{T_c = \frac{w v^2}{g}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This tension is **uniform along the entire belt** and is independent of the pulley size. It acts equally on the tight and slack sides.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Brakes and Dynamometers (No Derivations per KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus compliance:** Per the KTU 2024 module description, no derivations are required for Brakes and Dynamometers. The following matrix gives the **final governing equations**, the **physical meaning** of each term, and the **qualitative insight** required to answer exam questions.

| **Component** | **Governing Equation** | **Variable Meaning** | **Qualitative Insight** |
|---------------|------------------------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| **Simple Band Brake (Differential)** | $T_1 / T_2 = e^{\mu \theta}$ (both sides) | $T_1$ = tight, $T_2$ = slack, $\theta$ = wrap angle | If both ends are fixed to a lever at different points, the brake is **differential** — direction of rotation matters. |
| **Band Brake — Self-energizing** | If rotation tightens the band → $T_1$ on the **fixed** end | $T_1$ is determined by $\mu$ and $T_2$ | Brake becomes self-locking if $\mu \to \tan(\phi)$ where $\phi$ is the half-wrap angle. |
| **Block Brake — Single Block** | $M_t = \mu N R$ | $N$ = normal force on block, $R$ = drum radius | Symmetric or pivoted blocks give different $N$ values. |
| **Block Brake — Double Block (symmetric)** | $M_t = \mu (N_1 + N_2) R$ | $N_1, N_2$ = normal forces on each block | If levers are equal and pivoted at equal distance, $N_1 = N_2$ and $M_t = 2 \mu N R$. |
| **Block Brake — Braking Efficiency** | $\eta_b = \mu (h + \mu a) / (h - \mu a)$ | $h$ = vertical pivot distance, $a$ = horizontal pivot distance | $\eta_b > 1$ → **self-energizing**; $\eta_b < 1$ → self-de-energizing. |
| **Disk Brake — Single Pad** | $M_t = \mu N R_{\text{mean}}$ | $R_{\text{mean}}$ = mean pad radius | Single-side disk brake is rare in practice (uneven wear). |
| **Disk Brake — Double Pad (Automotive)** | $M_t = 2 \mu N R_{\text{mean}}$ | Factor 2 accounts for both sides of disk | Standard automotive disk brake has 2 or 4 pistons. |
| **Rope Brake Dynamometer** | $M_t = (W - S) R$ | $W$ = tight-side weight, $S$ = slack-side weight | Used for medium-power engines (up to $\sim$50 kW). |
| **Prony Brake Dynamometer** | $P = 2 \pi N (F \cdot l) / 60$ | $F$ = spring balance reading, $l$ = lever arm | Wooden blocks used; good for laboratory demonstration. |
| **Epicyclic Dynamometer** | Torque $\propto$ spring deflection | Output read on a calibrated scale | Used for high-speed and high-power testing (no heat absorption). |
| **Hydraulic Dynamometer** | Energy absorbed = $\rho Q g H$ | $Q$ = flow, $H$ = head, $\rho$ = density of water | Used for very large engines (marine, locomotive). |

### 3.4.1 Quick-Reference Logic Steps for Block Brake Force Analysis

Even though **no derivations are required**, KTU often tests the FBD logic. Here is the **non-derivation** analytical chain (no equations derived from first principles — just identification and substitution):

1. **Identify** the geometry: locate the pivot, the line of action of the applied force, the line of action of the normal reaction, and the friction force ($\mu N$).
2. **Identify** the drum rotation direction (clockwise or counter-clockwise) — this sets the direction of the friction force on the block.
3. **Apply** moment equilibrium of the lever about the pivot: $\sum M_{\text{pivot}} = 0$.
4. **Substitute** $F_{\text{friction}} = \mu N$ and solve for the unknown force or torque.

### 3.4.2 Energy and Power Relationship in Braking

When a brake brings a body from angular velocity $\omega_0$ to rest, the kinetic energy dissipated is:

$$E_{\text{kinetic}} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega_0^2$$

The **rate of heat generation** at any instant is:

$$Q = M_t \cdot \omega = \mu N R \omega$$

This heat must be **dissipated to the surroundings** — failure to dissipate it leads to **brake fade** (loss of friction coefficient at high temperature) and potential brake failure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Warning:** Students often forget that the **direction of rotation** of the drum affects the direction of the friction force on the block in a block brake. This changes the sign of certain terms in the moment equation. Always draw a clear FBD.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid diagrams are used to render professional, KTU-board-quality schematics. Each diagram is carefully labeled with clean, alphanumeric identifiers (no markdown formatting inside labels).

## 4.1 Belt Drive — Open Configuration Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A1[Driver Pulley]
        P1((Pulley 1<br/>D1, N1))
    end
    subgraph A2[Driven Pulley]
        P2((Pulley 2<br/>D2, N2))
    end
    T1[TIGHT SIDE<br/>Tension T1] -->|wraps over| P1
    P1 -->|slack side<br/>Tension T2| T2[SLACK SIDE]
    T2 --> P2
    P2 -->|tight side| T1
    CW1[Rotation: CW] --- P1
    CW2[Rotation: CW] --- P2
    A1 ---|Center distance C| A2
```

**Interpretation:** Both pulleys rotate in the **same direction** (here, clockwise). The tight side has tension $T_1$ and the slack side has tension $T_2$. The two pulleys are separated by center distance $C$.

## 4.2 Belt Drive — Crossed Configuration Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph B1[Driver Pulley]
        Q1((Pulley 1<br/>D1, N1))
    end
    subgraph B2[Driven Pulley]
        Q2((Pulley 2<br/>D2, N2))
    end
    X1((Cross<br/>Point)) --- Q1
    X1 --- Q2
    CW1[Rotation: CW] --- Q1
    CCW2[Rotation: CCW] --- Q2
    B1 ---|Center distance C| B2
    Q1 -->|one straight run| X1
    Q1 -->|other straight run| X1
    X1 --> Q2
    X1 --> Q2
```

**Interpretation:** The two straight runs of the belt **cross at the midpoint** between the pulleys. This forces the two pulleys to rotate in **opposite directions** (here, driver CW, driven CCW).

## 4.3 Simple Band Brake — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SHAFT[Rotating Shaft] --> DRUM[Drum<br/>Radius R]
    DRUM -->|wrapped by| BAND[Friction Band]
    BAND -->|fixed at| END1[End A: Fulcrum / Pivot]
    BAND -->|actuated at| END2[End B: Lever]
    LEV[Lever] -->|Force P applied| END2
    BAND -->|Normal Pressure| FRIC[Friction Force<br/>mu times Tn]
    FRIC -->|generates| TORQUE[Braking Torque Mt]
    HEAT[Heat Dissipation] -->|energy out| AIR[Surroundings]
    TORQUE --> HEAT
```

**Interpretation:** The rotating drum is gripped by the friction band. The band has one end fixed (End A) and the other end attached to a lever (End B) where the operating force $P$ is applied. The friction creates a braking torque that converts the shaft's kinetic energy into heat.

## 4.4 Block Brake — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DRUM2[Rotating Drum<br/>Radius R] -->|rotates| CONTACT[Friction Contact Zone]
    CONTACT -->|Normal N| BLOCK1[Block / Shoe 1<br/>Friction lining]
    CONTACT -->|Normal N| BLOCK2[Block / Shoe 2<br/>Friction lining]
    BLOCK1 -->|Reaction force| LEV1[Lever 1]
    BLOCK2 -->|Reaction force| LEV2[Lever 2]
    LEV1 -->|Pivot| PIVOT1[Pivot A]
    LEV2 -->|Pivot| PIVOT2[Pivot B]
    LEV1 -->|Force F| ACT[Actuator<br/>Hydraulic / Mechanical]
    LEV2 -->|Force F| ACT
    ACT -->|generates| N[Normal Force N on blocks]
    N --> CONTACT
    CONTACT -->|produces| MT2[Braking Torque Mt = 2 mu N R]
```

**Interpretation:** A double-block (two-shoe) brake uses symmetric blocks on opposite sides of the drum. The actuator applies force through the levers, pressing the blocks against the drum. Friction on both sides doubles the braking torque.

## 4.5 Disk Brake — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SHAFT3[Rotating Shaft] -->|attached to| DISK[Disk / Rotor<br/>Mean radius Rm]
    DISK -->|face 1| PAD1[Friction Pad 1<br/>Inboard caliper]
    DISK -->|face 2| PAD2[Friction Pad 2<br/>Outboard caliper]
    PAD1 -->|actuated by| PISTON1[Piston 1<br/>Hydraulic pressure]
    PAD2 -->|actuated by| PISTON2[Piston 2<br/>Hydraulic pressure]
    PISTON1 -->|line to| MASTER[Master Cylinder<br/>Driver input]
    PISTON2 -->|line to| MASTER
    PAD1 -->|friction| FRIC1[Friction mu N]
    PAD2 -->|friction| FRIC2[Friction mu N]
    FRIC1 -->|torque contribution| TORQUE2[Braking Torque Mt]
    FRIC2 -->|torque contribution| TORQUE2
    DISK -->|centrifugal| VENT[Air Cooling<br/>Ventilated rotor]
```

**Interpretation:** A modern automotive disk brake has a rotor (disk) attached to the wheel hub, and a caliper with pistons that press friction pads on **both sides** of the rotor when hydraulic pressure is applied. The resulting friction on both faces provides powerful, well-distributed braking. The exposed disk surface allows for superior heat dissipation.

## 4.6 Dynamometer — Functional Architecture (Rope Brake)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E[Engine / Motor<br/>Shaft to be tested] -->|drives| DRUM3[Pulley / Drum on shaft]
    DRUM3 -->|wrapped by| ROPE[Multi-turn rope]
    ROPE -->|End 1| WT[Tight-side weight W]
    ROPE -->|End 2| WS[Slack-side weight S]
    WT -->|creates| T1FORCE[Tangential force W]
    WS -->|creates| T2FORCE[Tangential force S]
    T1FORCE -->|net force| NETF[W minus S]
    NETF -->|torque| MTORQUE[Torque Mt = W minus S times R]
    DRUM3 -->|rotation N| SPD[Speed in rpm]
    MTORQUE -->|with N| POWER[Power P = 2 pi N Mt / 60]
    SPD --> POWER
```

**Interpretation:** A rope brake dynamometer uses a rope wound around a rotating drum, with known weights at each end. The difference in weights gives the tangential braking force, and hence the torque. Combined with the measured speed, the power transmitted by the shaft is calculated.

## 4.7 Master Comparison Diagram — Open vs. Crossed Belt

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Two Pulleys on Parallel Shafts] --> Q1{Do shafts rotate<br/>in the same<br/>direction?}
    Q1 -->|YES| OPEN[OPEN BELT DRIVE]
    Q1 -->|NO| CROSS[CROSSED BELT DRIVE]
    OPEN --> O1[Wrap angles different]
    OPEN --> O2[Length: 2C + pi/2 times D1 + D2 + D2 minus D1 squared over 4C]
    OPEN --> O3[Belt runs straight, parallel]
    OPEN --> O4[Lower wear, preferred for high speeds]
    CROSS --> C1[Wrap angles equal: pi + 2 alpha on both]
    CROSS --> C2[Length: 2C + pi/2 times D1 + D2 + D1 + D2 squared over 4C]
    CROSS --> C3[Belt crosses at midpoint]
    CROSS --> C4[Higher wear at crossover]
    O4 --> USES1[Common applications:<br/>fans, pumps, conveyors]
    C4 --> USES2[Common applications:<br/>countershaft setups, reversing]
```
