# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All questions below are modelled strictly on KTU 2024 ESE patterns.** They reflect the typical mark distribution (3-mark short questions, 14-mark long questions with internal choice) and the depth expected in Board evaluations.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define an Open Belt Drive and a Crossed Belt Drive. State any two differences between them. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

An **open belt drive** is a flexible belt drive in which the belt is looped around two pulleys mounted on parallel shafts such that the straight portions of the belt run **parallel** to each other without crossing. Both pulleys rotate in the **same direction**.

A **crossed belt drive** is a flexible belt drive in which the belt is looped around two pulleys in a **figure-of-eight** configuration, with the two straight portions crossing each other at the midpoint between the pulleys. The two pulleys rotate in **opposite directions**.

**Two differences:**

| Aspect | Open Belt | Crossed Belt |
|--------|-----------|--------------|
| Rotation of pulleys | Same direction | Opposite directions |
| Wrap angle | $\pi + 2\alpha$ on larger, $\pi - 2\alpha$ on smaller (unequal) | $\pi + 2\alpha$ on **both** (equal) |
| Belt length | $2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D_1+D_2) + \frac{(D_2-D_1)^2}{4C}$ | $2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D_1+D_2) + \frac{(D_1+D_2)^2}{4C}$ |
| Wear | Lower (no crossover friction) | Higher (rubbing at crossover) |
| Power capacity | Higher (preferred for high speeds) | Lower (used at lower speeds) |

---

### Question 2: What is a Dynamometer? Differentiate between Absorption and Transmission Dynamometers. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **dynamometer** is a device used to measure the **torque, speed, and power** transmitted by a rotating shaft. It does so by balancing the torque of the shaft against a known force or moment.

**Differences:**

| Aspect | Absorption Dynamometer | Transmission Dynamometer |
|--------|------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Energy** | Absorbs and dissipates the power as heat (e.g., friction, hydraulic) | Does not absorb net energy; only measures the transmitted torque |
| **Use** | Testing engines, motors, prime movers | Continuous power-flow measurement in transmission lines |
| **Examples** | Rope brake, Prony brake, Hydraulic, Froude (epicyclic absorption type) | Epicyclic (transmission type), Torsion, Belt transmission dynamometer |
| **Cooling** | Requires cooling arrangement (water or air) | No cooling required |
| **Power limit** | Limited by heat dissipation capacity | Practically unlimited |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (typically (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks). Two alternative choices are given — answer **either** Choice A **or** Choice B. Both choices below are completely independent and cover the breadth of this module.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Belt Drive Analysis

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Two parallel shafts are connected by an **open belt** drive. The diameters of the pulleys are 300 mm and 150 mm respectively, and the center distance between the shafts is 4 m. If the belt runs at a linear speed of 15 m/s, find:
1. The angle of contact on each pulley.
2. The length of the belt.
3. The velocity ratio (assuming no slip).

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** In a crossed belt drive connecting two pulleys of diameters 200 mm and 400 mm with a center distance of 3 m, calculate:
1. The angle of contact on each pulley.
2. The length of the belt.
3. The difference in length compared to an open belt of the same geometry.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given:**
- $D_1 = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.3 \text{ m}$, $D_2 = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$
- $C = 4 \text{ m}$, $v = 15 \text{ m/s}$

**Step 1 — Angle of contact:**

$$\sin \alpha = \frac{D_2 - D_1}{2C} = \frac{0.15 - 0.3}{2 \times 4} = \frac{-0.15}{8} = -0.01875$$

Since $D_2 < D_1$, the larger pulley is pulley 1. The negative sign indicates that the larger pulley has the **larger wrap angle**.

$$\alpha = \arcsin(0.01875) \approx 1.075° \approx 0.01876 \text{ rad}$$

**Angle of contact on smaller pulley (pulley 2):**

$$\theta_2 = \pi - 2\alpha = 3.1416 - 2(0.01876) = 3.1041 \text{ rad} \approx 177.85°$$

**Angle of contact on larger pulley (pulley 1):**

$$\theta_1 = \pi + 2\alpha = 3.1416 + 2(0.01876) = 3.1791 \text{ rad} \approx 182.15°$$

**Step 2 — Length of belt:**

$$L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D_1 + D_2) + \frac{(D_2 - D_1)^2}{4C}$$

$$L = 2(4) + \frac{\pi}{2}(0.3 + 0.15) + \frac{(0.15 - 0.3)^2}{4 \times 4}$$

$$L = 8 + \frac{\pi}{2}(0.45) + \frac{(0.0225)}{16}$$

$$L = 8 + 0.7069 + 0.0014 = 8.7083 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Velocity ratio:**

$$i = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{D_2}{D_1} = \frac{0.15}{0.3} = 0.5$$

So the driven pulley rotates at **twice** the speed of the driver.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** Crossed belt, $D_1 = 0.2 \text{ m}$, $D_2 = 0.4 \text{ m}$, $C = 3 \text{ m}$.

**Step 1 — Angle of contact:**

$$\sin \alpha = \frac{D_1 + D_2}{2C} = \frac{0.2 + 0.4}{2 \times 3} = \frac{0.6}{6} = 0.1$$

$$\alpha = \arcsin(0.1) \approx 5.739° \approx 0.1002 \text{ rad}$$

Wrap angle (same on both pulleys for crossed belt):

$$\theta_1 = \theta_2 = \pi + 2\alpha = 3.1416 + 2(0.1002) = 3.3420 \text{ rad} \approx 191.48°$$

**Step 2 — Length of belt:**

$$L_{\text{cross}} = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D_1 + D_2) + \frac{(D_1 + D_2)^2}{4C}$$

$$L_{\text{cross}} = 2(3) + \frac{\pi}{2}(0.2 + 0.4) + \frac{(0.2 + 0.4)^2}{4 \times 3}$$

$$L_{\text{cross}} = 6 + 0.9425 + \frac{0.36}{12}$$

$$L_{\text{cross}} = 6 + 0.9425 + 0.03 = 6.9725 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Difference from open belt:**

$$L_{\text{open}} = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D_1 + D_2) + \frac{(D_2 - D_1)^2}{4C}$$

$$L_{\text{open}} = 6 + 0.9425 + \frac{(0.4 - 0.2)^2}{12} = 6 + 0.9425 + \frac{0.04}{12}$$

$$L_{\text{open}} = 6 + 0.9425 + 0.00333 = 6.9458 \text{ m}$$

$$\Delta L = L_{\text{cross}} - L_{\text{open}} = 6.9725 - 6.9458 = 0.0267 \text{ m} = 26.7 \text{ mm}$$

**Verification using shortcut:** $\Delta L = D_1 D_2 / C = (0.2)(0.4) / 3 = 0.08/3 = 0.02667 \text{ m}$ ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Brakes and Dynamometers

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** A **simple band brake** is applied to a drum of diameter 600 mm rotating at 200 rpm. The band has a coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.3$ with the drum, and the wrap angle is $270°$. The maximum tension in the band is limited to 5 kN.
1. Find the braking torque developed.
2. Find the power absorbed by the brake.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A **double block brake** has each block pivoted at a horizontal distance $a = 150 \text{ mm}$ from the center of the drum, and the line of action of the actuating force is at a vertical distance $h = 200 \text{ mm}$ above the pivot. The drum diameter is 500 mm, the coefficient of friction is $\mu = 0.35$, and the actuating force per block is 600 N. The drum rotates at 240 rpm.
1. Determine the braking torque.
2. Determine the braking efficiency and comment on whether the brake is self-energizing.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given:**
- $D = 600 \text{ mm}$, so $R = 0.3 \text{ m}$
- $N = 200 \text{ rpm}$
- $\mu = 0.3$, $\theta = 270° = 270 \times \pi / 180 = 4.7124 \text{ rad}$
- $T_1 = 5 \text{ kN} = 5000 \text{ N}$ (maximum tension)

**Step 1 — Find $T_2$ using Euler's belt friction equation:**

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta} = e^{0.3 \times 4.7124} = e^{1.4137} = 4.1108$$

$$T_2 = \frac{T_1}{4.1108} = \frac{5000}{4.1108} = 1216.33 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Braking torque:**

$$M_t = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot R = (5000 - 1216.33) \times 0.3 = 3783.67 \times 0.3 = 1135.10 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Power absorbed:**

$$P = \frac{2 \pi N M_t}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 200 \times 1135.10}{60}$$

$$P = \frac{1,426,371}{60} = 23,772.85 \text{ W} \approx 23.77 \text{ kW}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Drum diameter $D = 500 \text{ mm}$, so $R = 0.25 \text{ m}$
- $a = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$, $h = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$
- $\mu = 0.35$, actuating force per block $F = 600 \text{ N}$
- $N = 240 \text{ rpm}$

**Step 1 — Find normal force on the block:**

Taking moments of the lever about the pivot. For a single pivoted block, when the drum rotates such that the friction force $\mu N$ acts **towards the pivot** in the horizontal direction, the moment equation about the pivot is:

$$F \cdot h = N \cdot a + \mu N \cdot h$$

Wait — careful with sign. If the friction force on the block acts **horizontally towards the pivot** (self-energizing direction), the moment equation is:

$$F \cdot h = N \cdot a - \mu N \cdot h$$

If the friction force acts **away from the pivot** (self-de-energizing direction), then:

$$F \cdot h = N \cdot a + \mu N \cdot h$$

Using the **self-de-energizing** (more conservative) case:

$$F h = N a + \mu N h \implies F h = N(a + \mu h)$$

$$N = \frac{F \cdot h}{a + \mu h} = \frac{600 \times 0.2}{0.15 + 0.35 \times 0.2} = \frac{120}{0.15 + 0.07} = \frac{120}{0.22} = 545.45 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Braking torque per block:**

$$M_{t,\text{block}} = \mu N R = 0.35 \times 545.45 \times 0.25 = 47.73 \text{ N·m}$$

**For double block, both blocks contribute equally:**

$$M_t = 2 \times 47.73 = 95.45 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Braking efficiency:**

$$\eta_b = \frac{\mu (h + \mu a)}{h - \mu a} = \frac{0.35 (0.2 + 0.35 \times 0.15)}{0.2 - 0.35 \times 0.15}$$

$$\eta_b = \frac{0.35 \times (0.2 + 0.0525)}{0.2 - 0.0525} = \frac{0.35 \times 0.2525}{0.1475} = \frac{0.08838}{0.1475} = 0.5992$$

**Step 4 — Power absorbed:**

$$P = \frac{2 \pi N M_t}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 240 \times 95.45}{60} = \frac{143,996.6}{60} = 2399.94 \text{ W} \approx 2.4 \text{ kW}$$

**Comment on self-energizing:** Since $\eta_b = 0.5992 < 1$, the brake is **self-de-energizing**. The rotation of the drum works **against** the braking action, requiring more actuating force than would be needed if the drum were stationary.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Brake Questions):**
> 1. **Sign convention in the moment equation** — Students commonly lose 2-3 marks by mis-stating whether the friction force helps or opposes the applied force. Always draw the FBD.
> 2. **Brake efficiency formula** — Some students incorrectly write the numerator and denominator. Memorize: $\eta_b = \mu(h + \mu a) / (h - \mu a)$.
> 3. **Double block — factor of 2** — Don't forget that a double block brake has **twice** the friction surface area (or contributes two equal braking torques).
> 4. **Units consistency** — Keep $R$, $a$, $h$ all in metres when computing force/torque in SI.
> 5. **Wrap angle in radians** — Euler's belt friction equation ($T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu\theta}$) requires $\theta$ in **radians**, not degrees.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Belt Drive Questions):**
> 1. **Open vs. crossed $\sin \alpha$ sign** — The difference $(D_2 - D_1)$ for open, but $(D_1 + D_2)$ for crossed. Mixing these up costs full marks.
> 2. **Wrap angle difference** — Open belt has **unequal** wrap angles on the two pulleys; crossed belt has **equal** wrap angles.
> 3. **Length formula** — Use the one that matches the configuration.
> 4. **Centrifugal tension** — Often ignored in basic problems; mention it explicitly for high-speed drives.

---

## 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for the night before the exam:**

### Belt Drive Essentials
- [ ] **Open belt**: same direction rotation, unequal wrap angles ($\pi \pm 2\alpha$), length formula with $(D_2 - D_1)^2$ term.
- [ ] **Crossed belt**: opposite direction rotation, equal wrap angles ($\pi + 2\alpha$ on both), length formula with $(D_1 + D_2)^2$ term.
- [ ] **$\sin \alpha$**: $(D_2 - D_1)/2C$ for open, $(D_1 + D_2)/2C$ for crossed.
- [ ] **Velocity ratio** $i = D_2 / D_1 = N_1 / N_2$ (no-slip assumption).
- [ ] **Belt velocity** $v = \pi D N / 60$ (m/s).
- [ ] **Euler's equation** $T_1 / T_2 = e^{\mu\theta}$ — always use $\theta$ in **radians**.
- [ ] **Centrifugal tension** $T_c = w v^2 / g$ — added to both tight and slack side tensions in high-speed belts.
- [ ] **Maximum power** condition: $v_{\max} = \sqrt{T g / (3w)}$.
- [ ] **No slip condition**: $T_1 / T_2 \le e^{\mu \theta}$.

### Brake Essentials
- [ ] **Simple band brake** = flexible band + drum. Uses Euler's belt friction principle.
- [ ] **Block brake** = rigid block(s) with friction lining. Self-energizing depends on rotation direction.
- [ ] **Disk brake** = disk with friction pads on both faces. Used in modern automobiles and high-speed applications.
- [ ] **Braking efficiency** $\eta_b > 1$ → self-energizing; $\eta_b < 1$ → self-de-energizing.
- [ ] **Braking torque** is always $M_t = (\text{friction force}) \times R$ (or $R_{\text{mean}}$ for disk).
- [ ] **Heat dissipation** is the limiting factor for brake power capacity.

### Dynamometer Essentials
- [ ] **Absorption dynamometer** — dissipates energy as heat (rope brake, Prony, hydraulic).
- [ ] **Transmission dynamometer** — measures without absorbing net energy (epicyclic, torsion).
- [ ] **Power formula** $P = 2 \pi N M_t / 60$ applies universally.
- [ ] **Rope brake dynamometer** — simplest, uses $(W - S) R$ for torque.

### Numerical Answer Quick-Reference (from Question A above)
- [ ] Open belt angles: $\theta_{\text{large}} = 182.15°$, $\theta_{\text{small}} = 177.85°$.
- [ ] Open belt length: $L \approx 8.71 \text{ m}$.
- [ ] Crossed belt angles: $\theta_1 = \theta_2 \approx 191.48°$.
- [ ] Crossed belt length: $L \approx 6.97 \text{ m}$.

### Numerical Answer Quick-Reference (from Question B above)
- [ ] Band brake torque: $M_t \approx 1135 \text{ N·m}$.
- [ ] Band brake power: $P \approx 23.77 \text{ kW}$.
- [ ] Block brake torque: $M_t \approx 95.45 \text{ N·m}$.
- [ ] Block brake efficiency: $\eta_b \approx 0.6$ (self-de-energizing).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute exam tip:** If you forget the wrap angle formulas in the exam, derive them in 30 seconds from the geometry: the straight portions of the belt are inclined at angle $\alpha$ to the line of centers; the wrap angle on each pulley differs from $\pi$ by $\pm 2\alpha$ depending on whether the pulley is the "near" or "far" side. The exact sign convention comes from drawing a quick sketch.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute tip on brakes:** When a brake problem gives you a "drum rotating in a specific direction," always draw the FBD with the friction force pointing in the **opposite** direction of rotation at the contact surface. The lever moment equation will then naturally incorporate the correct sign.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rope Brake Dynamometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET404 - THEORY OF MACHINES AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Rope Brake Dynamometer**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Rope Brake Dynamometer** is a mechanical, **absorption-type dynamometer** used to measure the **brake power (B.P.)** of an engine or motor. It operates on the principle of **friction** between a rope (or band) wrapped around a rotating pulley or flywheel and the weights applied at the ends of the rope. The frictional resistance (brake torque) opposes the rotation, and the **net effective load** is measured by the difference in tensions on the tight and slack sides of the rope.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Rope Brake Dynamometer is classified as an **Absorption Dynamometer** — the energy absorbed by friction is ultimately dissipated as **heat** to the surroundings, which is why such devices are limited to low-to-medium speed engines.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you are riding a bicycle and you squeeze the hand brake. The brake pad rubs against the rim of the wheel, creating friction. This friction opposes the rotation of the wheel, slowing you down. The energy of motion is converted into heat (you can feel the rim getting warm after a long descent).

Now, scale this up: instead of a brake pad and a bicycle wheel, imagine a **thick cotton rope** wrapped around a **spinning flywheel** of an engine, with a **weight (W)** hanging on one side and a **smaller weight (S)** hanging on the other side, both over pulleys. The rope grips the flywheel due to friction. The engine has to "fight" this friction to keep rotating. The bigger the difference (W − S), the more power the engine is delivering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Connection:** Rope brake dynamometers are commonly used in **engine test beds** of small IC engines, **pump test rigs**, and **motor testing laboratories** in Kerala engineering colleges to measure the brake power output before the engine is certified for sale.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities (SI Units)

- **Brake Torque (T_b)** → Newton-metre (**N·m**)
- **Brake Power (B.P.)** → Watts (**W**) or **kW**
- **Coefficient of Friction (μ)** → dimensionless
- **Angle of wrap (θ)** → radians (**rad**)
- **Angular velocity (ω)** → rad/s ; **Speed (N)** → RPM
- **Diameter of drum (D)** or Radius (R) → metres (**m**)
- **Dead weights (W, S)** → Newtons (**N**)
- **Acceleration due to gravity (g)** → **9.81 m/s²**

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Convention in KTU Problems:** Weights are always expressed as *forces in Newtons*. If a problem says "a 10 kg weight", we convert it as **W = m × g = 10 × 9.81 = 98.1 N**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. The engine/motor under test is coupled to a **flywheel or brake drum** of known radius **R**.
2. A flexible rope (cotton, hemp, or sometimes a friction lining) is wrapped **partially or fully** around the drum.
3. One end of the rope carries a **tight-side weight (W)** and the other end carries a **slack-side weight (S)**, each passing over guide pulleys.
4. As the drum rotates (say, anti-clockwise), friction drags the rope along, increasing tension on one side (**T₁ = W**, tight side) and reducing it on the other (**T₂ = S**, slack side).
5. The **net frictional force** acting tangentially on the drum is **(W − S)**, which produces a **braking torque** opposing rotation.
6. The engine must supply this resisting torque to maintain steady rotation; this is the **brake torque**.
7. **Brake Power** is computed as: Brake Torque × Angular Velocity.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why a difference in weights?** — If W = S, the rope would float with zero net torque — no useful measurement. The difference creates the **useful braking effect**.
- **Why the rope is flexible?** — It conforms to the drum, maximising contact area and ensuring uniform frictional grip.
- **Why is heat generated?** — Friction converts mechanical energy into thermal energy, which must be dissipated (often with water cooling in large dynamometers).

## 2.3 Theoretical Relationship: Tension Ratio and Friction

For a rope wrapped over an angular contact **θ** (in radians) around a drum with coefficient of friction **μ**, the **belt-friction equation** (Euler-Eytelwein) applies:

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$$

where:
- **T₁** = tension on the **tight side** (N)
- **T₂** = tension on the **slack side** (N)
- **μ** = coefficient of friction between rope and drum
- **θ** = angle of wrap (radians) = (wrap fraction) × **2π**

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Net Effective Load (F) | $F = W - S$ | N | W = tight side load, S = slack side load |
| 2 | Brake Torque | $T_b = (W - S) \cdot R = F \cdot R$ | N·m | R = drum radius |
| 3 | Brake Torque (in terms of T₁, T₂) | $T_b = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot R$ | N·m | Direct from friction |
| 4 | Belt-Friction Equation | $T_1 = T_2 \cdot e^{\mu \theta}$ | N | Euler's belt-friction relation |
| 5 | Tension Ratio | $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$ | dimensionless | Valid for impending slip |
| 6 | Angular Velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | rad/s | N = speed in RPM |
| 7 | Brake Power (in Watts) | $P = T_b \cdot \omega = \dfrac{2\pi N T_b}{60}$ | W | SI unit |
| 8 | Brake Power (in kW) | $P = \dfrac{2\pi N (W-S) R}{60 \times 1000}$ | kW | Convenient for KTU problems |
| 9 | Angle of wrap (full wrap) | $\theta = 2\pi$ rad = 360° | rad | One complete turn |
| 10 | Half wrap | $\theta = \pi$ rad = 180° | rad | Rope goes halfway around |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule for KTU Board Exams:** When the problem says "weight" in kg, **always multiply by g = 9.81 m/s²** to convert to Newtons. Forgetting this is the single most common reason students lose 2–3 marks.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Engine test beds:** Measure shaft power of small petrol/diesel engines.
- **Motor testing:** Determine output power of electric motors before installation.
- **Pump and compressor testing:** Quantify mechanical power input.
- **Educational labs:** Mandatory experiment in the **Fluid Mechanics and Machinery Lab** of KTU Mechanical Engineering curriculum.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Brake Torque Equation

Consider a rope brake dynamometer with a drum of radius **R** rotating at **N** RPM. A rope is wrapped around the drum with tension **T₁ = W** on the tight side and **T₂ = S** on the slack side. Both tensions act **tangentially** to the drum in opposite directions.

**Step 1: Identify the resisting forces.**

The tangential forces on the drum are:
- $T_1 = W$ acting in the direction of rotation (trying to drag the drum with it).
- $T_2 = S$ acting opposite to the direction of rotation (resisting the drum).

**Step 2: Net tangential force on the drum.**

$$F_{net} = T_1 - T_2 = W - S$$

**Step 3: Convert net force to torque.**

Torque is force multiplied by the perpendicular distance (radius) from the axis of rotation:

$$T_b = F_{net} \times R = (W - S) \cdot R$$

**Step 4: Express in terms of T₁ and T₂.**

$$T_b = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot R$$

This is the **fundamental brake torque equation** of a rope brake dynamometer.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Brake Power Equation

**Step 1: Relate angular velocity to RPM.**

For a shaft rotating at **N** revolutions per minute, the angular displacement in one minute is **2πN** radians. Therefore, angular velocity in rad/s is:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

**Step 2: Apply the basic power equation.**

Power is torque times angular velocity:

$$P = T_b \cdot \omega$$

**Step 3: Substitute the expressions for T_b and ω.**

$$P = (W - S) \cdot R \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

**Step 4: Simplify and express in kW (typical KTU convention).**

$$P = \frac{2 \pi N (W - S) R}{60 \times 1000} \;\; \text{kW}$$

This is the **standard KTU board-exam formula** for brake power using a rope brake dynamometer.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Belt-Friction Equation (Euler's Relation)

Consider an elemental arc of the rope subtending angle **dθ** at the drum centre. Let **T** be the tension on one side and **(T + dT)** on the other side after slipping is impending. Let the normal reaction on the elemental arc be **dN**.

**Step 1: Equilibrium of forces in the normal direction.**

The two tensions resolve into a normal component. For small dθ, the resultant normal force is:

$$dN = T \cdot \sin\left(\frac{d\theta}{2}\right) + (T + dT) \cdot \sin\left(\frac{d\theta}{2}\right)$$

For small angles, $\sin(d\theta/2) \approx d\theta/2$, so:

$$dN = 2T \cdot \frac{d\theta}{2} = T \cdot d\theta$$

(neglecting the second-order term $dT \cdot d\theta$).

**Step 2: Equilibrium in the tangential direction.**

The frictional force balances the difference in tensions:

$$\mu \cdot dN = dT$$

**Step 3: Substitute dN.**

$$\mu \cdot T \cdot d\theta = dT$$

**Step 4: Separate variables and integrate.**

$$\frac{dT}{T} = \mu \cdot d\theta$$

Integrating from **T₂** (slack) to **T₁** (tight) and from **0** to **θ**:

$$\int_{T_2}^{T_1} \frac{dT}{T} = \mu \int_{0}^{\theta} d\theta$$

$$[\ln T]_{T_2}^{T_1} = \mu \theta$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right) = \mu \theta$$

**Step 5: Exponentiate to obtain Euler's belt-friction formula.**

$$\boxed{\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}}$$

This is the famous **Euler-Eytelwein belt-friction equation**, which is the basis of all rope and band brake analyses.

---

## 3.4 KTU Standard Numerical Solved Example (Model Answer Style)

**Problem:** A rope brake dynamometer is used to test a 4-stroke petrol engine. The brake drum has a diameter of **600 mm** and rotates at **500 RPM**. The tight-side weight is **400 N** and the slack-side weight is **50 N**. The rope makes 1.5 turns around the drum. Calculate:
1. The brake torque.
2. The brake power in kW.
3. The coefficient of friction, given the angle of wrap.

**Given Data:**
- Diameter D = 600 mm → R = 0.3 m
- N = 500 RPM
- W = T₁ = 400 N
- S = T₂ = 50 N
- Number of turns = 1.5 → θ = 1.5 × 2π = 3π rad

**Step 1: Calculate Brake Torque.**

$$T_b = (W - S) \cdot R = (400 - 50) \times 0.3 = 350 \times 0.3 = 105 \; \text{N·m}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Angular Velocity.**

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi \times 500}{60} = \frac{1000 \pi}{60} = 52.36 \; \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Brake Power.**

$$P = T_b \cdot \omega = 105 \times 52.36 = 5497.8 \; \text{W} = 5.498 \; \text{kW}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Coefficient of Friction.**

Using the belt-friction equation:

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$$

$$\frac{400}{50} = 8 = e^{\mu \times 3\pi}$$

Taking natural log on both sides:

$$\ln(8) = 3\pi \mu$$

$$2.0794 = 9.4248 \cdot \mu$$

$$\mu = \frac{2.0794}{9.4248} = 0.2206 \approx 0.221$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Note:** Always show units in **every** step. Always state the formula before substitution. Always box the final answer. These three habits alone can boost your score by 5–8 marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Operating Architecture of a Rope Brake Dynamometer

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of a rope brake dynamometer system, showing how mechanical energy flows from the prime mover to the brake assembly, and how the load measurement is achieved.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine or Motor under Test] -->|Shaft Power Input| B[Coupling]
    B --> C[Brake Drum / Flywheel of Radius R]
    C -->|Rotates with Speed N rpm| D[Flexible Rope Wrapped over Drum]
    D --> E[Friction Contact over Angle theta]
    E --> F[Tight Side Tension T1 = W]
    E --> G[Slack Side Tension T2 = S]
    F --> H[Guide Pulley 1]
    G --> I[Guide Pulley 2]
    H --> J[Dead Weight W on Tight Side]
    I --> K[Dead Weight S on Slack Side]
    J --> L[Net Effective Load F = W minus S]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Brake Torque Tb = F x R]
    M --> N[Angular Velocity omega = 2 pi N / 60]
    M --> O[Brake Power P = Tb x omega]
    N --> O
    E --> P[Heat Dissipation to Surroundings]
    P --> Q[Energy Absorbed by Friction]
```

## 4.2 Free-Body Diagram of the Brake Drum (Force Analysis)

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **force analysis** on the brake drum, illustrating the direction of tensions and the resulting torque.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Brake Drum Center O] --> B[Radius R drawn to Contact Point]
    B --> C[Tight Side Tension T1 = W applied tangentially]
    B --> D[Slack Side Tension T2 = S applied tangentially opposite]
    C --> E[Frictional Torque Tb opposes rotation]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Net Braking Force Fnet = T1 minus T2]
    F --> G[Tb = Fnet x R = W minus S times R]
    G --> H[Drum rotates at angular velocity omega]
    H --> I[Power Absorbed P = Tb x omega]
```

## 4.3 Functional Signal Flow: From Load to Power Reading

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator adjusts Dead Weights W and S] --> B[Net Load F = W minus S established]
    B --> C[Engine reaches steady State at Speed N]
    C --> D[Tb measured as F x R]
    D --> E[omega calculated from Tachometer Reading]
    E --> F[Brake Power P = Tb x omega computed]
    F --> G[Result Displayed in kW on Test Rig]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Schematic Limitations:** The Mermaid diagrams above represent **functional block architectures** rather than exact physical drawings. In a real KTU lab, the actual setup includes a water-cooled drum (to dissipate heat) and a spring balance in place of one of the weights for finer measurement.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a rope brake dynamometer and state its working principle.**

**Model Answer:**

A **rope brake dynamometer** is an **absorption-type dynamometer** used to measure the **brake power** of an engine or motor. It works on the principle of **friction** between a rope wrapped around a rotating brake drum and the **dead weights** applied at both ends of the rope. The frictional resistance (brake torque) opposes the rotation of the drum, and the **net effective load** is the difference in tensions on the tight and slack sides of the rope. The brake power is calculated as the product of this net load, the drum radius, and the angular velocity of the shaft.

$$\text{B.P.} = \frac{2 \pi N (W - S) R}{60 \times 1000} \;\; \text{kW}$$

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the Euler belt-friction equation and explain the significance of the angle of wrap.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Euler belt-friction equation** relates the tensions on the tight and slack sides of a rope wrapped around a drum:

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$$

where **T₁** and **T₂** are the tight and slack side tensions, **μ** is the coefficient of friction, and **θ** is the angle of wrap in radians.

**Significance of the angle of wrap:** A larger angle of wrap increases the ratio T₁/T₂ exponentially, allowing a much greater braking effect for the same coefficient of friction. This is why designers use multiple turns of rope around the drum in rope brake dynamometers.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** A rope brake dynamometer is used to test a 4-stroke single-cylinder diesel engine. The effective diameter of the brake drum is **700 mm** and the engine speed is **450 RPM**. The weight on the tight side is **600 N** and that on the slack side is **80 N**. The rope makes **2 complete turns** around the drum. Determine:
1. The brake torque developed. (5 marks)
2. The brake power in kW. (4 marks)
3. The coefficient of friction between the rope and the drum. (5 marks)

**(b)** A **rope brake dynamometer** has a drum diameter of **500 mm** running at **600 RPM**. The coefficient of friction between the rope and drum is **0.25** and the rope makes **1.25 turns**. If the slack-side load is **40 N**, find the tight-side load and the brake power. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks Solution

**Given:**
- D = 700 mm → R = 0.35 m
- N = 450 RPM
- W = T₁ = 600 N
- S = T₂ = 80 N
- Turns = 2 → θ = 2 × 2π = 4π rad

**Step 1: Brake Torque (5 marks).**

$$T_b = (W - S) \cdot R = (600 - 80) \times 0.35 = 520 \times 0.35 = 182 \; \text{N·m}$$

**Step 2: Angular Velocity (intermediate).**

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi \times 450}{60} = 15 \pi = 47.124 \; \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3: Brake Power (4 marks).**

$$P = T_b \times \omega = 182 \times 47.124 = 8576.6 \; \text{W} = 8.577 \; \text{kW}$$

**Step 4: Coefficient of Friction (5 marks).**

Applying Euler's equation:

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta} \Rightarrow \frac{600}{80} = 7.5 = e^{\mu \times 4\pi}$$

$$\ln(7.5) = 4\pi \mu \Rightarrow 2.0149 = 12.566 \mu$$

$$\mu = \frac{2.0149}{12.566} = 0.1603 \approx 0.16$$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks Solution

**Given:**
- D = 500 mm → R = 0.25 m
- N = 600 RPM
- μ = 0.25
- Turns = 1.25 → θ = 1.25 × 2π = 2.5π rad
- T₂ = 40 N (slack side)

**Step 1: Find the Tight-Side Load T₁ (4 marks).**

$$T_1 = T_2 \cdot e^{\mu \theta} = 40 \times e^{0.25 \times 2.5 \pi} = 40 \times e^{1.9635}$$

$$T_1 = 40 \times 7.123 = 284.9 \; \text{N} \approx 285 \; \text{N}$$

**Step 2: Find the Brake Torque.**

$$T_b = (T_1 - T_2) \times R = (285 - 40) \times 0.25 = 245 \times 0.25 = 61.25 \; \text{N·m}$$

**Step 3: Find the Brake Power (3 marks).**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 600}{60} = 20\pi = 62.832 \; \text{rad/s}$$

$$P = T_b \times \omega = 61.25 \times 62.832 = 3848.4 \; \text{W} = 3.85 \; \text{kW}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a rope brake dynamometer**. Derive the expression for **brake power** developed. (7 marks)

**(b)** A **rope brake dynamometer** has the following test data: Drum diameter = **800 mm**, Speed = **400 RPM**, Tight-side weight = **500 N**, Slack-side weight = **60 N**, Coefficient of friction = **0.30**. Determine:
1. The angle of wrap if the rope makes 1.75 turns. (2 marks)
2. The brake torque and brake power. (3 marks)
3. Whether the rope will slip or hold, given the actual tension ratio observed is **7.8**. (2 marks)

---

### Model Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks Model Answer

**Construction:** A rope brake dynamometer consists of a **brake drum (or flywheel)** keyed to the engine shaft, a **flexible rope (cotton/hemp)** wound around the drum, **two guide pulleys**, and **two dead weights** suspended from the ends of the rope over the pulleys.

**Working:** As the engine rotates the drum, friction between the rope and drum creates a tension difference: **T₁ (tight) > T₂ (slack)**. The net tangential force **(T₁ − T₂)** produces a **brake torque** opposing rotation. The engine expends energy to overcome this torque, and the energy is dissipated as heat.

**Derivation of Brake Power:**

$$P = \frac{2 \pi N (T_1 - T_2) R}{60 \times 1000} \; \text{kW}$$

where **N** is in RPM, **T₁** and **T₂** in N, and **R** in m.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks Solution

**Given:**
- D = 800 mm → R = 0.4 m
- N = 400 RPM
- W = T₁ = 500 N
- S = T₂ = 60 N
- μ = 0.30
- Turns = 1.75

**Step 1: Angle of Wrap (2 marks).**

$$\theta = 1.75 \times 2\pi = 3.5 \pi = 10.996 \; \text{rad}$$

**Step 2: Brake Torque and Power (3 marks).**

$$T_b = (W - S) \cdot R = (500 - 60) \times 0.4 = 440 \times 0.4 = 176 \; \text{N·m}$$

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 400}{60} = \frac{800\pi}{60} = 41.888 \; \text{rad/s}$$

$$P = 176 \times 41.888 = 7372.3 \; \text{W} = 7.37 \; \text{kW}$$

**Step 3: Check for Slip (2 marks).**

Maximum (theoretical) tension ratio by Euler's formula:

$$\left(\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right)_{max} = e^{\mu \theta} = e^{0.30 \times 10.996} = e^{3.299} = 27.08$$

Observed tension ratio: 500/60 = 8.33.

Since 8.33 < 27.08, the rope **will not slip** — it is operating well within the safe frictional grip range. (If the observed ratio exceeded 27.08, the rope would slip.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the g-factor** — When mass is given in kg, students often write it directly as N. Always convert: **Force (N) = mass (kg) × 9.81 m/s²**.
> 2. **Confusing radian and degree for θ** — θ must always be in **radians** in the Euler equation. A common error: using 360° instead of **2π rad**, leading to a wrong μ.
> 3. **Units of R in metres** — Drum diameter is often given in mm. Always convert to **metres** before substituting.
> 4. **Power unit mismatch** — Mixing W and kW leads to wrong final answers. State the unit clearly.
> 5. **Sign convention** — The braking torque opposes rotation; do not add it to the driving torque.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Rope brake dynamometer is an **absorption-type** dynamometer that converts mechanical energy into **heat**.
- ✅ The fundamental equation is: $T_b = (W - S) \cdot R$ where W and S are tight and slack side loads in N.
- ✅ Brake power formula: $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N (W - S) R}{60 \times 1000}$ kW (most-used form in KTU problems).
- ✅ Euler's belt-friction equation: $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$, with **θ in radians**.
- ✅ A **larger angle of wrap** gives **exponentially higher** braking capacity for the same μ.
- ✅ **W (tight) > S (slack)** always; their difference is the net effective load.
- ✅ For multiple turns, $\theta = (\text{number of turns}) \times 2\pi$ rad.
- ✅ **g = 9.81 m/s²** must be used for kg-to-Newton conversion.
- ✅ **R must be in metres**; **N must be in RPM**; final power is usually required in **kW**.
- ✅ Always cross-check that the **observed tension ratio** is less than **$e^{\mu \theta}$** to confirm no slip occurs.
- ✅ Practical applications: **engine test beds, motor testing labs, pump test rigs, KTU undergraduate labs**.
- ✅ Limitations: heat dissipation, rope wear, not suitable for very high-speed engines.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET404 - THEORY OF MACHINES AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Behaviour of Materials - Elastic and Plastic, Concept of Stress and Strain, Hooke's Law (2D), Principal Stresses, Maximum Shear Stress, Bending and Torsional Stresses, Combining Stresses (2D), Mohr's Circle, Stress Concentration Factor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Behaviour of Materials: Elastic vs. Plastic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> The **mechanical behaviour of materials** under load is classified into two principal regimes based on the **recovery of deformation** upon unloading:
> 1. **Elastic Behaviour** — The material returns completely to its original dimensions once the applied load is removed. Deformation is **reversible** and governed by Hooke's Law.
> 2. **Plastic Behaviour** — The material undergoes **permanent (irreversible) deformation** after the load exceeds a critical threshold known as the **yield point**. The atomic bonds reorganise permanently.

### Intuitive Analogy

Imagine stretching a **rubber band** — pull it gently and let go, it snaps back (elastic). Pull it extremely hard, it stretches out of shape permanently (plastic). Engineering metals behave similarly, but the threshold (yield) is much sharper. A **spring** is engineered to stay in the elastic zone, while a **sheet-metal panel** after a car crash is in the plastic zone.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Yield Point Values (Standard Mild Steel, IS 2062):**
> - Proportional Limit ≈ **200 MPa**
> - Yield Stress, $\sigma_y$ ≈ **250 MPa**
> - Ultimate Tensile Strength ≈ **410 MPa**

---

## 1.2 Concept of Stress

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stress ($\sigma$ or $\tau$):** Internal resisting force per unit area developed inside a body when an external load is applied.
> - **Normal Stress ($\sigma$):** Acts perpendicular to the cross-section. Units: **N/m² (Pa)**, with engineering values typically in **MPa (10⁶ Pa)** or **GPa (10⁹ Pa)**.
> - **Shear Stress ($\tau$):** Acts tangentially (parallel) to the cross-section. Same units as normal stress.

### Intuitive Analogy

Think of stress as the **"crowd pressure"** at a concert gate. The force per square metre of gate area that the gate frame must resist. If the crowd pushes straight on (normal), the gate bends; if they push sideways (shear), the gate slides.

---

## 1.3 Concept of Strain

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strain ($\varepsilon$ or $\gamma$):** A **dimensionless** measure of deformation representing the change in dimension relative to the original dimension.
> - **Normal Strain ($\varepsilon$):** Change in length per unit original length.
> - **Shear Strain ($\gamma$):** Change in angle (in radians) between two originally perpendicular lines.

### Intuitive Analogy

If a 100 mm rod stretches by 0.5 mm, that's a strain of 0.5/100 = 0.005 (or 0.5%). Strain is always **unitless** because it's a ratio of like quantities.

---

## 1.4 Hooke's Law in 2D (Biaxial State)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hooke's Law (2D Generalised Form):** For a 2D plane stress or plane strain condition, the stress-strain relations are coupled and governed by **Poisson's ratio ($\nu$)** and the **Modulus of Elasticity ($E$)**.

The 2D Hooke's Law equations in terms of stress are:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left(\sigma_x - \nu\,\sigma_y\right)$$

$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left(\sigma_y - \nu\,\sigma_x\right)$$

And the **shear form**:

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G}$$

where $G$ is the **Shear Modulus (Modulus of Rigidity)**, related to $E$ and $\nu$ by:

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$

### Intuitive Analogy

A stretched rubber sheet in one direction also contracts in the perpendicular direction — this is the **Poisson effect**. If you stretch a rubber band lengthwise, it gets thinner. This coupling is exactly what the 2D Hooke's Law describes.

---

## 1.5 Principal Stresses

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principal Stresses ($\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$):** The **maximum and minimum normal stresses** acting on planes (called **principal planes**) where the **shear stress is zero**. These are the **eigenvalues** of the 2D stress tensor.

For a 2D stress element with $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, and $\tau_{xy}$:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

By KTU sign convention: $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2$ (algebraically).

### Intuitive Analogy

Imagine an oblique element of material inside a loaded body. As you rotate the element, the normal stress on its faces changes continuously. At two specific orientations, the shear vanishes and the normal stress is either maximum or minimum — these are the **principal directions**.

---

## 1.6 Maximum Shear Stress

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum Shear Stress ($\tau_{max}$):** The greatest tangential stress on any plane in the material, occurring on planes oriented at **45° to the principal planes**.

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

### Intuitive Analogy

A square block under pure tension $\sigma_1$ on its faces — if you slice it diagonally at 45°, the slice experiences pure shear of magnitude $\sigma_1/2$. This is why **ductile materials under tension often shear off at 45°** — that is the plane of maximum shear.

---

## 1.7 Bending Stress (Flexure Formula)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bending Stress:** The normal stress distribution across the cross-section of a beam subjected to a **bending moment $M$**.

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M \cdot y}{I} = \frac{M}{Z}$$

where:
- $M$ = Bending moment (N·m)
- $y$ = Distance from the neutral axis (m)
- $I$ = Second moment of area about the neutral axis (m⁴)
- $Z = I/y_{max}$ = **Section Modulus** (m³)

### Intuitive Analogy

When you bend a ruler over your finger, the top fibres stretch (tension) and the bottom fibres compress. The middle — the **neutral axis** — feels zero stress. The bending stress varies **linearly** from the neutral axis.

---

## 1.8 Torsional Shear Stress

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Torsional Shear Stress:** The shear stress in a shaft transmitting a torque $T$.

$$\tau = \frac{T \cdot r}{J} = \frac{T}{Z_p}$$

where:
- $T$ = Applied torque (N·m)
- $r$ = Radial distance from shaft axis (m)
- $J$ = Polar second moment of area (m⁴)
- $Z_p = J/r$ = **Polar Section Modulus** (m³)

For a **solid circular shaft** of diameter $d$:

$$J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}, \quad \tau_{max} = \frac{16\,T}{\pi d^3}$$

For a **hollow circular shaft** with outer dia $D$ and inner dia $d$:

$$J = \frac{\pi(D^4 - d^4)}{32}, \quad \tau_{max} = \frac{16\,T}{\pi\,D^3\left(1 - k^4\right)}, \quad k = d/D$$

### Intuitive Analogy

Twisting a screwdriver handle to drive a screw — the shaft experiences torsional shear. Maximum stress is at the **outer surface**, zero at the centre. This is why hollow shafts are efficient for torsion: the central core carries very little stress.

---

## 1.9 Combined / Compound Stresses (2D)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Combined Stress State:** When a machine element is simultaneously subjected to **bending, torsion, axial load, and/or shear**, the stress element at a critical point has both normal stresses ($\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$) and shear stress ($\tau_{xy}$). The **principal stresses** are then calculated from this combined state, and design checks (e.g., **distortion energy theory** or **maximum shear stress theory**) are applied.

For a shaft carrying both bending moment $M$ and torque $T$, at any point on the surface:
- Bending produces normal stress: $\sigma_x = 32M/(\pi d^3)$
- Torsion produces shear stress: $\tau_{xy} = 16T/(\pi d^3)$

The **principal stresses** are then computed using the formula in §1.5.

### Intuitive Analogy

A car's **drive shaft** is the perfect example: it transmits torque (torsion) AND bends due to the rear axle moving up and down with the road. Both stresses combine at the surface, and engineers must check the **combined** peak stress, not just one at a time.

---

## 1.10 Mohr's Circle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mohr's Circle (2D):** A **graphical representation** of the transformation of normal and shear stresses on a rotated element. The circle is plotted in the $\sigma$-$\tau$ plane with:
> - **Centre** at $C = \left(\frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2},\, 0\right)$
> - **Radius** $R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$
> - **$\sigma_1, \sigma_2$** are the extreme horizontal intercepts.
> - **$\tau_{max}$** is the topmost / bottommost point on the circle.

### Intuitive Analogy

Mohr's Circle is a "stress compass" — instead of doing trigonometry every time, you plot the known stress state and read off the principal stresses, maximum shear, and principal direction angles **geometrically**.

---

## 1.11 Stress Concentration Factor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stress Concentration Factor ($K_t$):** A dimensionless factor that quantifies the **amplification of nominal stress** at geometric discontinuities such as holes, keyways, fillets, notches, and abrupt changes in cross-section.

$$K_t = \frac{\sigma_{max}}{\sigma_{nom}}$$

where:
- $\sigma_{max}$ = Peak stress at the discontinuity
- $\sigma_{nom}$ = Nominal (average) stress in the net section away from the discontinuity

Values of $K_t$ are obtained from **standard charts (Peterson's, Roark's Formulas for Stress and Strain)** based on geometry and loading type.

### Intuitive Analogy

A paper with a small tear is much easier to tear further because the stress "piles up" at the tip of the tear. Similarly, a sharp corner in a machine part becomes a stress hotspot. This is why good design uses **generous fillets** at corners.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Stress-Strain Curve — Behavioural Zones

When a ductile material (e.g., mild steel) is loaded in tension, the engineering stress-strain curve passes through:

1. **Elastic Region** — Linear, governed by Hooke's Law: $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$
2. **Proportional Limit** — End of linear region.
3. **Elastic Limit** — End of recoverable region.
4. **Yield Point ($\sigma_y$)** — Onset of plastic deformation.
5. **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS, $\sigma_u$)** — Maximum stress the material can carry.
6. **Fracture Point** — Final separation.

For a perfectly **elastic-plastic** (idealised) material, the stress-strain relation is bilinear: elastic slope $E$ up to $\sigma_y$, then perfectly plastic at constant $\sigma_y$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Strain Energy Concepts:**
> - **Modulus of Resilience** = Area under the elastic portion of the curve = $\sigma_y^2 / (2E)$.
> - **Modulus of Toughness** = Total area under the curve up to fracture.

---

## 2.2 Hooke's Law in 2D — Physical Reasoning

In 1D, Hooke's Law states $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$. In 2D, however, the strain in one direction is influenced by the stress in the perpendicular direction through **Poisson's ratio $\nu$**. This coupling makes 2D analysis essential for:

- Pressure vessel walls (hoop and axial stresses).
- Thin plates under biaxial load.
- Rotating discs (flywheels, turbine rotors).

The **2D stress-strain relations (plane stress)** assume $\sigma_z = 0$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_x \\ \varepsilon_y \\ \gamma_{xy} \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{E}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -\nu & 0 \\ -\nu & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 2(1+\nu) \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x \\ \sigma_y \\ \tau_{xy} \end{bmatrix}$$

---

## 2.3 Derivation Strategy for Principal Stresses

Consider a 2D stress element. Resolving forces on a face inclined at angle $\theta$ to the x-axis (positive counter-clockwise) gives the **transformation equations**:

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$$

$$\tau_\theta = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta$$

Setting $\dfrac{d\sigma_\theta}{d\theta} = 0$ yields the **principal angle** $\theta_p$:

$$\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

Substituting back gives the principal stress values in §1.5.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **Cheat Sheet** consolidates every formula you will need for Module-3 problems in the KTU 2024 ESE. Memorise the units and applicability.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Normal Stress | $\sigma = P/A$ | Pa, MPa |
| 2 | Shear Stress | $\tau = V/A$ | Pa, MPa |
| 3 | Normal Strain | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Shear Strain | $\gamma = \Delta x / L$ (rad) | Dimensionless, radians |
| 5 | Hooke's Law (1D) | $\sigma = E\,\varepsilon$ | $E$ in Pa |
| 6 | Hooke's Law (2D) | $\varepsilon_x = (\sigma_x - \nu\sigma_y)/E$ | Plane stress |
| 7 | Poisson's Ratio Relation | $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$ | $-1 \le \nu \le 0.5$ |
| 8 | Principal Stress | $\sigma_{1,2} = (\sigma_x+\sigma_y)/2 \pm \sqrt{[(\sigma_x-\sigma_y)/2]^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | $\sigma_1 \ge \sigma_2$ |
| 9 | Principal Angle | $\tan 2\theta_p = 2\tau_{xy}/(\sigma_x-\sigma_y)$ | Counter-clockwise positive |
| 10 | Maximum Shear Stress | $\tau_{max} = (\sigma_1-\sigma_2)/2$ | At 45° to principal plane |
| 11 | Bending Stress | $\sigma_b = M y / I = M / Z$ | $Z = I/y_{max}$ (m³) |
| 12 | Torsional Shear Stress | $\tau = T r / J = T / Z_p$ | Solid: $J = \pi d^4/32$ |
| 13 | Hollow Shaft Torsion | $\tau_{max} = 16T/[\pi D^3(1-k^4)]$, $k=d/D$ | $D$=outer, $d$=inner dia |
| 14 | Transformation Equations | $\sigma_\theta$, $\tau_\theta$ (see §2.3) | Valid for any $\theta$ |
| 15 | Mohr's Circle Centre | $C = [(\sigma_x+\sigma_y)/2,\, 0]$ | On $\sigma$-axis |
| 16 | Mohr's Circle Radius | $R = \sqrt{[(\sigma_x-\sigma_y)/2]^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Always positive |
| 17 | Stress Concentration | $K_t = \sigma_{max}/\sigma_{nom}$ | Read from charts |
| 18 | Effective Stress (DE) | $\sigma_{e} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2^2}$ | Distortion energy |
| 19 | Effective Stress (MSS) | $\sigma_{e} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_2$ | Max shear stress theory |
| 20 | Bulk Modulus | $K = E/[3(1-2\nu)]$ | Hydrostatic loading |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall (KTU Examiner's Trap):** When using the principal angle formula, $\tan 2\theta_p = 2\tau_{xy}/(\sigma_x-\sigma_y)$, the **angle $2\theta_p$ is doubled**. Always halve it before plotting Mohr's circle. Also, $\theta_p$ and $\theta_p + 90°$ give the two principal directions.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Stress State Concerned | Why It Matters |
|-------------|------------------------|----------------|
| Pressure Vessels | Biaxial ($\sigma_h, \sigma_a$) | Hoop stress = 2× axial; controls wall thickness |
| Crankshafts | Combined bending + torsion | Fillet $K_t$ causes fatigue failure origin |
| Welded Joints | Residual + applied stress | Brittle fracture risk |
| Gears | Hertzian contact + bending | Tooth root fillet $K_t$ critical |
| Bolted Connections | Tension + shear (combined) | Shear-tension interaction |
| Flywheels | Rotating disc (biaxial) | Burst speed governed by hoop stress |
| Turbine Blades | Centrifugal + bending + torsion | Combined state requires Mohr's circle |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Principal Stresses (2D)

### Step 1 — Element Setup

Consider a 2D stress element with stresses $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$ (normal) and $\tau_{xy}$ (shear). The shear stresses on perpendicular faces are **equal in magnitude and opposite in sign** (complementary shear principle), i.e., $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$.

### Step 2 — Resolve on an Inclined Plane

Cut a wedge from the element along a plane making angle $\theta$ with the $x$-axis. The wedge has:
- Inclined face area: $dA$
- Horizontal face area: $dA \cos\theta$
- Vertical face area: $dA \sin\theta$

### Step 3 — Equilibrium of the Wedge

Summing forces **perpendicular** to the inclined face (normal direction):

$$\sigma_\theta \, dA = \sigma_x \, dA \cos\theta \cdot \cos\theta + \sigma_y \, dA \sin\theta \cdot \sin\theta + 2\tau_{xy} \, dA \sin\theta \cos\theta$$

The factor 2 on $\tau_{xy}$ arises because **both** shear stresses contribute normal force components on the inclined face.

Dividing by $dA$ and using the identities $\cos^2\theta = (1+\cos 2\theta)/2$, $\sin^2\theta = (1-\cos 2\theta)/2$, and $2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin 2\theta$:

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta \qquad \text{(1)}$$

### Step 4 — Equilibrium in Shear Direction

Summing forces along the inclined face:

$$\tau_\theta \, dA = -\sigma_x \, dA \cos\theta \cdot \sin\theta + \sigma_y \, dA \sin\theta \cdot \cos\theta + \tau_{xy}\,dA(\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta)$$

Using double-angle identities, the simplified form is:

$$\tau_\theta = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta \qquad \text{(2)}$$

### Step 5 — Maximise $\sigma_\theta$

To find the **principal stress**, set $\dfrac{d\sigma_\theta}{d\theta} = 0$:

$$\frac{d\sigma_\theta}{d\theta} = -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\sin 2\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} \qquad \text{(3)}$$

### Step 6 — Substitute Back for $\sigma_1, \sigma_2$

Using $\sin 2\theta_p$ and $\cos 2\theta_p$ from Equation (3), substitute into Equation (1). The standard result is:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} \qquad \text{(4)}$$

### Step 7 — Maximum Shear Stress

From the Mohr's circle geometry, the maximum shear stress equals the radius:

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} \qquad \text{(5)}$$

The orientation of $\tau_{max}$ is $\theta_s = \theta_p \pm 45°$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Bending Stress Formula

### Step 1 — Assumptions (Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory)

1. Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis after bending.
2. The material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law.
3. The radius of curvature is large compared with beam depth.

### Step 2 — Strain Variation

A fibre at distance $y$ from the neutral axis undergoes a longitudinal strain:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{(\rho + y)\,d\theta - \rho\,d\theta}{\rho\,d\theta} = \frac{y}{\rho}$$

where $\rho$ is the radius of curvature. Strain is **linear** in $y$.

### Step 3 — Stress from Hooke's Law

$$\sigma = E\,\varepsilon = \frac{E\,y}{\rho}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Moment Equilibrium

The bending moment $M$ equals the integral of moment of stress over the area:

$$M = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA = \int_A \frac{E\,y}{\rho}\cdot y\,dA = \frac{E}{\rho}\int_A y^2\,dA$$

Define $I = \int_A y^2\,dA$ (second moment of area). Thus:

$$\frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{M}{E\,I} \qquad \text{(6)}$$

### Step 5 — Combine

Substituting Equation (6) into the stress equation:

$$\sigma = \frac{E\,y}{\rho} = \frac{M\,y}{I} \qquad \text{(7)}$$

The **section modulus** $Z = I / y_{max}$ gives $\sigma_{max} = M / Z$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Torsional Shear Stress (Solid Circular Shaft)

### Step 1 — Assumption (St. Venant's Theory)

For a circular shaft, a cross-section rotates as a rigid disc. A radial line on the end face remains straight, and the cross-section remains plane and undistorted. Twisting a length $L$ by angle $\phi$ causes a fibre at radius $r$ to shear by:

$$\gamma = \frac{r\,\phi}{L}$$

### Step 2 — Stress from Hooke's Law (Shear)

$$\tau = G\,\gamma = \frac{G\,r\,\phi}{L}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Torque Equilibrium

The torque $T$ is the moment of the shear stress distribution:

$$T = \int_A \tau \cdot r\,dA = \int_A \frac{G\,r\,\phi}{L}\cdot r\,dA = \frac{G\,\phi}{L}\int_A r^2\,dA$$

Define $J = \int_A r^2\,dA$ (polar second moment of area). For a solid circle of diameter $d$:

$$J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}$$

### Step 4 — Final Torsion Formula

$$\tau = \frac{T\,r}{J}, \quad \tau_{max} = \frac{T\,r_{max}}{J} = \frac{16\,T}{\pi\,d^3} \qquad \text{(8)}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Mohr's Circle Equations

Starting from Equations (1) and (2), introduce the **center** and **radius** parameters:

$$C = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}, \quad R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

Equation (1) becomes:

$$\sigma_\theta = C + R\cos(2\theta - 2\alpha)$$

where $\tan 2\alpha = 2\tau_{xy}/(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)$, with $\alpha = \theta_p$ being the principal angle.

Equation (2) becomes:

$$\tau_\theta = R\sin(2\theta - 2\alpha)$$

These are **parametric equations** of a circle in $(\sigma_\theta, \tau_\theta)$ with centre $(C, 0)$ and radius $R$. Hence **Mohr's Circle** is the locus of all possible $(\sigma_\theta, \tau_\theta)$ pairs as $\theta$ varies.

**Key points on Mohr's Circle:**
- **Point X**: $(\sigma_x, \tau_{xy})$
- **Point Y**: $(\sigma_y, -\tau_{xy})$
- **Point P1**: $(\sigma_1, 0)$
- **Point P2**: $(\sigma_2, 0)$
- **Point S**: top of circle = $(\sigma_x+\sigma_y)/2, R)$ — gives $\tau_{max}$

**Angle Convention:** Angles on Mohr's circle are **$2\theta$**, i.e., doubled from physical angles. **Clockwise rotation on the element** is **counter-clockwise on Mohr's circle** by the same physical angle, but **doubled**.

---

## 3.5 Combined Bending + Torsion on a Shaft (Step-by-Step)

For a circular shaft of diameter $d$ under simultaneous **bending moment $M$** and **torque $T$**:

1. **Bending normal stress at the extreme fibre (surface):**
$$\sigma_x = \frac{32 M}{\pi d^3}$$

2. **Torsional shear stress at the surface:**
$$\tau_{xy} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$$

3. **The other normal stress on the surface is zero** (no axial load): $\sigma_y = 0$.

4. **Principal stresses:**
$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

5. **Maximum shear stress:**
$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

6. **Equivalent moment concept** (very useful for KTU problems):
$$M_e = \frac{1}{2}\left(M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}\right) \quad \text{(DE Theory)}$$

$$T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} \quad \text{(Equivalent Twisting Moment)}$$

The shaft is then designed as if subjected to a bending moment $M_e$ alone, or equivalently to a torque $T_e$ alone.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A steel shaft of diameter $60\,\text{mm}$ transmits a torque of $2\,\text{kN·m}$ and a bending moment of $1.5\,\text{kN·m}$. Find the principal stresses and maximum shear stress at the surface. Take $E = 200\,\text{GPa}$, $\nu = 0.3$.

**Step 1 — Bending Stress:**

$$\sigma_x = \frac{32 M}{\pi d^3} = \frac{32 \times 1.5 \times 10^3}{\pi (0.06)^3} = \frac{48000}{\pi \times 2.16 \times 10^{-4}}$$

Computing the denominator: $\pi \times 2.16 \times 10^{-4} = 6.7858 \times 10^{-4}$ m³.

$$\sigma_x = \frac{48000}{6.7858 \times 10^{-4}} = 70.74 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 70.74 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Torsional Shear Stress:**

$$\tau_{xy} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 2 \times 10^3}{\pi (0.06)^3} = \frac{32000}{6.7858 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\tau_{xy} = 47.16 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 47.16 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Principal Stresses:**

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{70.74}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{70.74}{2}\right)^2 + (47.16)^2}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 35.37 \pm \sqrt{1251.04 + 2224.07} = 35.37 \pm \sqrt{3475.11}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 35.37 \pm 58.95 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_1 = 94.32 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = -23.58 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Shear Stress:**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \frac{94.32 - (-23.58)}{2} = 58.95 \text{ MPa}$$

This matches the radius calculation in Step 3, confirming the result.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Diagram: Stress-Analysis Logic Flow

This block diagram maps the decision sequence a designer follows when a shaft or element is subjected to combined loading — exactly the path expected in KTU 14-mark problems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Loads on Element] --> B{Loading Type?}
    B -->|Axial| C[Use σ = P/A only]
    B -->|Torsion| D[Use τ = Tr/J, solid or hollow]
    B -->|Bending| E[Use σ = My/I, find extreme fibre]
    B -->|Combined M + T| F[Compute σ_x from M and τ_xy from T at surface]
    F --> G[Set σ_y = 0 for pure bending + torsion case]
    G --> H[Compute σ_1, σ_2 from principal stress formula]
    H --> I[Compute τ_max = σ_1 - σ_2 over 2]
    I --> J{Material Type?}
    J -->|Ductile| K[Apply DE Theory: σ_e = sqrt σ_1² − σ_1σ_2 + σ_2²]
    J -->|Brittle| L[Apply Rankine: σ_1 ≤ σ_allow / n]
    K --> M[Compute Factor of Safety n = σ_y / σ_e]
    L --> M
    M --> N[End: Accept or Redesign with K_t factor]
    C --> O[Compare σ with σ_allow]
    D --> O
    E --> O
```

---

## 4.2 Mohr's Circle — Construction Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Plot Point X: σ_x, τ_xy] --> B[Plot Point Y: σ_y, −τ_xy]
    B --> C[Draw line XY]
    C --> D[Locate Centre C on σ-axis at σ_x + σ_y over 2, 0]
    D --> E[Draw Circle with radius R = half of XY]
    E --> F[Mark extreme horizontal points: σ_1, σ_2]
    E --> G[Mark top and bottom points: ±τ_max]
    F --> H[Principal directions at angles θ_p and θ_p + 90]
    G --> I[τ_max planes at 45 degrees to principal planes]
```

---

## 4.3 Stress-Strain Curve — Behavioural Zones Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Origin O] --> B[Linear Elastic: σ = Eε]
    B --> C[Proportional Limit]
    C --> D[Elastic Limit]
    D --> E[Yield Point σ_y]
    E --> F[Plastic Region - Strain Hardening]
    F --> G[Ultimate Tensile Strength σ_u]
    G --> H[Necking and Fracture]
    E -.-> I[Resilience: Area under elastic part = σ_y² / 2E]
    H -.-> J[Toughness: Total area under curve]
```

---

## 4.4 Combined Stress Element — Visual Schematic (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph elemA[Element with Bending only]
        A1[σ_x top = +M/Z] --> A2[σ_x bottom = −M/Z]
        A2 --> A3[Neutral Axis: σ = 0]
    end
    subgraph elemB[Element with Torsion only]
        B1[τ_xy on x-face] --> B2[τ_yx on y-face, complementary]
        B2 --> B3[Pure shear state, no normal stress]
    end
    subgraph elemC[Combined: Bending + Torsion]
        C1[σ_x from M on top fibre] --> C2[τ_xy from T on top fibre]
        C2 --> C3[σ_y = 0 assumed]
        C3 --> C4[Compute σ_1, σ_2]
        C4 --> C5[Compute τ_max]
    end
```

---

## 4.5 Stress Concentration at a Fillet — Block View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Smaller shaft diameter d] --> B[Sharp Fillet radius r]
    B --> C[Larger shaft diameter D]
    B -.-> D[Stress Amplification: σ_max = K_t × σ_nom]
    D --> E[Read K_t from chart using D over d and r over d]
    E --> F[Design: Increase r or use gradual transition]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Design Rule:** For a stepped shaft with shoulder fillet, choose the fillet radius $r$ as large as possible (typically $r \approx 0.1 D$) to minimise $K_t$. A generous fillet can reduce $K_t$ from 3.0 to 1.5, halving the peak stress.

---
