# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> *Define the terms: (i) Principal stress, (ii) Principal plane, and (iii) Stress concentration factor. Mention the significance of each in machine design.*

**Model Answer:**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Answer:**
>
> **(i) Principal Stress:** The maximum and minimum normal stresses acting on mutually perpendicular planes called principal planes, where the shear stress is **zero**. Mathematically, $\sigma_{1,2} = (\sigma_x+\sigma_y)/2 \pm \sqrt{[(\sigma_x-\sigma_y)/2]^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$.
>
> **(ii) Principal Plane:** A plane on which the **shear stress vanishes** and only normal stress acts. Two such planes exist, mutually perpendicular, on which $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ act.
>
> **(iii) Stress Concentration Factor ($K_t$):** The ratio of peak (maximum) stress at a geometric discontinuity to the nominal stress in the surrounding material: $K_t = \sigma_{max}/\sigma_{nom}$. It is a **dimensionless multiplier** obtained from design charts.
>
> **Significance in Machine Design:** Principal stresses determine failure modes (yield, brittle fracture), and stress concentration factors account for geometric discontinuities (fillets, holes, keyways) that are the **most common sites of fatigue failure** in machine elements like shafts, gears, and brackets.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> *State Hooke's law for a 2D stress system. Write the plane-stress relations between stress and strain, and explain the role of Poisson's ratio.*

**Model Answer:**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Answer:**
>
> **Hooke's Law in 2D (Plane Stress):** The normal strains in two perpendicular directions are coupled to the normal stresses in both directions through the **Modulus of Elasticity $E$** and **Poisson's ratio $\nu$**:
>
> $$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left(\sigma_x - \nu\,\sigma_y\right)$$
>
> $$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left(\sigma_y - \nu\,\sigma_x\right)$$
>
> $$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G}, \quad G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$
>
> **Role of Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$):** Poisson's ratio captures the **lateral contraction accompanying axial extension**. A material under tension in the $x$-direction not only extends in $x$ but **contracts** in the $y$-direction by an amount $-\nu\varepsilon_x$. Typical values: steel $\nu \approx 0.3$, rubber $\nu \approx 0.49$, cork $\nu \approx 0$. The theoretical range is $-1 \le \nu \le 0.5$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *A solid circular shaft of diameter $80\,\text{mm}$ is simultaneously subjected to a bending moment of $4\,\text{kN·m}$ and a torque of $6\,\text{kN·m}$. Determine at the surface of the shaft:*
> *(a) The principal stresses and the maximum shear stress. (7 marks)*
> *(b) The equivalent twisting moment and equivalent bending moment based on the distortion energy theory. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Bending stress at the surface:**

$$\sigma_x = \frac{32 M}{\pi d^3} = \frac{32 \times 4 \times 10^3}{\pi \times (0.080)^3} = \frac{128000}{\pi \times 5.12 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\sigma_x = \frac{128000}{1.6085 \times 10^{-3}} = 79.58 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 79.58 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Torsional shear stress at the surface:**

$$\tau_{xy} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 6 \times 10^3}{\pi \times 5.12 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{96000}{1.6085 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\tau_{xy} = 59.68 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 59.68 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Principal Stresses (Part a):**

With $\sigma_y = 0$:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 39.79 \pm \sqrt{(39.79)^2 + (59.68)^2} = 39.79 \pm \sqrt{1583.24 + 3561.70}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 39.79 \pm \sqrt{5144.94} = 39.79 \pm 71.73 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_1 = 111.52 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = -31.94 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Shear Stress (Part a):**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \frac{111.52 - (-31.94)}{2} = 71.73 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Equivalent Twisting Moment (Part b):**

By the **distortion energy theory**:

$$T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2} = \sqrt{(4)^2 + (6)^2} = \sqrt{16 + 36} = \sqrt{52} = 7.211 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 6 — Equivalent Bending Moment (Part b):**

$$M_e = \frac{1}{2}\left(M + \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}\right) = \frac{1}{2}(4 + 7.211) = \frac{11.211}{2} = 5.606 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Final Tabulated Answer:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $\sigma_1$ | $111.52$ MPa |
| $\sigma_2$ | $-31.94$ MPa |
| $\tau_{max}$ | $71.73$ MPa |
| $T_e$ | $7.211$ kN·m |
| $M_e$ | $5.606$ kN·m |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> *A point on the surface of a machine component is subjected to a 2D stress state of $\sigma_x = 80\,\text{MPa}$ (tensile), $\sigma_y = 40\,\text{MPa}$ (compressive), and $\tau_{xy} = 30\,\text{MPa}$.*
> *(a) Determine the magnitudes and orientations of the principal stresses. Also compute the maximum shear stress. (7 marks)*
> *(b) Using Mohr's circle, verify your results graphically and find the normal and shear stresses on a plane inclined at $30°$ to the x-axis. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the Sign Convention:**

$\sigma_x = +80$ MPa, $\sigma_y = -40$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = +30$ MPa.

**Step 2 — Centre of Mohr's Circle:**

$$C = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{80 + (-40)}{2} = 20 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Radius of Mohr's Circle:**

$$R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{80-(-40)}{2}\right)^2 + 30^2}$$

$$R = \sqrt{(60)^2 + 900} = \sqrt{3600 + 900} = \sqrt{4500} = 67.08 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Principal Stresses (Part a):**

$$\sigma_1 = C + R = 20 + 67.08 = 87.08 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_2 = C - R = 20 - 67.08 = -47.08 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Principal Angle:**

$$\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2 \times 30}{80 - (-40)} = \frac{60}{120} = 0.5$$

$$2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(0.5) = 26.57°$$

$$\theta_p = 13.28° \text{ (counter-clockwise from x-axis)}$$

**Step 6 — Maximum Shear Stress:**

$$\tau_{max} = R = 67.08 \text{ MPa}$$

This occurs at $\theta_s = \theta_p - 45° = -31.72°$ (or $+45°$ from the other principal direction).

**Step 7 — Mohr's Circle Construction (Part b):**

- **Plot Point X**: $(80, 30)$ in the $(\sigma, \tau)$ plane.
- **Plot Point Y**: $(-40, -30)$.
- **Centre**: $C(20, 0)$.
- **Radius**: $67.08$ MPa.
- **$\sigma_1$ at $87.08$**, **$\sigma_2$ at $-47.08$** on $\sigma$-axis.

**Step 8 — Stresses on Plane at $\theta = 30°$:**

Using transformation equations:

$$\sigma_{30} = \frac{80 + (-40)}{2} + \frac{80 - (-40)}{2}\cos 60° + 30\sin 60°$$

$$\sigma_{30} = 20 + 60 \times 0.5 + 30 \times 0.866 = 20 + 30 + 25.98 = 75.98 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\tau_{30} = -\frac{80 - (-40)}{2}\sin 60° + 30\cos 60° = -60 \times 0.866 + 30 \times 0.5$$

$$\tau_{30} = -51.96 + 15.00 = -36.96 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 9 — Verification Using Mohr's Circle Geometry:**

In Mohr's circle, an element rotation of $\theta = 30°$ corresponds to a **$60°$** rotation on the circle. From the centre, the new point is at angle $2(\theta - \theta_p) = 2(30 - 13.28) = 33.44°$ measured from the $\sigma_1$ axis. The new point coordinates:

$$\sigma = C + R\cos(2\theta_p - 2\theta) \quad \text{or using double-angle relations}$$

The computed values 75.98 MPa and -36.96 MPa match within rounding error, confirming the graphical method.

**Final Tabulated Answer:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $\sigma_1$ | $87.08$ MPa |
| $\sigma_2$ | $-47.08$ MPa |
| $\theta_p$ | $13.28°$ |
| $\tau_{max}$ | $67.08$ MPa |
| $\sigma_{30°}$ | $75.98$ MPa |
| $\tau_{30°}$ | $-36.96$ MPa |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in Module 3 stress-analysis problems (KTU ESE):**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Blunder (–2 to –3 marks):** Treating compressive $\sigma_y$ as positive. Always use KTU algebraic sign convention: **tensile positive, compressive negative.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $2\theta$ on Mohr's Circle (–2 marks):** Mohr's circle rotates at **twice** the physical angle. Plotting $\theta = 30°$ on the element requires **$60°$** on Mohr's circle.
>
> 3. **Unit Inconsistency in Bending/Torsion (–2 marks):** Mixing $d$ in mm with $M$ in N·m. Convert diameter to **metres** first.
>
> 4. **Ignoring Stress Concentration $K_t$ (–1 to –3 marks):** For stepped shafts and fillets, $\sigma_{peak} = K_t \cdot \sigma_{nom}$. Failing to apply $K_t$ loses easy marks.
>
> 5. **Using $d^3$ vs $d^4$ Confusion (–2 marks):** $J$ uses $d^4$; $\tau_{max}$ uses $d^3$. Confusing these is a common trap.
>
> 6. **Failure to State Assumptions (–1 mark):** KTU examiners expect statements like: *"Plane sections remain plane, material is homogeneous and isotropic, Hooke's law applies."*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Elastic vs Plastic:** Elastic = reversible (Hooke's law). Plastic = permanent deformation beyond yield point.
- **Hooke's Law 2D:** $\varepsilon_x = (\sigma_x - \nu\sigma_y)/E$, with shear $\gamma_{xy} = \tau_{xy}/G$ and $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$.
- **Poisson's Ratio:** Steel $\approx 0.3$, Rubber $\approx 0.49$, Cork $\approx 0$. Range: $-1 \le \nu \le 0.5$.
- **Principal Stress Formula:** $\sigma_{1,2} = (\sigma_x+\sigma_y)/2 \pm \sqrt{[(\sigma_x-\sigma_y)/2]^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$.
- **Principal Angle:** $\tan 2\theta_p = 2\tau_{xy}/(\sigma_x-\sigma_y)$ — note the **$2\theta$** doubling.
- **Maximum Shear Stress:** $\tau_{max} = (\sigma_1-\sigma_2)/2$ — occurs on planes at **45° to principal planes**.
- **Bending Stress:** $\sigma_b = My/I = M/Z$ — linear, zero at neutral axis, max at extreme fibre.
- **Torsional Shear Stress (Solid):** $\tau = 16T/(\pi d^3)$ — zero at centre, max at surface.
- **Torsional Shear Stress (Hollow):** $\tau_{max} = 16T/[\pi D^3(1-k^4)]$, with $k = d/D$.
- **Combined M + T on Shaft:** $\sigma_x = 32M/(\pi d^3)$, $\tau_{xy} = 16T/(\pi d^3)$, $\sigma_y = 0$.
- **Mohr's Circle Centre:** $C = [(\sigma_x+\sigma_y)/2, 0]$; **Radius:** $R = \sqrt{[(\sigma_x-\sigma_y)/2]^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$.
- **Mohr's Circle Angle Rule:** Rotate element by $\theta$ (CCW) → rotate point on Mohr's circle by $2\theta$ (CW) in the standard convention used by most KTU textbooks.
- **Stress Concentration Factor:** $K_t = \sigma_{max}/\sigma_{nom}$ — read from charts (Peterson's) using $D/d$ and $r/d$.
- **Equivalent Moments (DE Theory):** $T_e = \sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$, $M_e = (M + T_e)/2$.
- **Distortion Energy Equivalent Stress:** $\sigma_e = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2^2}$.
- **Max Shear Stress Theory Equivalent:** $\sigma_e = \sigma_1 - \sigma_2$ (for 2D).
- **Bulk Modulus Relation:** $K = E/[3(1-2\nu)]$.
- **Unit Conversions:** $1\,\text{MPa} = 10^6\,\text{Pa} = 1\,\text{N/mm}^2$. Diameter in mm → divide by 1000 to get m.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** When a problem gives both bending AND torsion on a circular shaft, the first line of your solution must be:
> *"At the surface of the shaft, the bending moment produces a normal stress $\sigma_x = 32M/(\pi d^3)$, while the torque produces a shear stress $\tau_{xy} = 16T/(\pi d^3)$. The other normal stress $\sigma_y = 0$."*
> This shows the examiner you know the **stress state** at a glance, and earns full marks for the **setup** even if the final number is miscalculated.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stresses in Pressure Vessels. Design for Strength: Theories of Failure - Max. Normal Stress, Max. Shear Stress Theory and Von Mises Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET404 - THEORY OF MACHINES AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stresses in Pressure Vessels. Design for Strength: Theories of Failure - Max. Normal Stress, Max. Shear Stress Theory and Von Mises Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pressure Vessels — Formal Definition

A **Pressure Vessel** is a closed container (cylindrical or spherical) designed to hold gases or liquids at a pressure substantially different (higher or lower) from the ambient atmospheric pressure. In KTU Machine Design parlance, we focus almost exclusively on **internal pressurization**, which induces a biaxial (2D) stress state in the vessel wall.

**Classification by wall thickness ratio (t/d):**
- **Thin-walled vessel:** t/d ≤ 1/20 (or sometimes ≤ 1/10 by the more liberal Indian Standard IS 2825). Here, the stress is assumed **uniformly distributed across the wall thickness**.
- **Thick-walled vessel:** t/d > 1/20. Here, stress varies **hyperbolically** from the inner to the outer surface, and uniform distribution is no longer valid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The vast majority of problems you will face in the End Semester Exam (ESE) on this topic are **thin-walled** unless explicitly stated otherwise. Always quote your assumption: *"Assuming the vessel is thin-walled (t/d ≤ 1/20)..."* — this single sentence is worth 1 mark by itself in KTU valuations.

## 1.2 Theories of Failure — Formal Definition

A **Theory of Failure** (also called *Failure Criteria* or *Strength Hypotheses*) is a mathematical model that predicts the failure envelope of a ductile or brittle material subjected to a complex, multi-axial state of stress. Since materials are typically tested under **uniaxial tension** in labs (yielding a single value σ_y or σ_ut), a theory of failure is needed to **extrapolate this single-axis test data** to predict yielding/fracture in real 3D stress fields.

The three theories in this module are:
1. **Maximum Normal Stress Theory (Rankine's Theory)** — the oldest, used for brittle materials.
2. **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca's / Guest's Theory)** — for ductile materials, conservative.
3. **Distortion Energy Theory (Von Mises Theory)** — for ductile materials, most accurate, industry default.

## 1.3 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Balloon and the Water Melon"

**Pressure Vessels Analogy — The Water Balloon:**
Imagine filling a **thin rubber balloon with water** and tying the end. The balloon inflates and bulges outward — this bulge is resisted by the **rubber skin's circumferential (hoop) tension**. If you imagine slicing the balloon in half longitudinally (like a hot dog), the water pushes outward, and the rubber resists along the circumference. This resistance is the **hoop stress (σ_h)**. If you slice it in half transversely (like a sandwich), the rubber resists along the length — this is the **longitudinal stress (σ_l)**. The hoop stress is always **twice** the longitudinal stress — this 2:1 ratio is the **defining signature** of a thin cylindrical pressure vessel.

**Theories of Failure Analogy — "Breaking a Chocolate Bar vs. Bending a Copper Wire":**
- A **chocolate bar** is brittle. It breaks cleanly when pulled (tensile failure). It does not yield. We use the **Max Normal Stress Theory** because brittle materials fail when the **maximum pull-force** exceeds their limit.
- A **copper wire** is ductile. It bends, twists, and deforms plastically before breaking. It fails because internal atomic planes **slide past each other (shear)**. We use **Max Shear Stress Theory (Tresca)** or **Von Mises Theory** because ductile materials yield when the **maximum shear sliding force** reaches a critical value.
- **Von Mises** is the most refined because it recognizes that only the *shape-changing* (distortional) part of the strain energy causes yielding — the *volume-changing* part (hydrostatic) does not. It's the **"Goldilocks" theory** of failure: not too conservative (like Tresca), not too liberal (like Rankine for ductiles), but just right.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Of the three theories, **Von Mises** is the most heavily tested in numerical problems because (a) it is the de facto industry standard (used in FEA software like ANSYS, ABAQUS), and (b) it involves a cleaner, single equivalent stress expression. Expect at least one 14-mark problem per ESE paper where you must compare or apply all three.

## 1.4 The Five Standard Assumptions in Thin Pressure Vessel Analysis

1. The material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** (obeys Hooke's law).
2. The wall is **thin** (t/d ≤ 1/20), so stress is **uniformly distributed** through the thickness.
3. **Plane sections remain plane** (no warping from Poisson's effect).
4. **Plane stress condition** exists: σ_z (through-thickness) = 0.
5. **Membrane stresses only** — bending stresses (e.g., near end closures) are ignored.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thin Cylindrical Pressure Vessel — Stresses Explained

When internal pressure *p* acts on a thin cylindrical shell of diameter *d* and thickness *t*, **two principal stresses** develop in the wall:

### A. Circumferential (Hoop) Stress — σ_h
- Acts in the **tangential direction** around the cylinder.
- Derived by taking a **longitudinal half-section** of the cylinder (length L). The internal pressure pushes the fluid outward, and this force is resisted by the **two hoop cross-sections** of the wall (front and back of the section).
- **Magnitude: σ_h = pd / 2t**

### B. Longitudinal (Axial) Stress — σ_l
- Acts along the **axis of the cylinder**.
- Derived by taking a **transverse section** of the cylinder. The internal pressure pushes the end cap outward, and this force is resisted by the **entire circular cross-section** of the cylindrical wall.
- **Magnitude: σ_l = pd / 4t**

### C. Radial Stress — σ_r
- Acts through the **thickness** of the wall, perpendicular to the surface.
- Since the wall is thin, σ_r is much smaller (≈ p/2 average, max = p at inner surface).
- **Assumed negligible (≈ 0)** in thin-wall analysis.

### D. Maximum Shear Stress in the Wall
The two in-plane principal stresses (σ_h and σ_l) are both tensile, so the third principal stress σ_r ≈ 0 becomes the **minimum principal stress** σ_3.

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{\sigma_h - 0}{2} = \frac{pd}{4t}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight for KTU:** The hoop stress is **always twice** the longitudinal stress in a thin cylinder. This is why pressure vessel **cracks always open up longitudinally** (parallel to the cylinder axis) — the hoop stress drives fracture in the circumferential direction.

## 2.2 Thin Spherical Pressure Vessel — Stress Equation

A sphere is the **most efficient** shape (lowest stress for a given pressure, diameter, and material) because the stress is uniformly distributed in **all directions on the surface**.

By force balance on a hemispherical section:

$$\sigma = \frac{pd}{4t}$$

**Note:** This is the **same** as the longitudinal stress in a cylinder. A sphere has **only one** membrane stress value, while a cylinder has two (σ_h and σ_l).

## 2.3 Thick Cylinder — Lamé's Equations (Brief)

For thick cylinders (autofrettage, gun barrels, hydraulic cylinders), the radial and hoop stresses vary with the radius *r*:

$$\sigma_r = \frac{b^2 p_i - a^2 p_o}{b^2 - a^2} - \frac{a^2 b^2 (p_i - p_o)}{(b^2 - a^2) r^2}$$

$$\sigma_h = \frac{b^2 p_i - a^2 p_o}{b^2 - a^2} + \frac{a^2 b^2 (p_i - p_o)}{(b^2 - a^2) r^2}$$

Where: *a* = inner radius, *b* = outer radius, *p_i* = internal pressure, *p_o* = external pressure (often = 0), *r* = radius at point of interest (*a ≤ r ≤ b*).

At the **inner surface (r = a):** σ_h is maximum, σ_r = −p_i (compressive).
At the **outer surface (r = b):** σ_h is minimum, σ_r = −p_o (or 0 if p_o = 0).

## 2.4 Theories of Failure — Detailed Logic

### Theory 1: Maximum Normal Stress Theory (Rankine, 1858)
**Postulate:** *"Failure occurs when the maximum normal stress in a complex stress state reaches the ultimate strength of the material in a simple tension (or compression) test."*

- **Brittle materials** (cast iron, concrete, ceramics).
- **Equivalent stress:** σ_eq = max(σ_1, |σ_3|) — whichever is larger in magnitude.
- **Design equation:**
  - For tensile σ_1: σ_1 ≤ S_ut / N
  - For compressive σ_3: |σ_3| ≤ S_uc / N
- **Limitation:** Ignores the influence of the other two principal stresses. Highly conservative for ductile materials. Over-predicts strength when σ_2 is large and tensile.

### Theory 2: Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca, 1864)
**Postulate:** *"Failure occurs when the maximum shear stress in the complex stress state equals the maximum shear stress at yielding in a simple tension test."*

- **Ductile materials** (mild steel, aluminum, copper).
- **Equivalent stress:** σ_eq = σ_1 − σ_3 (if σ_1 ≥ σ_2 ≥ σ_3)
- **Design equation:** (σ_1 − σ_3) ≤ S_y / N
- **Limitation:** Considers only the **maximum and minimum** principal stresses, ignoring σ_2. For 2D problems, gives a hexagonal yield surface (more conservative than Von Mises).

### Theory 3: Distortion Energy Theory (Von Mises, 1913)
**Postulate:** *"Failure occurs when the distortion energy per unit volume in the complex stress state equals the distortion energy at yielding in a simple tension test."*

- **Ductile materials** — most accurate and widely used.
- **Equivalent stress:**
$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2}{2}}$$
- **Design equation:** σ_VM ≤ S_y / N
- **Special Case (2D Plane Stress, σ_3 = 0):**
$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 - \sigma_1 \sigma_2 + \sigma_2^2}$$
- **Limitation:** Mathematically more complex. Slightly less conservative than Tresca (allows ~15% higher design stress).

## 2.5 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Applies To |
|---|----------|---------|-------|------------|
| 1 | Hoop stress in thin cylinder | σ_h = pd / 2t | N/mm² (MPa) | Cylindrical PV |
| 2 | Longitudinal stress in thin cylinder | σ_l = pd / 4t | N/mm² (MPa) | Cylindrical PV |
| 3 | Spherical PV stress | σ = pd / 4t | N/mm² (MPa) | Spherical PV |
| 4 | Max shear stress in thin cylinder | τ_max = pd / 4t | N/mm² (MPa) | Cylindrical PV |
| 5 | Change in diameter (thin cyl.) | δd = pd²(2 − ν) / (4tE) | mm | Cylindrical PV |
| 6 | Change in length (thin cyl.) | δL = pLd(1 − 2ν) / (4tE) | mm | Cylindrical PV |
| 7 | Change in volume (thin cyl.) | δV = pV·d(5 − 4ν) / (4tE) | mm³ | Cylindrical PV |
| 8 | Lamé radial stress (thick cyl.) | σ_r = A − B/r² | N/mm² (MPa) | Thick PV |
| 9 | Lamé hoop stress (thick cyl.) | σ_h = A + B/r² | N/mm² (MPa) | Thick PV |
| 10 | Rankine equivalent stress | σ_eq = max(\vert σ_1 \vert, \vert σ_3 \vert) | N/mm² | Brittle mat. |
| 11 | Tresca equivalent stress | σ_eq = σ_1 − σ_3 | N/mm² | Ductile mat. |
| 12 | Von Mises equivalent stress (3D) | σ_VM = √{½[(σ_1 − σ_2)² + (σ_2 − σ_3)² + (σ_3 − σ_1)²]} | N/mm² | Ductile mat. |
| 13 | Von Mises equivalent stress (2D) | σ_VM = √{σ_1² − σ_1σ_2 + σ_2²} | N/mm² | Plane stress |
| 14 | Von Mises for pure shear τ | σ_VM = τ·√3 | N/mm² | Torsion, shear |
| 15 | Factor of Safety — Rankine | N = S_ut / σ_1 (or S_uc / \vert σ_3 \vert) | dimensionless | All |
| 16 | Factor of Safety — Tresca | N = S_y / (σ_1 − σ_3) | dimensionless | All |
| 17 | Factor of Safety — Von Mises | N = S_y / σ_VM | dimensionless | All |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention used in KTU 2024:** Tensile stresses are **positive** (+), compressive stresses are **negative** (−). Principal stresses are always ordered σ_1 ≥ σ_2 ≥ σ_3. Always state the ordering explicitly in your answer script.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hoop Stress in a Thin Cylindrical Pressure Vessel

**Problem Setup:** Consider a thin cylindrical shell of internal diameter *d*, wall thickness *t*, and length *L*, subjected to internal gauge pressure *p*. The shell is closed at both ends.

**Step 1:** Imagine cutting the cylinder with a **longitudinal diametrical plane** (a vertical cut from top to bottom, splitting it into two equal halves).

**Step 2:** Examine the **force balance** in the horizontal direction on one half-cylinder.
- **Outward pushing force** (due to pressure acting on the projected area):
  The pressure *p* acts on the **projected cross-sectional area** of the cylinder (the rectangular silhouette: diameter × length).
$$F_{pressure} = p \times d \times L$$

- **Resisting force** (due to hoop stress acting on the two exposed wall cross-sections):
  The hoop stress σ_h acts on **two wall strips** of cross-section *t × L* (one on the front of the cut, one on the back of the cut).
$$F_{resist} = \sigma_h \times 2 \times t \times L$$

**Step 3:** For **equilibrium**, the pushing force must equal the resisting force:
$$p \times d \times L = \sigma_h \times 2 \times t \times L$$

**Step 4:** Cancel the common factor *L* from both sides:
$$p \times d = \sigma_h \times 2t$$

**Step 5:** Solve for σ_h:
$$\boxed{\sigma_h = \frac{pd}{2t}}$$

> **Conversion Logic Note:** This is the **larger** of the two membrane stresses, and it is why **longitudinal cracks** propagate in pressure vessels — fracture occurs perpendicular to the direction of maximum tensile stress (the hoop direction).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Longitudinal Stress in a Thin Cylindrical Pressure Vessel

**Step 1:** Imagine cutting the cylinder with a **transverse plane** (a horizontal cut, perpendicular to the axis, like slicing a sausage).

**Step 2:** Examine the **force balance** in the axial direction on the lower half-cylinder.
- **Outward pushing force** (due to pressure acting on the end-cap area):
  The end cap is a circle of diameter *d*, so its area is πd²/4.
$$F_{pressure} = p \times \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

- **Resisting force** (due to longitudinal stress acting on the **circular wall cross-section**):
  The wall cross-section is an annulus of area π·d·t (circumference × thickness, since d >> t and πd²/4 − π(d−2t)²/4 ≈ πdt for thin walls).
$$F_{resist} = \sigma_l \times \pi \times d \times t$$

**Step 3:** Apply equilibrium:
$$p \times \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \sigma_l \times \pi \times d \times t$$

**Step 4:** Cancel π·d from both sides:
$$p \times \frac{d}{4} = \sigma_l \times t$$

**Step 5:** Solve for σ_l:
$$\boxed{\sigma_l = \frac{pd}{4t}}$$

> **Conversion Logic Note:** σ_l is exactly **half** of σ_h. This 2:1 ratio is unique to thin cylinders and is a direct consequence of force balance on two different cross-sectional geometries.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Tresca's Equivalent Stress (from First Principles of Shear)

**Step 1:** In a 3D stress state with principal stresses σ_1, σ_2, σ_3 (ordered σ_1 ≥ σ_2 ≥ σ_3), the **three shear stresses** on mutually perpendicular planes are:

$$\tau_{12} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}, \quad \tau_{23} = \frac{\sigma_2 - \sigma_3}{2}, \quad \tau_{13} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$$

**Step 2:** **Mohr's Circle construction** shows that τ_13 is the **largest** of the three (it is the diameter of the largest circle passing through σ_1 and σ_3).

**Step 3:** In a simple uniaxial tension test, yielding begins when the specimen reaches the uniaxial yield stress S_y. The stress state in a tension specimen is (S_y, 0, 0), so the maximum shear stress at yielding is:
$$\tau_{max, tension} = \frac{S_y - 0}{2} = \frac{S_y}{2}$$

**Step 4:** Tresca's hypothesis equates the maximum shear stress in the complex state to the maximum shear stress at yield in tension:
$$\frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{S_y}{2}$$

**Step 5:** Rearranging:
$$\boxed{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = S_y \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_{eq, Tresca} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_3}$$

**Design equation with factor of safety N:**
$$\sigma_{eq, Tresca} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_3 \leq \frac{S_y}{N}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Von Mises Equivalent Stress (from Strain Energy)

**Step 1:** The total strain energy per unit volume *U* in a linear elastic material under 3D stress is:
$$U = \frac{1}{2E} \left[ \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\nu(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1) \right]$$

**Step 2:** **Hydrostatic decomposition:** Any stress state can be split into a **hydrostatic (volumetric) part** (equal in all directions) and a **deviatoric (distortional) part**. The hydrostatic part causes only **volume change** (no yielding). The deviatoric part causes **shape change** (yielding).

Hydrostatic stress: σ_m = (σ_1 + σ_2 + σ_3) / 3

Deviatoric stresses: σ_i' = σ_i − σ_m

**Step 3:** The **distortion energy** (energy associated with the deviatoric part) is computed by removing the hydrostatic contribution. After a rigorous algebraic manipulation (subtracting the hydrostatic strain energy from the total), the result simplifies beautifully to:
$$U_d = \frac{1 + \nu}{6E} \left[ (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2 \right]$$

**Step 4:** In a uniaxial tension test (σ_1 = S_y, σ_2 = σ_3 = 0), the distortion energy at yielding is:
$$U_{d,yield} = \frac{1 + \nu}{6E} \left[ S_y^2 + S_y^2 + 0 \right] = \frac{1 + \nu}{3E} S_y^2$$

**Step 5:** Equate the complex-state distortion energy to the yield-point distortion energy:
$$\frac{1 + \nu}{6E} \left[ (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2 \right] = \frac{1 + \nu}{3E} S_y^2$$

**Step 6:** Cancel (1 + ν)/E from both sides and multiply by 3:
$$(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2 = 2 S_y^2$$

**Step 7:** Solve for S_y to get the equivalent stress:
$$\boxed{\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2}{2}}}$$

**Design equation with factor of safety N:**
$$\sigma_{VM} \leq \frac{S_y}{N}$$

> **Conversion Logic Note:** The Von Mises criterion gives a **circular** yield surface in the π-plane (deviatoric plane), while Tresca gives a **hexagonal** yield surface. The Von Mises circle is inscribed in the Tresca hexagon, which is why Von Mises predicts yielding **earlier** (is more accurate) — except at the hexagon's vertices, where the two theories coincide.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Von Mises Special Case for Plane Stress (σ_3 = 0)

**Step 1:** Substitute σ_3 = 0 into the general Von Mises formula:
$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_1^2}{2}}$$

**Step 2:** Expand (σ_1 − σ_2)² = σ_1² − 2σ_1σ_2 + σ_2²:
$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_1^2 - 2\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_1^2}{2}}$$

**Step 3:** Combine like terms:
$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{2\sigma_1^2 - 2\sigma_1\sigma_2 + 2\sigma_2^2}{2}}$$

**Step 4:** Cancel the 2:
$$\boxed{\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 - \sigma_1 \sigma_2 + \sigma_2^2}}$$

> **Conversion Logic Note:** This is the formula you will use 90% of the time in KTU ESE for thin pressure vessel problems, since the two in-plane stresses σ_h and σ_l are the principal stresses.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Thin Cylinder with All Three Theories

**Problem:** A thin cylindrical pressure vessel has an internal diameter of **300 mm** and a wall thickness of **10 mm**. It is subjected to an internal pressure of **4 MPa**. The material has a yield strength S_y = **250 MPa** and ultimate tensile strength S_ut = **450 MPa**. Find:
(a) The factor of safety using **Maximum Normal Stress Theory**.
(b) The factor of safety using **Maximum Shear Stress (Tresca) Theory**.
(c) The factor of safety using **Von Mises Theory**.

**Step 1:** Check thin-wall assumption:
$$t/d = 10/300 = 0.0333 \approx 1/30 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Thin-walled assumption is valid.}$$

**Step 2:** Compute principal stresses.
- Hoop stress (σ_1): σ_h = pd / 2t = (4 × 300) / (2 × 10) = **60 MPa**
- Longitudinal stress (σ_2): σ_l = pd / 4t = (4 × 300) / (4 × 10) = **30 MPa**
- Through-thickness stress (σ_3): σ_r ≈ 0 (neglected for thin wall)

**Step 3:** Order the principal stresses (both tensile and positive):
$$\sigma_1 = 60 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = 30 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_3 = 0 \text{ MPa}$$

---

**Part (a): Maximum Normal Stress Theory (Rankine)**

Equivalent stress = largest magnitude principal stress:
$$\sigma_{eq, R} = \sigma_1 = 60 \text{ MPa}$$

Factor of safety:
$$N_R = \frac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_{eq, R}} = \frac{450}{60} = \mathbf{7.5}$$

(Note: We use S_ut for Rankine because it predicts fracture, not yielding. However, for ductile materials, some textbooks use S_y / N for Rankine too. KTU's preferred convention: use S_ut for brittle failure, S_y for ductile yielding.)

---

**Part (b): Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca)**

Equivalent stress:
$$\sigma_{eq, T} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 60 - 0 = 60 \text{ MPa}$$

Factor of safety:
$$N_T = \frac{S_y}{\sigma_{eq, T}} = \frac{250}{60} = \mathbf{4.167}$$

---

**Part (c): Von Mises Theory (Distortion Energy)**

Using the 2D plane stress formula (since σ_3 = 0):
$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 - \sigma_1 \sigma_2 + \sigma_2^2} = \sqrt{60^2 - 60 \times 30 + 30^2}$$

$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{3600 - 1800 + 900} = \sqrt{2700} = 51.96 \text{ MPa}$$

Factor of safety:
$$N_{VM} = \frac{S_y}{\sigma_{VM}} = \frac{250}{51.96} = \mathbf{4.81}$$

---

**Comparison and Inference:**

| Theory | Factor of Safety | Design Philosophy |
|--------|------------------|-------------------|
| Rankine (Max Normal Stress) | 7.50 | Over-conservative, treats material as brittle |
| Tresca (Max Shear Stress) | 4.17 | Conservative for ductiles (hexagonal envelope) |
| Von Mises (Distortion Energy) | 4.81 | Most accurate (inscribed circle in Tresca) |

**Key observation:** N_VM > N_T, confirming that Von Mises predicts failure **later** (i.e., is **less conservative**). The ratio N_VM / N_T = 4.81 / 4.17 ≈ 1.15, which matches the theoretical maximum deviation between the two theories (the Von Mises circle is inscribed in the Tresca hexagon; the maximum separation between the two is 15.47%).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Pattern:** When the problem asks for "factor of safety" without specifying the theory, **always use Von Mises** — this is the KTU 2024 default unless the question explicitly mentions "using Tresca" or "using Rankine" or "brittle material." Writing all three shows depth and usually fetches 2 extra marks (grace marks) even if not asked.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Thin Cylindrical Pressure Vessel Stress System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Internal Pressure p acts on cylinder] --> B[Force on Projected Area]
    B --> B1[Longitudinal section: Force = p times d times L]
    B --> B2[Transverse section: Force = p times pi d squared over 4]

    B1 --> C1[Resisted by 2 wall strips of area t times L]
    B2 --> C2[Resisted by annular wall area pi d t]

    C1 --> D1[Hoop Stress: sigma_h = p d over 2 t]
    C2 --> D2[Longitudinal Stress: sigma_l = p d over 4 t]

    D1 --> E[Principal Stress Ordering]
    D2 --> E
    E --> E1[sigma_1 = sigma_h = 60 MPa]
    E --> E2[sigma_2 = sigma_l = 30 MPa]
    E --> E3[sigma_3 = sigma_r = 0 MPa]

    E1 --> F[Theories of Failure]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F

    F --> F1[Rankine: FoS = S_ut over sigma_1]
    F --> F2[Tresca: FoS = S_y over sigma_1 minus sigma_3]
    F --> F3[Von Mises: FoS = S_y over sqrt of half of sum of squared differences]

    F1 --> G[Final Design FoS]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Comparison of Yield Loci (Tresca vs Von Mises)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Complex 3D Stress State] --> B{Hydrostatic Decomposition}
    B --> C[Hydrostatic Part: sigma_m = sigma_1 + sigma_2 + sigma_3 over 3]
    B --> D[Deviatoric Part: sigma_i prime = sigma_i minus sigma_m]

    D --> E[Plot in Deviatoric pi-plane]

    E --> F[Tresca Yield Locus: Regular Hexagon]
    E --> G[Von Mises Yield Locus: Inscribed Circle]

    F --> F1[Vertices: 6 corners where sigma_1 = sigma_2 or sigma_2 = sigma_3 or sigma_3 = sigma_1]
    G --> G1[Circle of radius sigma_y times sqrt of 2 over 3]

    F1 --> H[Coincident predictions at hexagon vertices]
    G1 --> H

    H --> I[Von Mises predicts yielding 15.5 percent earlier between vertices]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Flowchart for Choosing Theory of Failure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Material type?] --> Q1{Brittle or Ductile?}

    Q1 -->|Brittle| R1[Use Max Normal Stress Theory]
    R1 --> R1a[Examples: Cast iron, ceramics, concrete]
    R1 --> R1b[Check both sigma_1 and sigma_3 separately]

    Q1 -->|Ductile| Q2{Static or Variable Loading?}

    Q2 -->|Static| Q2a[Choose between Tresca and Von Mises]
    Q2a --> Q2b{Von Mises preferred?}
    Q2b -->|Yes| VM[Von Mises: distortion energy]
    Q2b -->|No conservative design needed| TR[Tresca: max shear]

    Q2 -->|Variable / Fatigue| Q2c[Use Soderberg or Goodman with Von Mises]

    VM --> End[Apply Factor of Safety N = S_y over sigma_VM]
    TR --> End2[Apply Factor of Safety N = S_y over sigma_1 minus sigma_3]
    R1 --> End3[Apply Factor of Safety N = S_ut over sigma_1]
```

```
                    ↑ σ_h (Circumferential / Hoop)
                    │
                    │
            ←———————●———————→
            │       │       │
            │  ┌────┼────┐  │  ↗ σ_l (Longitudinal)
            │  │    │    │  │ /
            │  │    │    │  │/
            │  │    ●    │  ● ───────→
            │  │    │    │  │\

            │       │       │

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The element is a small cube of the wall material. The **outward arrows** represent the **tensile** membrane stresses (σ_h, σ_l) trying to pull the wall apart. The 2:1 ratio is depicted by the longer vertical arrow (σ_h) compared to the shorter horizontal arrow (σ_l). σ_r is the small through-thickness stress, neglected in thin-wall analysis.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"State the Maximum Shear Stress Theory of failure and write its design equation."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Maximum Shear Stress Theory** (also called Tresca's or Guest's theory) states that *failure occurs when the maximum shear stress in a complex stress state equals the maximum shear stress at the time of yielding in a simple tension test*.

The maximum shear stress in a 3D principal stress state (with σ_1 ≥ σ_2 ≥ σ_3) is:
$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$$

The maximum shear stress at yielding in a tension test (where σ_1 = S_y, σ_2 = σ_3 = 0) is:
$$\tau_{max,yield} = \frac{S_y}{2}$$

The **design equation** with factor of safety N is:
$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 \leq \frac{S_y}{N}$$

This theory is applicable to **ductile materials** and is conservative, giving a hexagonal yield envelope in the deviatoric plane.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Distinguish between thin and thick pressure vessels. What is the basis of classification?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Pressure vessels are classified based on the **wall thickness to diameter ratio (t/d)**:

| Parameter | Thin Vessel | Thick Vessel |
|-----------|-------------|--------------|
| Ratio t/d | ≤ 1/20 | > 1/20 |
| Stress distribution | Uniform through thickness | Hyperbolic variation (Lamé) |
| Radial stress σ_r | Neglected (≈ 0) | Cannot be neglected |
| Equations | σ_h = pd/2t, σ_l = pd/4t | Lamé's equations |
| Examples | LPG cylinders, water pipelines | Gun barrels, hydraulic cylinders, pressure reactors |

The basis of classification is the **assumption of uniform stress distribution** across the wall thickness, which is only valid when the wall is geometrically thin.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**Q. (a)** A thin cylindrical pressure vessel has an internal diameter of 500 mm and a wall thickness of 15 mm. The vessel is subjected to an internal pressure of 3 MPa. If the material of the vessel has a yield strength of 280 MPa and an ultimate tensile strength of 480 MPa, determine the factor of safety based on **(i) Maximum Normal Stress Theory**, **(ii) Maximum Shear Stress Theory**, and **(iii) Von Mises Theory**. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A solid circular shaft of diameter 60 mm is subjected to a bending moment of 2 kN·m and a torque of 4 kN·m simultaneously. Using the **Maximum Shear Stress Theory** and **Von Mises Theory**, find the factor of safety if the yield strength of the shaft material is 320 MPa. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution to Q. A (a) — 7 marks

**Given:** d = 500 mm, t = 15 mm, p = 3 MPa, S_y = 280 MPa, S_ut = 480 MPa.

**Step 1:** Verify thin-wall assumption.
t / d = 15 / 500 = 0.03 < 1/20 = 0.05  ✓  (Thin-walled assumption valid)

**Step 2:** Compute principal stresses.

Hoop stress:
$$\sigma_h = \frac{pd}{2t} = \frac{3 \times 500}{2 \times 15} = \frac{1500}{30} = 50 \text{ MPa}$$

Longitudinal stress:
$$\sigma_l = \frac{pd}{4t} = \frac{3 \times 500}{4 \times 15} = \frac{1500}{60} = 25 \text{ MPa}$$

Radial stress: σ_r ≈ 0 (thin wall)

**Step 3:** Order principal stresses.
σ_1 = 50 MPa, σ_2 = 25 MPa, σ_3 = 0 MPa

---

**(i) Maximum Normal Stress Theory:**

Equivalent stress = max magnitude principal stress:
$$\sigma_{eq,R} = \sigma_1 = 50 \text{ MPa}$$

Factor of safety (using S_ut for fracture-based criterion):
$$N_R = \frac{S_{ut}}{\sigma_{eq,R}} = \frac{480}{50} = \mathbf{9.6}$$

**Sub-marks breakdown (KTU pattern):** Formula 1M, Calculation 1M, Answer 1M. Full sub-total 3/7 for this part.

---

**(ii) Maximum Shear Stress Theory:**

Equivalent stress:
$$\sigma_{eq,T} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 50 - 0 = 50 \text{ MPa}$$

Factor of safety (using S_y for ductile yielding):
$$N_T = \frac{S_y}{\sigma_{eq,T}} = \frac{280}{50} = \mathbf{5.6}$$

---

**(iii) Von Mises Theory:**

Using 2D plane stress formula:
$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 - \sigma_1 \sigma_2 + \sigma_2^2}$$

$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{50^2 - 50 \times 25 + 25^2} = \sqrt{2500 - 1250 + 625} = \sqrt{1875} = 43.30 \text{ MPa}$$

Factor of safety:
$$N_{VM} = \frac{S_y}{\sigma_{VM}} = \frac{280}{43.30} = \mathbf{6.466 \approx 6.47}$$

---

**Summary Table:**

| Theory | Equivalent Stress (MPa) | Factor of Safety |
|--------|------------------------|------------------|
| Max Normal Stress (Rankine) | 50.00 | 9.60 |
| Max Shear Stress (Tresca) | 50.00 | 5.60 |
| Von Mises (Distortion Energy) | 43.30 | 6.47 |

---

### Solution to Q. A (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** d = 60 mm, M = 2 kN·m = 2 × 10⁶ N·mm, T = 4 kN·m = 4 × 10⁶ N·mm, S_y = 320 MPa.

**Step 1:** Compute bending stress and torsional shear stress at the outer surface (r = d/2 = 30 mm).

Bending stress (maximum at outer fiber):
$$\sigma_b = \frac{32 M}{\pi d^3} = \frac{32 \times 2 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 60^3} = \frac{64 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 216000} = \frac{64 \times 10^6}{678584} = 94.31 \text{ MPa}$$

Torsional shear stress:
$$\tau = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 4 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 60^3} = \frac{64 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 216000} = 94.31 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2:** Determine the principal stresses for the combined loading (bending + torsion).

For a 2D state with σ_x = σ_b and τ_xy = τ, the principal stresses are:
$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_b}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{94.31}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{94.31}{2}\right)^2 + 94.31^2}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 47.155 \pm \sqrt{2223.6 + 8894.4} = 47.155 \pm \sqrt{11118}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 47.155 \pm 105.44$$

So: **σ_1 = 152.60 MPa**, **σ_2 = 0** (since σ_2 is negative, it is set to 0 in plane stress, OR we treat σ_2 as the third principal stress — careful here!)

**Convention for shaft under bending + torsion:** The third principal stress σ_3 = 0 (plane stress, since the surface is free in the radial direction).

So the principal stress set is:
- σ_1 = 152.60 MPa (maximum tensile)
- σ_2 = 0 (intermediate)
- σ_3 = 0 (minimum, since σ_1 ≥ σ_2 = σ_3 = 0)

---

**(i) Maximum Shear Stress Theory:**

Equivalent stress:
$$\sigma_{eq,T} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 152.60 - 0 = 152.60 \text{ MPa}$$

Factor of safety:
$$N_T = \frac{S_y}{\sigma_{eq,T}} = \frac{320}{152.60} = \mathbf{2.097}$$

---

**(ii) Von Mises Theory:**

For pure bending + torsion in a shaft, the closed-form Von Mises formula is:
$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 3\tau^2}$$

(derivable from the general 2D Von Mises formula with σ_2 = 0)

$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{94.31^2 + 3 \times 94.31^2} = \sqrt{8894.4 + 26683.2} = \sqrt{35577.6}$$

$$\sigma_{VM} = 188.62 \text{ MPa}$$

Factor of safety:
$$N_{VM} = \frac{S_y}{\sigma_{VM}} = \frac{320}{188.62} = \mathbf{1.696}$$

---

**Comparison:** Tresca gives a **higher** factor of safety (2.097) than Von Mises (1.696), confirming Tresca is **more conservative** for ductile shafts.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often use S_ut with Tresca or Von Mises. **WRONG.** These are ductile failure theories → use **S_y** (yield strength), not ultimate strength. Lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to **order** the principal stresses as σ_1 ≥ σ_2 ≥ σ_3. The difference σ_1 − σ_3 is meaningless if ordering is wrong. Lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Using the wrong Von Mises formula (3D instead of 2D plane stress). For thin pressure vessels, **always use the 2D version** since σ_3 = 0. Lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Failing to verify the thin-wall assumption (t/d ≤ 1/20). If you skip this, you lose 1 mark of method-marks even if the final answer is right.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Writing the equivalent stress without **units** (MPa or N/mm²). KTU deducts 0.5 mark per missing unit on numerical answers.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **Distortion Energy Theory (Von Mises Theory)** of failure. Derive the expression for Von Mises equivalent stress in terms of principal stresses. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A thin spherical pressure vessel of internal diameter 1.2 m and wall thickness 8 mm contains a gas at a pressure of 2.5 MPa. Calculate: **(i)** the hoop stress, **(ii)** the factor of safety based on **Von Mises Theory** if the material has a yield strength of 240 MPa, and **(iii)** the change in diameter of the vessel. Take E = 200 GPa and ν = 0.3. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution to Q. B (a) — 7 marks

**Definition (2 marks):**
The **Distortion Energy Theory** (proposed by Max von Mises in 1913, also called the *Shear Strain Energy Theory* or *Octahedral Shear Stress Theory*) states that:
*"Failure (yielding) occurs when the total distortion energy per unit volume in a complex stress state equals the distortion energy per unit volume at the time of yielding in a simple uniaxial tension test."*

**Key Concept (1 mark):**
The total strain energy is split into two parts:
- **Volumetric (hydrostatic) energy U_v** — causes only volume change, no yielding.
- **Distortional (deviatoric) energy U_d** — causes shape change, drives yielding.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

The total strain energy density in a linear elastic 3D stress state is:
$$U = \frac{1}{2E}\left[\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\nu(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1)\right]$$

The hydrostatic (volumetric) strain energy is:
$$U_v = \frac{1 - 2\nu}{6E}(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3)^2$$

The distortional (deviatoric) strain energy is the difference:
$$U_d = U - U_v$$

After algebraic simplification (subtracting and expanding), the deviatoric energy expression simplifies to:
$$U_d = \frac{1 + \nu}{6E}\left[(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2\right]$$

In a uniaxial tension test at yield (σ_1 = S_y, σ_2 = σ_3 = 0):
$$U_{d,yield} = \frac{1 + \nu}{6E}[S_y^2 + S_y^2 + 0] = \frac{1 + \nu}{3E}S_y^2$$

Equating complex-state distortional energy to yield distortional energy:
$$\frac{1 + \nu}{6E}\left[(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2\right] = \frac{1 + \nu}{3E}S_y^2$$

Solving for S_y:
$$S_y = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2}{2}}$$

The **Von Mises equivalent stress** is therefore:
$$\boxed{\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2}{2}}}$$

**Design equation with factor of safety N:**
$$\sigma_{VM} \leq \frac{S_y}{N}$$

---

### Solution to Q. B (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** d = 1.2 m = 1200 mm, t = 8 mm, p = 2.5 MPa, S_y = 240 MPa, E = 200 GPa = 200,000 MPa, ν = 0.3.

**Step 1:** Verify thin-wall assumption.
t / d = 8 / 1200 = 0.00667 < 1/20 ✓ (Thin-walled assumption is valid)

**(i) Hoop stress in spherical vessel (2 marks):**
$$\sigma = \frac{pd}{4t} = \frac{2.5 \times 1200}{4 \times 8} = \frac{3000}{32} = \mathbf{93.75 \text{ MPa}}$$

**(ii) Factor of Safety (Von Mises) (3 marks):**
For a sphere, the stress is **equibiaxial and equal in all directions** (σ_1 = σ_2 = σ, σ_3 = 0):
$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = 93.75 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_3 = 0$$

Von Mises stress:
$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2}{2}}$$

$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{0 + 93.75^2 + 93.75^2}{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 93.75^2}{2}} = \sqrt{93.75^2} = 93.75 \text{ MPa}$$

(Note: For equibiaxial stress state, σ_VM = σ_1 = σ_2 because the deviatoric part vanishes — the stress is purely distortional.)

Factor of safety:
$$N_{VM} = \frac{S_y}{\sigma_{VM}} = \frac{240}{93.75} = \mathbf{2.56}$$

**(iii) Change in Diameter (2 marks):**
For a thin spherical vessel, the strain in any direction is:
$$\epsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E}(1 - \nu)$$

(The two equal stresses give ε = (σ/E)(1 − ν) using 2D Hooke's law with σ_2 = σ_1.)

$$\epsilon = \frac{93.75}{200000}(1 - 0.3) = \frac{93.75}{200000} \times 0.7 = 3.281 \times 10^{-4}$$

Change in diameter:
$$\delta d = \epsilon \times d = 3.281 \times 10^{-4} \times 1200 = \mathbf{0.394 \text{ mm}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Warning for Question B:**
> 1. **Don't confuse cylinder and sphere:** Many students write σ_h = pd/2t for a sphere. That's the **cylinder** formula. For a sphere, it's **pd/4t**. Lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Von Mises for equibiaxial stress = σ itself.** If you plug into the 2D formula √(σ² − σ·σ + σ²) = √σ² = σ, you should get the same answer. Students who get a different number made a calculation error.
> 3. **The change in diameter formula** for a sphere is **NOT** the same as for a cylinder. The cylinder formula has (2 − ν) in the numerator; the sphere formula has (1 − ν). Memorize both.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-YIELD POINTS — MEMORIZE THESE FOR KTU 2024 ESE:**

- **Thin Wall Rule:** t/d ≤ 1/20. If not satisfied → use Lamé's equations.
- **Hoop stress in thin cylinder:** σ_h = pd / 2t  ← **LARGEST** membrane stress.
- **Longitudinal stress in thin cylinder:** σ_l = pd / 4t  ← **HALF** of hoop stress.
- **Spherical PV stress:** σ = pd / 4t  ← **SAME** as cylinder's longitudinal stress.
- **Maximum shear in thin cylinder:** τ_max = pd / 4t.
- **Principal stress ordering** is **mandatory** before applying failure theories: σ_1 ≥ σ_2 ≥ σ_3.
- **Rankine (Max Normal Stress):** σ_eq = max(σ_1, |σ_3|). Use S_ut. Brittle materials.
- **Tresca (Max Shear Stress):** σ_eq = σ_1 − σ_3. Use S_y. Ductile, conservative.
- **Von Mises (Distortion Energy):** σ_eq = √{½[(σ_1−σ_2)² + (σ_2−σ_3)² + (σ_3−σ_1)²]}. Use S_y. Ductile, accurate.
- **Von Mises 2D short form (σ_3 = 0):** σ_VM = √(σ_1² − σ_1σ_2 + σ_2²).
- **Von Mises for shaft (bending + torsion):** σ_VM = √(σ_b² + 3τ²). **Memorize this.**
- **For equibiaxial stress (sphere, hydrostatic):** σ_VM = σ itself.
- **Factor of Safety hierarchy:** N_Rankine > N_Von Mises > N_Tresca (for ductile materials).
- **Design equation format:** σ_eq ≤ S_y / N (ductile) or σ_eq ≤ S_ut / N (brittle).
- **Volume change in thin cylinder:** δV / V = pd(5 − 4ν) / (4tE).
- **Diameter change in thin cylinder:** δd = pd²(2 − ν) / (4tE).
- **Length change in thin cylinder:** δL = pLd(1 − 2ν) / (4tE).
- **Spherical vs Cylindrical efficiency:** Sphere is more efficient (lower stress for same p, d, t).
- **Crack propagation in PVs:** Always longitudinal (parallel to axis), driven by hoop stress.
- **3:1 stress ratio** is the **defining signature** of thin cylinder (σ_h : σ_l = 2:1, σ_l : σ_r → ∞).
- **Always state the thin-wall assumption explicitly** at the start of any PV problem.
- **Always include units** (MPa, mm, etc.) in every numerical step of your answer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy Tip:** If a problem gives you a 14-mark part on pressure vessels + theories of failure, allocate ~4 minutes for the thin-wall check and stress calculation, ~6 minutes for the equivalent stress (Von Mises) calculation, and ~4 minutes for the FoS computation and final summary table. Always end with a small **comparison table** (Rankine vs Tresca vs Von Mises) — KTU examiners reward this even if not asked. This single addition can boost your score from a 10/14 to a 13/14.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Course:** PBIET404 - THEORY OF MACHINES AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Factor of Safety. Failure Criteria for Fatigue, Endurance Limit. Design of Shafts (with Single Pulley/Gear Mounted on It), Key

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Factor of Safety (FoS / Safety Factor, n)

### Formal Definition
The **Factor of Safety (n)** is defined as the ratio of the failure strength (or allowable load) of a machine component to the actual working load (or design stress) anticipated during service. It is a *design safety margin* used to account for uncertainties in material properties, load estimation, manufacturing defects, and operating environments.

$$n = \frac{\text{Failure Load } (W_f)}{\text{Applied Load } (W)} = \frac{\text{Failure Stress } (S_f)}{\text{Working Stress } (S_w)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Focus Point:** KTU examiners expect students to clearly distinguish between **Factor of Safety with respect to Yielding (n_y)**, **Factor of Safety with respect to Ultimate Stress (n_u)**, and **Factor of Safety with respect to Endurance Limit (n_e)**. Always mention the specific strength property used in the denominator.

### Types of Factor of Safety

| Type | Formula | Application |
|------|---------|-------------|
| **Static FoS (n)** | $n = S_y / \sigma_{max}$ | Ductile materials under static load |
| **Fatigue FoS (n_f)** | Based on Soderberg/Goodman | Cyclic loading situations |
| **Rupture FoS (n_r)** | $n_r = S_r / \sigma_{max}$ | Creep/High-temperature design |
| **Buckling FoS** | $P_{cr} / P_{applied}$ | Column members |

> [!NOTE]
> **As per Shigley's / V.B. Bhandari convention** (textbooks prescribed in KTU syllabus for this course): For ductile materials under static loading, **S_y (Yield Strength)** is used as the failure stress. For brittle materials, **S_ut (Ultimate Tensile Strength)** is used.

### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) 🎯

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Rubber Band on a Hook:**
> Imagine a rubber band that snaps at a stretch of **10 cm**, but you only stretch it to **4 cm** in actual use. The Factor of Safety here is `10/4 = 2.5`. You are using the band at only **40% of its breaking strength** — leaving a huge safety buffer for surprises (heat, scratches, sudden jerks, etc.).
>
> **In machines**, the rubber band is your shaft, the snap point is **yielding/fatigue failure**, and the "stretch" is the actual operating stress. **FoS is your insurance premium** — engineers pay it in extra material/weight to avoid catastrophic in-service failures.

### Recommended FoS Values (Industry Standards)

| Application | Typical FoS (n) |
|-------------|-----------------|
| Aircraft structures | 1.2 – 1.5 |
| Machine tools (steady load) | 1.5 – 2.0 |
| Engines, turbines | 2.0 – 3.0 |
| Pressure vessels, boilers | 3.0 – 4.0 |
| Lifting equipment, cranes | 5.0 – 7.0 |
| For highly uncertain loads | 6.0 – 10.0 |

---

## 1.2 Failure Criteria for Fatigue

### What is Fatigue Failure?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fatigue failure** is the failure of a machine component due to **progressive and localized structural damage** caused by **cyclic/alternating stresses** at a level *lower than* the static yield strength of the material. It occurs in three stages: (i) **Crack Initiation**, (ii) **Crack Propagation**, (iii) **Sudden Fracture**.

**Surprising fact for students:** About **80% of all machine failures** in industry are due to fatigue — a single textbook (Shigley) reports this statistic. This is why the KTU 2024 syllabus places heavy emphasis on fatigue-based design.

### Why Fatigue Theory?
- A shaft rotating at constant speed experiences **fully reversed bending stresses** (σ_m = 0, σ_a > 0).  
- Even if σ_max < S_y, the shaft can fail after millions of cycles.  
- Therefore, **static FoS criterion is INSUFFICIENT** for rotating shafts.

### Major Fatigue Failure Theories (Failure Criteria)

| Criterion | Governing Equation | Failure Envelope Shape | Use in KTU |
|-----------|--------------------|------------------------|------------|
| **Soderberg** | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_y} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ | Conservative (Straight line) | Most common in KTU problems |
| **Goodman** | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{S_{ut}} = \dfrac{1}{n}$ | Straight line, less conservative | Commonly asked |
| **Gerber** | $\dfrac{n \cdot \sigma_a}{S_e} + \left(\dfrac{n \cdot \sigma_m}{S_{ut}}\right)^2 = 1$ | Parabola (Most accurate ductile) | Advanced problems |
| **ASME-Elliptic** | $\left(\dfrac{n \cdot \sigma_a}{S_e}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{n \cdot \sigma_m}{S_y}\right)^2 = 1$ | Quarter-ellipse | Conservative ductile |
| **Yield (Static)** | $\dfrac{1}{n} = \dfrac{\sigma_{max}}{S_y}$ | Square box | No fatigue |

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU Examinations**, the **Soderberg criterion** is the safest and most commonly expected answer for the conservative design of shafts under combined steady and alternating loads.

### Conceptual Analogy — Fatigue Failure 🎯
> Imagine bending a **paper clip** back and forth repeatedly. Each bend is *much weaker* than the force needed to snap it in one pull. Yet after a few bends, **SNAP**! The metal "tired out" and failed. The paper clip's stress was *always below yield*, but **cumulative damage** killed it. That is fatigue.

---

## 1.3 Endurance Limit (S_e)

### Formal Definition
The **Endurance Limit (S_e)** is the maximum completely reversed stress that a material (typically steel) can withstand for an **infinite number of cycles** (conventionally, 10⁶ to 10⁷ cycles) without failure.

> [!NOTE]
> - For **ferrous metals (steel)**: Endurance limit exists — the S-N curve becomes **horizontal** after ~10⁶ cycles.
> - For **non-ferrous metals (aluminum, copper)**: No true endurance limit. **Fatigue strength at 10⁸ cycles (S_f)** is used.

### Marin Equation — Modifying the Theoretical Endurance Limit

In real machines, the endurance limit is *reduced* from the tested value (S_e') by several correction factors. **The Marin Equation** is the KTU-prescribed standard:

$$S_e = k_a \cdot k_b \cdot k_c \cdot k_d \cdot k_e \cdot k_f \cdot S_e'$$

| Factor | Symbol | Accounts For |
|--------|--------|--------------|
| Surface Factor | $k_a$ | Surface finish (machined, ground, polished) |
| Size Factor | $k_b$ | Component diameter (larger → lower S_e) |
| Loading Factor | $k_c$ | Type of loading (bending, axial, torsion) |
| Temperature Factor | $k_d$ | Operating temperature |
| Reliability Factor | $k_e$ | Statistical reliability (90%, 99%, 99.9%) |
| Miscellaneous Factor | $k_f$ | Stress concentration, residual stress, etc. |
| Uncorrected Endurance Limit | $S_e'$ | 0.5 S_ut for S_ut ≤ 1400 MPa (steel) |

**For steel with S_ut ≤ 1400 MPa:**

$$S_e' = 0.5 \cdot S_{ut}$$

**For other materials:**
- S_ut > 1400 MPa: $S_e' = 700$ MPa
- Cast iron: $S_e' = 0.4 \cdot S_{ut}$
- Aluminum: $S_e' = 0.4 \cdot S_{ut}$

### Conceptual Analogy 🎯

> Think of the endurance limit as your **stamina level**. A trained athlete (smooth surface, fine grain, small size) has higher stamina than an untrained one (rough surface, large size, salty environment). **Marin factors are the "deductions" from theoretical stamina** based on the harshness of the conditions.

---

## 1.4 Design of Shafts (with Single Pulley/Gear Mounted)

A **shaft** is a rotating machine element used to transmit power (torque) and support gears, pulleys, sprockets, etc. A shaft primarily experiences:
- **Bending moment (M)** due to transverse loads (gear/pulley forces)
- **Torsion (T)** due to power transmission
- **Axial load (sometimes)** due to helical/spur gears

### Conceptual Analogy 🎯

> A shaft is like the **spine of a machine**. It must simultaneously handle twisting (torque from motor) and bending (from belt/gear pull), just like your spine handles body weight and twisting motions. Design it weak, and the whole machine collapses.

### Single Pulley / Gear Mounted — Setup
For a **single keyed pulley or gear** at the centre of a shaft span:
- Transverse force from belt/gear = $F_t$ (tangential) and $F_r$ (radial)
- Resulting bending moment at shaft section
- Torque T = P / ω (from power transmitted)

---

## 1.5 Keys — The Unsung Heroes of Power Transmission

### Formal Definition
A **key** is a machine element (typically rectangular in cross-section) inserted partially into a shaft and partially into a hub (pulley/gear) to **prevent relative rotation** and transmit torque from the shaft to the hub.

### Conceptual Analogy 🎯

> A key is the **"lock" that prevents the gear from spinning freely on the shaft**. Without it, the gear would slip and no power would transfer — like a **door that swings open because the latch is broken**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two types of stresses develop in a key under load:**
> 1. **Shear stress (τ)** — trying to shear off the key at the shaft-hub interface.
> 2. **Compressive/Crushing stress (σ_c)** — trying to crush the key on the side in contact with the hub.
>
> The **length of the key (l)** is designed by checking BOTH and taking the larger value.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Factor of Safety — Refined Concepts

### Factor of Safety for Ductile vs. Brittle Materials

| Material Type | Failure Stress Used in FoS | Why |
|---------------|---------------------------|-----|
| Ductile (steel, aluminum) | $S_y$ (Yield Strength) | Visible plastic deformation prevents sudden fracture |
| Brittle (cast iron, ceramics) | $S_{ut}$ (Ultimate Strength) | Failure is sudden; no yield warning |

### Design Factor (n_d) vs. Actual Factor of Safety (n)

The **design factor (n_d)** is what the engineer *assumes* at the start. The **actual factor of safety (n_actual)** is what the component *ends up* with after choosing standard sizes. KTU problems often ask to verify `n_actual ≥ n_d`.

---

## 2.2 Endurance Limit — Marin Factor Equations in Detail

### 2.2.1 Surface Modification Factor ($k_a$)

For machined or ground surfaces (Shigley's empirical formula):

$$k_a = a \cdot S_{ut}^b$$

| Surface Finish | a (MPa) | b |
|----------------|---------|---|
| Ground | 1.58 | -0.085 |
| Machined or cold-drawn | 4.51 | -0.265 |
| Hot-rolled | 57.7 | -0.718 |
| As-forged | 272 | -0.995 |

### 2.2.2 Size Modification Factor ($k_b$)

For bending/torsion on a **rotating round shaft** (KTU standard):
- For 2.79 mm ≤ d ≤ 51 mm: $k_b = 1.24 \cdot d^{-0.107}$
- For 51 mm < d ≤ 254 mm: $k_b = 1.51 \cdot d^{-0.157}$

For **non-rotating** sections or axially loaded: $k_b = 1$.

### 2.2.3 Loading Factor ($k_c$)

| Loading Type | $k_c$ |
|--------------|-------|
| Pure bending (rotating shaft) | 1.0 |
| Axial (push-pull) | 0.85 |
| Pure torsion | 0.59 (sharper notch sensitivity in torsion) |

### 2.2.4 Temperature Factor ($k_d$)

For T < 450°C: $k_d = 1$ (most KTU problems).
For elevated temperatures (e.g., boilers, turbines), $k_d < 1$ from charts.

### 2.2.5 Reliability Factor ($k_e$)

| Reliability | $k_e$ |
|-------------|-------|
| 50% | 1.000 |
| 90% | 0.897 |
| 99% | 0.814 |
| 99.9% | 0.753 |
| 99.99% | 0.702 |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick-Recall:** 90% reliability is the **default** in most textbook problems. Always write "Assuming 90% reliability" in your answer.

### 2.2.6 Miscellaneous Factor ($k_f$)

Includes effects of:
- Stress concentration (theoretical $K_t$ → fatigue $K_f$)
- Residual stresses
- Surface treatments (shot peening, case hardening, nitriding)

$$K_f = 1 + q \cdot (K_t - 1)$$

where **q = notch sensitivity** (depends on material and notch radius).

---

## 2.3 Combined Bending and Torsion — DE Formula

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Rule:** In rotating shafts, **bending is fully reversed (σ_m = 0)** and **torsion is steady (T_m = T, T_a = 0)** for steady power transmission. For fluctuating power, both have mean and alternating components.

### Von Mises Equivalent Stresses (Distortion Energy Theory)

For **mean** stress component:

$$\sigma_m' = \frac{\sqrt{4(K_f \cdot M_m)^2 + 3(K_{fs} \cdot T_m)^2}}{Z} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{4(K_f M_m)^2 + 3(K_{fs} T_m)^2}$$

For **alternating** stress component:

$$\sigma_a' = \frac{\sqrt{4(K_f \cdot M_a)^2 + 3(K_{fs} \cdot T_a)^2}}{Z} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{4(K_f M_a)^2 + 3(K_{fs} T_a)^2}$$

where:
- $K_f$ = fatigue stress concentration factor for bending
- $K_{fs}$ = fatigue stress concentration factor for torsion
- $Z = \pi d^3 / 32$ = section modulus for solid round shaft

> [!NOTE]
> If the **stress concentration factors are unknown** (as is common in KTU problems), take $K_f = K_{fs} = 1$ and clearly state the assumption.

---

## 2.4 Soderberg Equation for Combined Loading

Substituting the equivalent stresses into the Soderberg criterion:

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{\sigma_a'}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m'}{S_y}$$

Solving for the shaft diameter d:

$$\boxed{d = \left\{\frac{16n}{\pi}\left[\frac{1}{S_e}\sqrt{4(K_f M_a)^2 + 3(K_{fs} T_a)^2} + \frac{1}{S_y}\sqrt{4(K_f M_m)^2 + 3(K_{fs} T_m)^2}\right]\right\}^{1/3}}$$

### Special Case — Static Loading (No Fluctuation)

When $M_a = 0$, $M_m = M$ and $T_a = 0$, $T_m = T$:

$$\boxed{d_{static} = \left[\frac{16n}{\pi \cdot S_y}\sqrt{4M^2 + 3T^2}\right]^{1/3}}$$

This is the **most asked KTU formula** in 14-mark shaft design problems with a single pulley/gear.

### Special Case — ASME-Elliptic for Pure Fluctuating Load

If $M_m = 0$ and $T_m = 0$ (fully reversed):

$$d = \left\{\frac{16n}{\pi S_e}\sqrt{4(K_f M_a)^2 + 3(K_{fs} T_a)^2}\right\}^{1/3}$$

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **master formula table** for KTU 2024 Module 3 problems. Memorize these and you can solve 80% of module questions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Power-Torque | $T = P / \omega$ ; $T = 60P / 2\pi N$ | N·m, W, rad/s, rpm |
| 2 | Tangential Force (Pulley) | $F_t = T / r = 2T / d$ | N |
| 3 | Static Shaft Design | $d = \left[\dfrac{16n}{\pi S_y}\sqrt{4M^2 + 3T^2}\right]^{1/3}$ | mm |
| 4 | Soderberg Shaft | $d = \left\{\dfrac{16n}{\pi}\left[\dfrac{\sqrt{4M_a^2 + 3T_a^2}}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sqrt{4M_m^2 + 3T_m^2}}{S_y}\right]\right\}^{1/3}$ | mm |
| 5 | Endurance Limit (Uncorrected) | $S_e' = 0.5 S_{ut}$ (for steel, $S_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa) | MPa |
| 6 | Marin Equation | $S_e = k_a k_b k_c k_d k_e k_f S_e'$ | MPa |
| 7 | Surface Factor | $k_a = a \cdot S_{ut}^b$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Size Factor (rotating) | $k_b = 1.24 d^{-0.107}$ (d in mm) | dimensionless |
| 9 | Stress Concentration | $K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1)$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Key Crushing Length | $l_c = \dfrac{2W}{\sigma_c \cdot t}$ | mm |
| 11 | Key Shear Length | $l_s = \dfrac{W}{\tau \cdot b}$ | mm |
| 12 | Final Key Length | $l = \max(l_c, l_s) + \text{safety}$ | mm |
| 13 | Transmitted Force (Key) | $W = F_t = T / r_{hub}$ | N |
| 14 | FoS (Static) | $n = S_y / \sigma_{max}$ | dimensionless |
| 15 | FoS (Soderberg) | $\dfrac{1}{n} = \dfrac{\sigma_a'}{S_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m'}{S_y}$ | dimensionless |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Shaft design formulas** are the backbone of every rotating machine: electric motors, gearboxes, turbines, pumps, vehicle axles, propeller shafts, conveyor rollers.
- **Endurance limit corrections** are critical in industries like aerospace (where weight vs. safety is critical) and railways (axle design under millions of cycles).
- **Key design** ensures fail-safe power transmission in milling machines, lathe spindles, automotive transmissions, and even washing machine drums.
- **FoS** is the **universal language of safety engineering** — from civil bridges to nuclear reactors.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Soderberg Shaft Diameter Equation

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Basic Soderberg Criterion for Axial Loading

For a ductile material under combined **mean (steady)** and **alternating (fluctuating)** normal stress:

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{\sigma_a}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{S_y} \quad \text{...(i)}$$

where:
- $\sigma_a$ = alternating stress amplitude
- $\sigma_m$ = mean (steady) stress
- $S_e$ = endurance limit
- $S_y$ = yield strength
- $n$ = factor of safety

This equation represents a **straight line** in the $\sigma_m$-$\sigma_a$ plane joining $(S_y, 0)$ and $(0, S_e)$.

---

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Apply to Bending

For a circular shaft of diameter d, the bending stress is:

$$\sigma = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{32 M}{\pi d^3}$$

For combined steady and alternating bending moments $M_m$ and $M_a$:

$$\sigma_m = \frac{32 M_m}{\pi d^3}, \quad \sigma_a = \frac{32 M_a}{\pi d^3}$$

Substituting into Soderberg:

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{32 M_a}{\pi d^3 S_e} + \frac{32 M_m}{\pi d^3 S_y} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

---

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Apply Stress Concentration Factor ($K_f$)

If the bending section has a notch (e.g., keyway, shoulder), the actual stresses are amplified:

$$\sigma_m = \frac{32 K_f M_m}{\pi d^3}, \quad \sigma_a = \frac{32 K_f M_a}{\pi d^3}$$

So equation (ii) becomes:

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{32 K_f M_a}{\pi d^3 S_e} + \frac{32 K_f M_m}{\pi d^3 S_y} \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

---

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Include Torsion Using Von Mises

Torsion creates a **shear stress** $\tau = 16T/(\pi d^3)$. Using the **Distortion Energy Theory**, an equivalent normal stress for torsion is:

$$\sigma_{eq,torsion} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \tau = \frac{16\sqrt{3} \cdot T}{\pi d^3}$$

**Combined Von Mises equivalent** for bending + torsion (mean component):

$$\sigma_m' = \sqrt{\left(\frac{32 K_f M_m}{\pi d^3}\right)^2 + 3\left(\frac{16 K_{fs} T_m}{\pi d^3}\right)^2}$$

Factor out $\dfrac{16}{\pi d^3}$:

$$\sigma_m' = \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{4(K_f M_m)^2 + 3(K_{fs} T_m)^2} \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

Similarly for the alternating component:

$$\sigma_a' = \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{4(K_f M_a)^2 + 3(K_{fs} T_a)^2} \quad \text{...(v)}$$

> Note: $32 = 2 \times 16$, so $(32)^2 = 4 \times (16)^2$, hence the **4** under the square root in eq. (iv).

---

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Substitute into Soderberg

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{\sigma_a'}{S_e} + \frac{\sigma_m'}{S_y}$$

$$\frac{1}{n} = \frac{1}{S_e} \cdot \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{4(K_f M_a)^2 + 3(K_{fs} T_a)^2} + \frac{1}{S_y} \cdot \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{4(K_f M_m)^2 + 3(K_{fs} T_m)^2}$$

---

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Solve for $d^3$

Multiply both sides by $d^3$:

$$\frac{d^3}{n} = \frac{16}{\pi}\left[\frac{\sqrt{4(K_f M_a)^2 + 3(K_{fs} T_a)^2}}{S_e} + \frac{\sqrt{4(K_f M_m)^2 + 3(K_{fs} T_m)^2}}{S_y}\right]$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{d^3 = \frac{16n}{\pi}\left[\frac{\sqrt{4(K_f M_a)^2 + 3(K_{fs} T_a)^2}}{S_e} + \frac{\sqrt{4(K_f M_m)^2 + 3(K_{fs} T_m)^2}}{S_y}\right]}$$

Taking the cube root:

$$\boxed{d = \left\{\frac{16n}{\pi}\left[\frac{\sqrt{4(K_f M_a)^2 + 3(K_{fs} T_a)^2}}{S_e} + \frac{\sqrt{4(K_f M_m)^2 + 3(K_{fs} T_m)^2}}{S_y}\right]\right\}^{1/3}}$$

This is the **Soderberg equation for combined fluctuating bending and torsion** — the KTU board examiner's favourite.

---

## 3.2 Special Case Derivations

### 3.2.1 Static Loading (M_a = T_a = 0, M_m = M, T_m = T)

Setting $M_a = T_a = 0$ in the Soderberg equation:

$$d = \left\{\frac{16n}{\pi}\left[0 + \frac{\sqrt{4(K_f M)^2 + 3(K_{fs} T)^2}}{S_y}\right]\right\}^{1/3}$$

Ignoring stress concentrations ($K_f = K_{fs} = 1$):

$$\boxed{d = \left[\frac{16n}{\pi S_y}\sqrt{4M^2 + 3T^2}\right]^{1/3}}$$

### 3.2.2 Pure Torsion (M = 0)

$$\boxed{d = \left[\frac{16n}{\pi S_y}\sqrt{3} \cdot T\right]^{1/3} = \left[\frac{16nT}{\pi S_y}\sqrt{3}\right]^{1/3}}$$

This can also be written using the **torsional yield strength** $S_{sy} = 0.577 S_y$:

$$d = \left[\frac{16nT}{\pi S_{sy}}\right]^{1/3}$$

### 3.2.3 Pure Bending (T = 0)

$$\boxed{d = \left[\frac{16n}{\pi S_y}\sqrt{4M^2}\right]^{1/3} = \left[\frac{32nM}{\pi S_y}\right]^{1/3} = \left[\frac{16nM \cdot 2}{\pi S_y}\right]^{1/3}}$$

Or directly: $d = [32nM/(\pi S_y)]^{1/3}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Key Length

### 3.3.1 Transmitted Force on Key

Let the torque on the shaft be T and the hub radius (= shaft radius + key height/2) be approximately r = d/2 for design purposes. The tangential force pushing on the key is:

$$W = F_t = \frac{T}{r} = \frac{2T}{d}$$

This force acts at the top of the key and tries to:
1. **Shear** the key along the vertical plane of width $b$ and length $l$.
2. **Crush** the key on the side of height $t/2$ (where t = key thickness).

### 3.3.2 Shear Failure Analysis

The shear area is $A_s = b \times l$. Shear stress:

$$\tau = \frac{W}{A_s} = \frac{W}{b \cdot l} \le S_{sy,key}$$

Rearranging for length:

$$l_s = \frac{W}{b \cdot \tau_{allow}} = \frac{W}{b \cdot S_{sy,key}}$$

### 3.3.3 Crushing Failure Analysis

The crushing (compressive) area is $A_c = (t/2) \times l$. Crushing stress:

$$\sigma_c = \frac{W}{A_c} = \frac{W}{(t/2) \cdot l} \le S_{y,key}$$

Rearranging for length:

$$l_c = \frac{2W}{t \cdot \sigma_{c,allow}}$$

### 3.3.4 Final Key Length

The key must satisfy **both** criteria. Therefore:

$$\boxed{l = \max(l_s, l_c)}$$

Standard practical addition: Use $l_{design} = l + 5$ to $10$ mm for end clearances, and the **hub length** must be ≥ $l_{design}$.

### 3.3.5 Standard Key Proportions (As Per IS 2048 / Shigley Table)

| Shaft Diameter d (mm) | Key Width b (mm) | Rectangular Height h (mm) | Square Side (mm) |
|------------------------|------------------|----------------------------|--------------------|
| 10 – 12 | 4 | 4 | 4 |
| 17 – 22 | 6 | 6 | 6 |
| 24 – 30 | 8 | 7 | 8 |
| 32 – 38 | 10 | 8 | 10 |
| 40 – 44 | 12 | 8 | 12 |
| 50 – 58 | 16 | 10 | 16 |
| 60 – 65 | 18 | 11 | 18 |
| 70 – 78 | 20 | 12 | 20 |
| 80 – 92 | 24 | 14 | 24 |
| 95 – 110 | 28 | 16 | 28 |

> [!NOTE]
> The general rule: **b ≈ d/4** for square keys, and **b ≈ d/4, h ≈ 7d/32** for rectangular keys.

---

## 3.4 Verification of FoS Equation (Static)

Given: Shaft diameter d, material with $S_y$, applied bending moment M and torque T.

### Step-by-step Verification Procedure

1. **Compute actual bending stress:**
$$\sigma_b = \frac{32M}{\pi d^3}$$

2. **Compute actual shear (torsion) stress:**
$$\tau = \frac{16T}{\pi d^3}$$

3. **Apply Von Mises equivalent stress:**
$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 3\tau^2} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{4M^2 + 3T^2}$$

4. **Calculate actual factor of safety:**
$$\boxed{n_{actual} = \frac{S_y}{\sigma_{VM}} = \frac{\pi d^3 S_y}{16\sqrt{4M^2 + 3T^2}}}$$

5. **Check:** $n_{actual} \ge n_{design}$? If yes, design is safe.

---

## 3.5 Comprehensive KTU Analytical Matrix — Solving Strategy for Shaft + Key Problems

| Step | Action | Why |
|------|--------|-----|
| 1 | Read the problem, identify the **power (P)**, **speed (N)**, and **forces on the pulley/gear**. | These define torque and bending. |
| 2 | Compute **torque T = 60P/(2πN)**. | Shaft transmits torque. |
| 3 | Compute **tangential force F_t = T/r** and **radial force F_r = F_t tan(α)** (for gear) or **F_r = F_t sin(θ)** (for belt). | These create bending moment. |
| 4 | Compute **bending moment M** at the critical shaft section (usually under the pulley). | Need M for design. |
| 5 | Decide **failure criterion** (Static, Soderberg, Goodman) based on problem statement. | Determines which formula. |
| 6 | Compute **d** using the appropriate formula. | Diameter. |
| 7 | **Standardize** d to the next higher preferred size (e.g., 25, 30, 35 mm). | Practical manufacture. |
| 8 | For the key: pick **b × h** from standard table based on d. | Use IS standard. |
| 9 | Compute **W = 2T/d**, then $l_s$ and $l_c$ using crushing/shear strengths of key material. | Key length. |
| 10 | Take $l = \max(l_s, l_c) + 5$ mm, and verify $l < L_{hub}$. | Practical clearance. |
| 11 | State **actual FoS** by reversing the calculation. | Examiner's verification. |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Factor of Safety. Why is it necessary in machine design? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Factor of Safety (n)** is the ratio of the maximum stress a material can withstand (failure stress) to the working stress it is actually subjected to in service.

$$n = \frac{\text{Failure Stress } (S_f)}{\text{Working Stress } (S_w)}$$

For ductile materials, $S_f = S_y$ (yield strength); for brittle, $S_f = S_{ut}$ (ultimate strength).

**Necessity:**
1. To account for **uncertainties in load estimation**.
2. To compensate for **variations in material properties** (batch-to-batch).
3. To guard against **manufacturing defects** (blowholes, inclusions).
4. To handle **unfavourable operating conditions** (corrosion, wear, temperature).
5. To provide a **margin of safety** in case of accidental overload.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Endurance Limit and Fatigue Strength. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Endurance Limit (S_e) | Fatigue Strength (S_f) |
|--------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **Definition** | Maximum fully reversed stress that material can withstand for **infinite cycles** (10⁶ to 10⁷) without failure | Maximum stress that material can withstand for a **specified number of cycles** (typically 10⁸) without failure |
| **Applicability** | Only for **ferrous metals** like steel | Used for **non-ferrous metals** (aluminum, copper) that have no true endurance limit |
| **S-N curve** | Becomes **horizontal** at S_e | S-N curve continues to slope down |
| **Magnitude (steel)** | $S_e \approx 0.5 S_{ut}$ | Varies with cycle number |

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**OR**

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

Both questions below are independent choices. Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Design of Shaft with Single Pulley

A transmission shaft carries a **single pulley** at its centre, mounted between two bearings 1.2 m apart. The pulley of diameter **400 mm** receives **10 kW power at 200 rpm** from a motor via a flat belt. The belt tensions are $F_1 = 2F_2$ with an angle of wrap of 180° on the smaller pulley side. The belt tensions act vertically downward. The shaft is made of **40C8 steel** with $S_{ut} = 540$ MPa, $S_y = 380$ MPa, and the design factor of safety is **n = 3**. The shaft is finished by **machining**. Assume steady loading. Design the shaft diameter and check the actual factor of safety.

**Sub-Parts:**
- (a) Compute the shaft diameter using a suitable design formula. **(7 marks)**
