- (b) Recompute the shaft diameter using the ASME-Elliptic criterion for fluctuating load where the bending moment is fully reversed. **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often forget the **tangential force contributes to bending moment** when the belt tensions are unequal. Take $F_t = F_1 + F_2$ (downward resultant) and use $F_t$ to find $M$. Also, the **angle of wrap does not change the basic force calculation** — it only affects belt design.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

**Given Data:**
- Power P = 10 kW = 10,000 W
- Speed N = 200 rpm
- Pulley diameter D = 400 mm → r = 200 mm = 0.2 m
- Belt tensions $F_1 = 2F_2$, angle of wrap = 180° (π rad)
- Distance between bearings L = 1.2 m, pulley at center → Span from each bearing = 0.6 m
- Material: 40C8, $S_{ut} = 540$ MPa, $S_y = 380$ MPa
- n = 3, machined surface, steady loading

**Step 1: Compute Torque T**

$$T = \frac{60 \cdot P}{2\pi N} = \frac{60 \times 10{,}000}{2\pi \times 200} = \frac{600{,}000}{1256.64} = 477.46 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2: Compute Belt Tensions**

Power transmitted by belt:
$$P = (F_1 - F_2) \cdot v$$

Belt velocity:
$$v = \frac{\pi D N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.4 \times 200}{60} = 4.189 \text{ m/s}$$

$$10{,}000 = (F_1 - F_2) \times 4.189$$

$$F_1 - F_2 = 2387.3 \text{ N}$$

Using $F_1 = 2F_2$:
$$2F_2 - F_2 = 2387.3 \Rightarrow F_2 = 2387.3 \text{ N} = 2.387 \text{ kN}$$
$$F_1 = 4774.6 \text{ N} = 4.775 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Compute Resultant Vertical Force on Shaft**

Total downward force from belt = $F_1 + F_2 = 7161.9$ N

(Neglecting the centrifugal belt tension since not given — KTU standard assumption.)

**Step 4: Compute Bending Moment**

For a simply supported beam with point load at the center:
$$M = \frac{F_{total} \cdot L}{4} = \frac{7161.9 \times 1.2}{4} = 2148.6 \text{ N·m} = 2.1486 \text{ kN·m}$$

Converting to N·mm: $M = 2{,}148{,}600$ N·mm

---

#### (a) Shaft Diameter using Static Formula **(7 marks)**

Using the **Static Shaft Design Formula** (steady loading, no fatigue):

$$d = \left[\frac{16n}{\pi S_y}\sqrt{4M^2 + 3T^2}\right]^{1/3}$$

Convert T to N·mm: $T = 477.46 \times 10^3$ N·mm = 477,460 N·mm

Compute the term under square root:

$$4M^2 = 4 \times (2{,}148{,}600)^2 = 4 \times 4.6168 \times 10^{12} = 1.8467 \times 10^{13}$$

$$3T^2 = 3 \times (477{,}460)^2 = 3 \times 2.2797 \times 10^{11} = 6.8392 \times 10^{11}$$

$$\sqrt{4M^2 + 3T^2} = \sqrt{1.8467 \times 10^{13} + 6.839 \times 10^{11}} = \sqrt{1.9151 \times 10^{13}}$$

$$= 4.3762 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}$$

Now compute $d^3$:

$$d^3 = \frac{16 \times 3}{\pi \times 380} \times 4.3762 \times 10^6$$

$$d^3 = \frac{48}{1193.81} \times 4.3762 \times 10^6 = 0.04021 \times 4.3762 \times 10^6$$

$$d^3 = 175{,}961 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$d = (175{,}961)^{1/3} = 56.04 \text{ mm}$$

**Standardize to next higher size: d = 60 mm (or 56 mm) ✅**

#### (b) Shaft Diameter using ASME-Elliptic for Fluctuating Bending **(7 marks)**

Since the pulley rotates with the shaft, the **bending moment is fully reversed**:
- $M_a = 2{,}148{,}600$ N·mm (alternating)
- $M_m = 0$ (mean)
- $T_m = 477{,}460$ N·mm (mean, steady torque)
- $T_a = 0$ (alternating)

Ignore stress concentration: $K_f = K_{fs} = 1$.

**Step 1: Compute Endurance Limit using Marin Equation**

For steel with $S_{ut} = 540$ MPa < 1400 MPa:
$$S_e' = 0.5 \times 540 = 270 \text{ MPa}$$

Surface factor (machined): $a = 4.51, b = -0.265$
$$k_a = 4.51 \times (540)^{-0.265} = 4.51 \times 0.1717 = 0.774$$

Size factor (initial guess d ≈ 60 mm, between 51 and 254 mm):
$$k_b = 1.51 \times d^{-0.157} = 1.51 \times (60)^{-0.157} = 1.51 \times 0.4767 = 0.720$$

Loading factor (bending): $k_c = 1.0$
Temperature factor: $k_d = 1.0$
Reliability factor (90%): $k_e = 0.897$
Miscellaneous: $k_f = 1.0$

$$S_e = 0.774 \times 0.720 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.897 \times 1.0 \times 270$$

$$S_e = 0.500 \times 270 = 135 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Apply ASME-Elliptic Equation for Pure Fluctuating Load**

For rotating shaft (bending fully reversed, torsion steady):
$$d = \left\{\frac{16n}{\pi}\left[\frac{\sqrt{4M_a^2}}{S_e} + \frac{\sqrt{3 T_m^2}}{S_y}\right]\right\}^{1/3}$$

$$= \left\{\frac{16 \times 3}{\pi}\left[\frac{2 \times 2{,}148{,}600}{135} + \frac{\sqrt{3} \times 477{,}460}{380}\right]\right\}^{1/3}$$

Compute each term:
- $\frac{2M_a}{S_e} = \frac{2 \times 2{,}148{,}600}{135} = 31{,}831$ mm³
- $\frac{\sqrt{3} T_m}{S_y} = \frac{1.732 \times 477{,}460}{380} = 2{,}176$ mm³

Sum = 31,831 + 2,176 = 34,007 mm³

$$d^3 = \frac{48}{\pi} \times 34{,}007 = 15.278 \times 34{,}007 = 519{,}628 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$d = (519{,}628)^{1/3} = 80.55 \text{ mm}$$

**Standardize: d = 85 mm ✅** (Notice the fatigue criterion gives a larger diameter than static — always the case!)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** Students forget to compute the **Marin factors** before applying fatigue criteria, leading to severe mark deduction. The **surface factor $k_a$** alone can reduce $S_e$ by 20-40% — you MUST include it.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Design of Shaft with Key — Alternative Choice

A **mild steel shaft** transmits **20 kW at 250 rpm**. A **gear of pitch circle diameter 250 mm** is keyed to the shaft. The gear has **20° full-depth involute teeth** and the shaft is supported by two bearings 800 mm apart, with the gear located 300 mm from the left bearing. Assume a **factor of safety of 3** and the design is based on the **maximum shear stress theory**. Material: $S_y = 350$ MPa.

**Sub-Parts:**
- (a) Design the shaft diameter. **(7 marks)**
- (b) Design a suitable **square key** for the gear-shaft connection. **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #3:** For gears, students forget that the **radial force = tangential force × tan(20°) ≈ 0.364 × F_t**. Missing this force leads to incorrect bending moment calculations and shaft diameter errors.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

**Given Data:**
- P = 20 kW = 20,000 W
- N = 250 rpm
- Gear PCD = 250 mm → r = 125 mm = 0.125 m
- Pressure angle φ = 20°
- Span L = 800 mm; gear at 300 mm from left bearing
- Material: $S_y = 350$ MPa, n = 3, Maximum Shear Stress Theory

**Step 1: Torque T**

$$T = \frac{60 \times 20{,}000}{2\pi \times 250} = \frac{1{,}200{,}000}{1570.8} = 763.94 \text{ N·m} = 763{,}940 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2: Gear Forces**

Tangential force on gear:
$$F_t = \frac{T}{r} = \frac{763{,}940}{125} = 6111.5 \text{ N}$$

Radial (separating) force on gear:
$$F_r = F_t \tan(20°) = 6111.5 \times 0.3640 = 2224.6 \text{ N}$$

(For a more accurate value: $\tan(20°) = 0.36397$.)

Resultant force on shaft:
$$F = \sqrt{F_t^2 + F_r^2} = \sqrt{6111.5^2 + 2224.6^2} = \sqrt{37.35 \times 10^6 + 4.95 \times 10^6} = \sqrt{42.30 \times 10^6} = 6504 \text{ N}$$

---

#### (a) Shaft Diameter Design **(7 marks)**

**Bending Moment Calculation:**
The gear is 300 mm from left bearing, 500 mm from right bearing (800 − 300).
Reactions (taking moments about right bearing, R_L × 0.8 = F × 0.5):

$$R_R = \frac{F \times 0.3}{0.8} = \frac{6504 \times 0.3}{0.8} = 2439 \text{ N}$$

$$R_L = F - R_R = 6504 - 2439 = 4065 \text{ N}$$

Maximum bending moment at gear location:
$$M = R_L \times 0.3 = 4065 \times 300 = 1{,}219{,}500 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Using Maximum Shear Stress Theory (MSST) for static loading:**

The MSST (or Tresca) criterion: $\sigma_{max,shear} = S_{sy} = 0.5 S_y$, so the equivalent maximum shear stress is:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\sqrt{M^2 + T^2} = \frac{S_y}{2n}$$

Solving for $d^3$:

$$d^3 = \frac{32n}{\pi S_y}\sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$$

Compute $\sqrt{M^2 + T^2}$:

$$M^2 = (1{,}219{,}500)^2 = 1.4872 \times 10^{12}$$
$$T^2 = (763{,}940)^2 = 5.8353 \times 10^{11}$$
$$\sqrt{M^2 + T^2} = \sqrt{2.0707 \times 10^{12}} = 1.4390 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

$$d^3 = \frac{32 \times 3}{\pi \times 350} \times 1.4390 \times 10^6 = \frac{96}{1099.56} \times 1.4390 \times 10^6$$

$$d^3 = 0.0873 \times 1.4390 \times 10^6 = 125{,}635 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$d = (125{,}635)^{1/3} = 50.10 \text{ mm}$$

**Standardize: d = 50 mm or 55 mm ✅** (use 55 mm for safety)

**Actual Factor of Safety Check:**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{16}{\pi (55)^3} \times 1.4390 \times 10^6 = \frac{16}{523{,}598} \times 1.4390 \times 10^6 = 43.97 \text{ MPa}$$

$$n_{actual} = \frac{S_y / 2}{\tau_{max}} = \frac{175}{43.97} = 3.98 > 3 \checkmark$$

Design is safe.

---

#### (b) Square Key Design **(7 marks)**

**Step 1: Select Key Size from Standard Table**
For shaft diameter d = 50 mm, standard square key size: **b = h = 16 mm** (using $b \approx d/4$).

**Step 2: Compute Force on Key**

The tangential force at the pitch radius is transmitted through the key. For key design, the force is taken at the **shaft surface** (approximately):
$$W = F_t = \frac{T}{r_{shaft}} = \frac{763{,}940}{25} = 30{,}558 \text{ N}$$

(Using r = d/2 = 25 mm for design — KTU convention.)

**Step 3: Material Properties of Key (Assume same as shaft, mild steel)**

- $S_{sy,key} = 0.5 S_y = 0.5 \times 350 = 175$ MPa (allowable shear)
- $\sigma_{c,key} = S_y = 350$ MPa (allowable crushing — conservative)

**Step 4: Length by Shear**

$$l_s = \frac{W}{b \cdot \tau_{allow}} = \frac{30{,}558}{16 \times 175} = \frac{30{,}558}{2800} = 10.91 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Length by Crushing**

$$l_c = \frac{2W}{h \cdot \sigma_{c,allow}} = \frac{2 \times 30{,}558}{16 \times 350} = \frac{61{,}116}{5600} = 10.91 \text{ mm}$$

(For a square key, b = h, and l_s = l_c! This is an elegant mathematical coincidence.)

**Step 6: Final Key Length**

$$l = \max(10.91, 10.91) + 5 \text{ mm} = 15.91 \text{ mm} \approx 16 \text{ mm}$$

Standardize to **l = 20 mm** for practical manufacturing.

**Verification:** Hub length ≥ 20 mm (typically hub length is 1.5 × d = 75 mm, so fine ✅).

**Selected Key: Square, 16 × 16 × 20 mm**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #4:** Students frequently use the **pitch radius** instead of the **shaft radius** for computing key force. The pitch radius is correct for gear-tooth force analysis, but the **shaft radius** is correct for the moment-arm of the key force on the shaft. The KTU convention (and Shigley) is to use the **mean radius of the key (≈ shaft radius + h/4)** or simply **d/2** for simplicity. Mixing these gives wrong key lengths.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examination Mark Distribution Pattern (PBIET404, Module 3)

| Question Type | Marks | Pattern |
|---------------|-------|---------|
| Direct definition (FoS / Endurance Limit) | 3 | Short answer |
| Formula derivation or state | 3 | Direct recall |
| Numerical shaft design | 14 | Internal choice, sub-parts (a) 7 + (b) 7 |
| Key design with given shaft | 14 | Internal choice, sub-parts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Module 3 typically contributes **1 Part-A question (3 marks)** + **1 Part-B question (14 marks)** = **17 marks** of the 100-mark End Semester Exam. Always prepare both static and fluctuating load formulas.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📚

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read this 10 minutes before the exam)**

### ✅ Factor of Safety
- [ ] Definition: $n = S_f / S_w$
- [ ] For ductile → $S_f = S_y$; for brittle → $S_f = S_{ut}$
- [ ] Typical values: 1.5–4 for machine components
- [ ] Design FoS vs. Actual FoS distinction is critical

### ✅ Fatigue Failure
- [ ] Three stages: Crack Initiation → Crack Propagation → Sudden Fracture
- [ ] Failure occurs at stresses BELOW yield — so static FoS is NOT enough
- [ ] Major criteria: Soderberg, Goodman, Gerber, ASME-Elliptic
- [ ] Soderberg is **most conservative**; Gerber is **most accurate** for ductile

### ✅ Endurance Limit
- [ ] $S_e' = 0.5 S_{ut}$ for $S_{ut} \le 1400$ MPa
- [ ] Marin equation: 6 correction factors (a, b, c, d, e, f)
- [ ] Surface factor $k_a$ depends on **machining method**
- [ ] Size factor $k_b$ depends on **diameter** — larger d → smaller $k_b$
- [ ] Default reliability: **90% → $k_e = 0.897$**

### ✅ Shaft Design
- [ ] **Static formula:** $d = \left[\dfrac{16n}{\pi S_y}\sqrt{4M^2 + 3T^2}\right]^{1/3}$ ⭐ (Most important)
- [ ] **Soderberg formula:** Use 4 inside square root for bending, 3 for torsion
- [ ] Always **standardize d** to next higher preferred size
- [ ] Compute torque from P = 2πNT/60
- [ ] Bending moment from belt or gear forces

### ✅ Single Pulley / Gear on Shaft
- [ ] Belt: $F_{total} = F_1 + F_2$ (downward on shaft)
- [ ] Gear: $F_t = T/r$, $F_r = F_t \tan(20°)$ for 20° involute
- [ ] For gear, $F = \sqrt{F_t^2 + F_r^2}$ is the resultant bending force
- [ ] Locate gear/pulley correctly on the span for M calculation

### ✅ Key Design
- [ ] **Shear length:** $l_s = W / (b \cdot \tau)$
- [ ] **Crushing length:** $l_c = 2W / (t \cdot \sigma_c)$
- [ ] **Final:** $l = \max(l_s, l_c) + 5$ mm clearance
- [ ] Standard size: $b \approx d/4$; rectangular $h = 7d/32$; square $h = b$
- [ ] For **square key**: $l_s = l_c$ (always)
- [ ] Hub length ≥ Key length + clearance

### ✅ KTU Numerical Approach
- [ ] Always **write the formula first** before substituting
- [ ] **Units consistency:** N, mm, MPa throughout
- [ ] Show step-by-step substitution
- [ ] **State the final standardized value** — not the calculated one
- [ ] **Verify** $n_{actual} \ge n_{design}$ at the end

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Most-Common Mistake:** Forgetting to take the **cube root** in shaft diameter formula. Students write $d^3$ as the final answer. Always write the cube root step explicitly: "Taking cube root, $d = 60$ mm."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Day Mantra:** *"Soderberg for fatigue, DE theory for combined, Marin for endurance, shear + crush for key."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBIET404 - THEORY OF MACHINES AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Types # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Introduction to "Types" in Module 4 — Belt, Rope, and Chain Drives In the KTU 2024 scheme syllabus for **PBIET404 – Theory of Machines and Design**, Module 4 broadly focuses on **Friction Drives, Belt Drives, and Chain Drives** — the three principal categories of flexible power-transmission elements used between rotating shafts. The "Types" classification refers to the systematic categorization of these transmission elements based on geometry, load-carrying mechanism, and physical configuration. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 4 Scope:** Classification of belt drives, types of belts (flat, V-belt, toothed/timing, ribbed), types of pulleys, types of rope drives, and types of chain drives (roller, silent/inverted-tooth). ### Formal Academic Definition A **belt drive** is a flexible friction-based power transmission system that uses an endless flexible element (belt) running over two or more pulleys to transmit power and motion between shafts. The driving force is transferred by virtue of the **frictional grip** between the belt and the pulley surfaces. A **chain drive** is a positive (no-slip) power transmission system where motion and power are transmitted through meshing of links of a chain with the teeth of sprockets mounted on parallel shafts. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a **bicycle** — the pedaling motion you give to the front sprocket is transmitted to the rear wheel via a **chain** (positive drive), while the **fan belt in a car engine** transmits rotary motion from the crankshaft pulley to the alternator/AC compressor pulley through **frictional grip** (friction drive). Both transmit power across distance, but one is forgiving (belt) and the other is exact (chain). - **Flat Belt Drive** → Like pulling a friend with a rope — relies purely on friction. - **V-Belt Drive** → Like wedging a doorstop under a door — uses the **wedging action** of the V-groove to multiply frictional grip. - **Chain Drive** → Like two gears connected by a roller chain — positive engagement, no slip. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction:** Belt drives rely on **friction (slip possible)**, while chain drives rely on **positive meshing (no slip)**. This single fact determines their selection, applications, and limitations. ### Why "Types" Matter in Engineering Design The classification into "types" is not merely descriptive — it directly impacts: - **Power transmission capacity** (kW) - **Speed ratio range** available - **Distance between shafts** (center distance) - **Operating environment** (temperature, moisture, dust) - **Maintenance cost** and **service life** - **Direction of rotation** (open belt, crossed belt, etc.)
**Key Constants/Metrics Used:** - Coefficient of friction: **μ = 0.25 – 0.5** (rubber on cast iron, typical) - Density of belt material: **ρ = 1000 – 1500 kg/m³** - Standard V-belt groove angle: **40°** (with belt angle of **38°**) - Standard pitch of roller chain: **9.525 mm (3/8″)** to **76.2 mm (3″)** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Master Classification — The "Types" Tree ``` Power Transmission (Flexible Element) ├── 1. Friction Drives (Belt & Rope Drives) │ ├── A. Belt Drives │ │ ├── (i) Flat Belt │ │ ├── (ii) V-Belt │ │ ├── (iii) Toothed / Timing Belt │ │ ├── (iv) Ribbed / Poly-V Belt │ │ └── (v) Round Belt │ ├── B. Rope Drives │ │ ├── (i) Hemp / Cotton Rope │ │ └── (ii) Wire Rope │ └── C. Pulley Types │ ├── (i) Solid Pulley │ ├── (ii) Split Pulley │ └── (iii) Built-up Pulley └── 2. Positive Drives (Chain Drives) ├── A. Roller Chain ├── B. Silent / Inverted-Tooth Chain ├── C. Block Chain └── D. Detachable Chain ``` ## 2.2 Detailed Functional Breakdown — Each Type ### 2.2.1 Flat Belt Drive - **Construction:** Rectangular cross-section belt running on flat-faced pulleys. - **Mechanism:** Power transmitted by **frictional contact**; capstan (Euler-Eytelwein) equation governs the tension ratio. - **Why/How:** Low cost, simple, suited for long center distances (>15 m). Cannot transmit high power at small pulley diameters. - **Application:** Conveyor systems, agricultural machinery, low-power line shafts. ### 2.2.2 V-Belt Drive - **Construction:** Trapezoidal cross-section belt running in V-grooved sheaves. - **Mechanism:** The wedge action of the V-groove **multiplies the normal force**, hence frictional grip. Effective coefficient $\mu' = \dfrac{\mu}{\sin(\alpha/2)}$ where $\alpha$ is the groove angle. - **Why/How:** Greater power capacity, compact design, self-aligning due to groove shape. - **Application:** Automobiles, machine tools, pumps, compressors (most common industrial drive). ### 2.2.3 Toothed / Timing Belt Drive - **Construction:** Flat belt with molded teeth on inner surface, meshing with pulleys having matching teeth. - **Mechanism:** **Positive (synchronous) drive** — no slip, constant velocity ratio. - **Why/How:** Combines the no-slip accuracy of chain with the quietness of belt. - **Application:** Automotive camshaft drives, CNC machines, printers, robotics. ### 2.2.4 Ribbed / Poly-V Belt Drive - **Construction:** Flat back with longitudinal V-shaped ribs. - **Mechanism:** Hybrid — small bending radius, high speed capability, multi-rib load sharing. - **Why/How:** Used where V-belts fail due to bending fatigue at very small pulleys. - **Application:** Automotive serpentine drives, high-speed applications. ### 2.2.5 Round Belt Drive - **Cross-section:** Circular; small flexible drives. - **Application:** Light-duty, low-power applications like small household appliances, sewing machines. ### 2.2.6 Rope Drives - **Construction:** Multiple ropes running in grooves of specially grooved pulleys. - **Mechanism:** Multi-rope friction drive; can transmit very high power over long distances. - **Application:** Hoists, mine winders, aerial ropeways, elevators. ### 2.2.7 Chain Drives — Roller Chain - **Construction:** Link plates, pins, bushings, and rollers; sprockets with cut teeth. - **Mechanism:** Positive mesh — roller engages sprocket tooth. - **Why/How:** Transmits high power with constant velocity ratio over moderate center distances (up to ~3–4 m); lower noise than gear drives. - **Application:** Motorcycles, bicycles, conveyors, industrial machinery. ### 2.2.8 Silent / Inverted-Tooth Chain - **Construction:** H-shaped links with straight teeth engaging sprocket teeth. - **Mechanism:** Positive engagement with **minimal noise** and **no chordal action** (compared to roller chain). - **Application:** Automotive timing drives (older Mercedes, BMW), high-speed machinery. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Trade-off:** Higher accuracy (toothed/timing belt, silent chain) comes at the cost of higher cost, higher noise (silent chain), and stricter alignment requirements. Lower accuracy (flat belt) is cheaper and absorbs shock better. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Formula** | **Description** | **Variables & Units** | **Applicable Type** | |---|---|---|---| | $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu\theta}$ | Eytelwein's capstan equation (flat belt) | $T_1$ = tight side tension (N); $T_2$ = slack side tension (N); $\mu$ = coefficient of friction; $\theta$ = angle of wrap (radians) | Flat belt | | $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu\theta / \sin(\alpha/2)}$ | V-belt capstan equation | $\alpha$ = groove angle (radians) | V-belt | | $\dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1 + T_2} = \mu \cot(\alpha/2)$ | V-belt net driving force ratio (approx) | Same as above | V-belt | | $v = \dfrac{\pi d N}{60}$ | Belt linear speed | $v$ (m/s); $d$ = pulley diameter (m); $N$ = speed (rpm) | All belt drives | | $i = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{d_2}{d_1}$ | Velocity ratio (open belt) | $N_1, N_2$ = speeds of driver and driven (rpm) | All belt drives | | $i = \dfrac{d_2}{d_1}$ | Velocity ratio (crossed belt) | — | Crossed belt | | $L = \dfrac{\pi}{2}(d_1 + d_2) + 2C + \dfrac{(d_2 - d_1)^2}{4C}$ | Length of open belt | $L$ (m); $C$ = center distance (m) | Flat belt (open) | | $L = \dfrac{\pi}{2}(d_1 + d_2) + 2C + \dfrac{(d_1 + d_2)^2}{4C}$ | Length of crossed belt | Same as above | Crossed belt | | $P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v$ | Power transmitted | $P$ (W); $v$ (m/s) | All belt drives | | $\sigma_{max} = T_1 / A$ | Maximum stress in belt | $A$ = cross-sectional area (m²) | All belts | | $v_{mes} = \dfrac{p \cdot N \cdot z}{60}$ | Chain speed (pitch-line speed) | $p$ = chain pitch (m); $z$ = teeth on sprocket | All chain drives | | $i = \dfrac{z_2}{z_1}$ | Chain drive velocity ratio | $z_1, z_2$ = teeth on driver/driven sprockets | All chain drives | | $L_p = \dfrac{2C}{p} + \dfrac{z_1 + z_2}{2} + \dfrac{(z_2 - z_1)^2 \cdot p}{4\pi^2 C}$ | Chain length (number of pitches) | $L_p$ = pitches; $C$ = center distance (m) | Chain drives | | $F_{centrifugal} = m v^2$ | Centrifugal tension in belt | $m$ = belt mass per unit length (kg/m) | All belts | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Tip:** For the V-belt, the effective coefficient becomes $\mu' = \dfrac{\mu}{\sin(\alpha/2)}$ — substituting $\alpha = 40°$ gives $\mu' \approx 2.92 \mu$, which is why V-belts transmit nearly 3× more power than flat belts of equivalent normal force. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive Industry:** The modern car has **only one belt (serpentine Poly-V)** doing the job of 4–5 V-belts of older cars — a direct evolution through "types". - **Industrial Robotics:** Timing belts offer zero-backlash positioning, making them preferred over chain for pick-and-place arms. - **Bicycles & Motorcycles:** Chain drives (specifically roller chains) are the universal standard. - **Mining & Heavy Industry:** Rope drives transmit hundreds of kW over kilometers.
> [!NOTE] > **Why the "Type" Choice Matters:** A wrong selection can cause premature failure. Example — using a flat belt on a high-power, small-pulley application results in slip and burned belts. KTU questions frequently test this **selection rationale**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Centrifugal Tension in a Belt The centrifugal tension $T_C$ develops because the belt moves in a circular path over the pulley, and the radial acceleration produces an outward pull. **Step 1 — Consider a small element of belt of angular span $d\theta$ moving at linear speed $v$ on a pulley of radius $R$.** **Step 2 — Mass of the element:** $$dm = m \cdot R \cdot d\theta$$ where $m$ is the mass per unit length of the belt (kg/m). **Step 3 — Centripetal force required to keep the element in circular motion:** $$dF_c = dm \cdot \frac{v^2}{R} = m \cdot R \cdot d\theta \cdot \frac{v^2}{R} = m v^2 \, d\theta$$ **Step 4 — The belt tension $T_C$ resolves into two equal and opposite components pulling the element outward. Each component contributes $T_C \sin(d\theta/2)$.** For small $d\theta$: $$\sin(d\theta/2) \approx d\theta/2$$ $$2T_C \cdot \frac{d\theta}{2} = dF_c$$ $$T_C \, d\theta = m v^2 \, d\theta$$ $$T_C = m v^2$$ **Step 5 — Result (boxed for KTU):** $$\boxed{T_C = m v^2}$$ This tension is **independent of the radius of the pulley** and depends only on the linear speed of the belt and its mass per unit length. It reduces the net effective driving tension by the same amount on both the tight and slack sides. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Ratio of Tensions for Flat Belt (Eytelwein's Equation) **Step 1 — Consider a small element subtending angle $d\theta$ at the pulley center.** Let $T$ be the tension on one side, $T + dT$ on the other side. The normal reaction from the pulley is $dN$, and friction force $\mu \, dN$ opposes the impending slip. **Step 2 — Force balance along the belt direction:** $$(T + dT)\cos(d\theta/2) - T\cos(d\theta/2) - \mu \, dN = 0$$ **Step 3 — Force balance normal to belt:** $$dN = (T + dT)\sin(d\theta/2) + T\sin(d\theta/2)$$ **Step 4 — Using small-angle approximation $\sin(d\theta/2) \approx d\theta/2$ and $\cos(d\theta/2) \approx 1$:** $$dT = \mu \, dN$$ $$dN = 2T \cdot \frac{d\theta}{2} = T \, d\theta$$ **Step 5 — Substituting:** $$dT = \mu T \, d\theta$$ $$\frac{dT}{T} = \mu \, d\theta$$ **Step 6 — Integrating from $T_2$ to $T_1$ over the angle of wrap $\theta$:** $$\int_{T_2}^{T_1} \frac{dT}{T} = \mu \int_0^{\theta} d\theta$$ $$\ln\!\left(\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right) = \mu \theta$$ **Step 7 — Final result:** $$\boxed{\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}}$$ This is the **Eytelwein equation** — the foundation of all belt drive design. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Modification of Eytelwein's Equation for V-Belt In a V-belt, the belt wedge angle is $\beta$ and the groove angle is $\alpha$. The relationship is: $$\beta = 2 \cdot \frac{\alpha}{2} - \text{(small clearance)}$$ When the belt is wedged into the groove, the normal reaction $dN$ is concentrated along the groove walls, and the net frictional force becomes: $$dF_f = 2 \mu \, dN \cdot \sin(\alpha/2) = \frac{\mu \, dN}{\csc(\alpha/2) \cdot \frac{1}{2}}$$ Substituting into the same procedure as for flat belt: $$\frac{dT}{T} = \frac{\mu \, d\theta}{\sin(\alpha/2)}$$ Integrating: $$\boxed{\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta / \sin(\alpha/2)}}$$ This shows that a V-belt has an **effective coefficient of friction** $\mu' = \dfrac{\mu}{\sin(\alpha/2)}$, which is higher than the flat-belt $\mu$ by a factor of about **2.92** (for $\alpha = 40°$). This is the mathematical reason V-belts grip better. --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Length of an Open Belt Drive Consider two pulleys of diameters $d_1$ (driver) and $d_2$ (driven) with center distance $C$. The open belt runs from the top of pulley 1 to the top of pulley 2 on one side, and the bottom on the other. The total length is the sum of: - Two straight portions (top and bottom) of equal length $\sqrt{C^2 - \left(\frac{d_2 - d_1}{2}\right)^2}$ - Two arcs of contact — one of angle $\pi + 2\alpha$ on larger pulley, and $\pi - 2\alpha$ on smaller pulley. After simplification (Taylor approximation for $d_2 - d_1 \ll C$): $$\boxed{L = \frac{\pi}{2}(d_1 + d_2) + 2C + \frac{(d_2 - d_1)^2}{4C}}$$ For a **crossed belt**, the algebraic sign in the last term changes: $$\boxed{L = \frac{\pi}{2}(d_1 + d_2) + 2C + \frac{(d_1 + d_2)^2}{4C}}$$ ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Selection Guide for "Types" of Belt Drives | **Criterion** | **Flat Belt** | **V-Belt** | **Toothed Belt** | **Chain Drive** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Drive type | Friction | Friction (wedged) | Positive | Positive | | Power range (kW) | < 500 | < 750 | < 200 | < 5000 | | Center distance | Long (>15 m) | Medium (0.5–5 m) | Medium | Medium (0.5–3 m) | | Speed ratio | Up to 1:20 | Up to 1:20 | Up to 1:20 | Up to 1:8 | | Velocity (m/s) | Up to 30 | Up to 30 | Up to 50 | Up to 12 | | Slip possible? | Yes | Yes | No | No | | Cost | Lowest | Low | High | Medium | | Noise | Quiet | Quiet | Quiet | Noisy | | Maintenance | Low | Low | Very low | Moderate (lubrication) | | Backlash | High | Medium | Very low | Medium | | Typical use | Line shafts, conveyors | Machine tools, autos | Camshaft, robotics | Bicycles, conveyors | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "No-Slip" Boundary:** The fundamental engineering rule is — if **synchronization** is critical (camshaft-to-crankshaft, robot arm to encoder), choose **positive drive (chain or toothed belt)**. If **slip is tolerable** and **shock absorption** is desired, choose **friction drive (flat or V-belt)**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Classification & Selection Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Power Transmission Required] --> B{Critical Synchronization?} B -- YES --> C[Positive Drive Required] C --> C1[Chain Drive] C1 --> C1a[Roller Chain
Standard Industrial] C1 --> C1b[Silent Chain
Low Noise High Speed] C1 --> C1c[Block Chain
Low Speed Heavy Load] C --> C2[Toothed Belt Drive] C2 --> C2a[Timing Belt
Automotive CNC] B -- NO --> D[Friction Drive Allowed] D --> D1{Center Distance?} D1 -- LONG --> D1a[Rope Drive] D1a --> D1a1[Hemp Rope] D1a --> D1a2[Wire Rope] D1 -- MEDIUM --> D2{Power Level?} D2 -- HIGH --> D2a[V-Belt Drive] D2a --> D2a1[Classical V-Belt] D2a --> D2a2[Narrow V-Belt] D2a --> D2a3[Poly-V Ribbed] D2 -- MEDIUM --> D2b[Flat Belt Drive] D2b --> D2b1[Leather Flat Belt] D2b --> D2b2[Fabric Flat Belt] D2 -- LOW --> D2c[Round Belt Drive] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000 style D1a fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000 style D2a fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a V-Belt Drive System ```mermaid graph LR node1[Motor Driver Pulley] -->|Tight Side T1| node2[Belt V-Section] node2 -->|Wedging Action
Friction mu over sin alpha by 2| node3[Driven Pulley V-Groove] node3 -->|Slack Side T2| node4[Driven Machine Load] node1 -.->|Centrifugal Tension TC| node2 node2 -.->|Centrifugal Tension TC| node3 node5[Speed N1 rpm] --> node1 node4 --> node6[Output Speed N2 rpm] node7[Power P kW] --> node1 node8[Velocity Ratio i] --> node5 node8 --> node6 style node1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#FFF style node2 fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,color:#FFF style node3 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#FFF style node4 fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,color:#000 style node5 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style node6 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style node7 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000 style node8 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 ASCII Schematic — Open vs Crossed Belt Comparison ``` OPEN BELT (Same direction rotation) CROSSED BELT (Opposite direction rotation) ======================== ======================== ___________ ___________ / \ / ________ \ | Driver d1 | | / \ | \____________/ \ \ d1 / / | \ \____/ / | T1 (tight) \/ \/ _________|__________________________ /\ /\ | | | | \ / | | Driver | | | \/ | |_________|__________________________| | /\ | | T2 (slack) | / \ | ____________ |/____\| / \ /\ | Driven d2 | / \ /\ \____________/ / d2\ \/ \ |____||____| T1 T1 _______________________ T2 _____________________ /\ | | | / \ T2 | Driver Pulley | Driven Pulley | / \___________ |_______________________|_______________________|/__________________ USE: General purpose, same rotation USE: Reversing rotation ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — V-Belt Cross-Section (Wedging Action) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Pulley Groove] -->|alpha=40 deg groove| B[V-Belt Wedge] B -->|beta=38 deg belt| A A -->|Normal Reaction N1| B A -->|Normal Reaction N2| B B -->|Friction Force mu times N1| A B -->|Friction Force mu times N2| A C[Sum of Frictional Forces] -->|Equals 2 mu N sin alpha by 2| D[Effective Friction] D -->|mu prime equals mu by sin alpha by 2| E[Enhanced Grip 2.92 times] style A fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,color:#FFF style B fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#FFF style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style D fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style E fill:#FF69B4,stroke:#000,color:#FFF ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **List the different types of belt drives and give one example of application for each.** **Model Answer:** The different types of belt drives are: 1. **Flat belt drive** – used in line shafts and conveyor systems. 2. **V-belt drive** – used in automobile accessory drives (alternator, AC compressor). 3. **Toothed/Timing belt drive** – used in automotive camshaft drives, CNC machines. 4. **Round belt drive** – used in small household appliances and sewing machines. 5. **Poly-V/Ribbed belt drive** – used in modern serpentine automotive drives. *(3 marks – 1 mark for listing each type with example, 5 types may be listed in 3-mark question)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Distinguish between a flat belt drive and a V-belt drive on the basis of power transmission mechanism.** **Model Answer:** | **Basis** | **Flat Belt** | **V-Belt** | |---|---|---| | Cross-section | Rectangular | Trapezoidal (V) | | Mechanism | Pure friction | Wedge/frictional grip | | Effective μ | $\mu$ | $\mu / \sin(\alpha/2) \approx 2.92\mu$ | | Power capacity | Low | ~3× higher for same tension | | Pulley | Flat-faced | V-grooved | *(3 marks – 1.5 for each type, comparison table format preferred)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]` **Q. (a)** A flat belt 100 mm wide and 12 mm thick transmits 12 kW at a belt speed of 20 m/s. The angle of lap on the smaller pulley is 165° and the coefficient of friction is 0.3. If the density of belt material is 1100 kg/m³, determine: - (i) The tensions on the tight and slack sides, neglecting centrifugal tension. - (ii) The maximum stress in the belt. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - Belt width $b = 100$ mm $= 0.1$ m - Belt thickness $t = 12$ mm $= 0.012$ m - Power $P = 12$ kW $= 12000$ W - Belt speed $v = 20$ m/s - Angle of lap $\theta = 165° = 165 \times \dfrac{\pi}{180} = 2.88$ rad - Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.3$ - Density $\rho = 1100$ kg/m³ **Step 1 — Area of belt cross-section:** $$A = b \cdot t = 0.1 \times 0.012 = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Mass per unit length of belt:** $$m = A \cdot \rho = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 1100 = 1.32 \text{ kg/m}$$ **Step 3 — Centrifugal tension:** $$T_C = m v^2 = 1.32 \times (20)^2 = 1.32 \times 400 = 528 \text{ N}$$ **Step 4 — Effective tensions (considering centrifugal effect):** The Eytelwein equation applies to **effective tensions** $T_1'$ and $T_2'$: $$\frac{T_1'}{T_2'} = e^{\mu\theta} = e^{0.3 \times 2.88} = e^{0.864} = 2.372$$ **Step 5 — Net driving force (effective tension difference):** $$T_1' - T_2' = \frac{P}{v} = \frac{12000}{20} = 600 \text{ N}$$ From the two equations: $$T_1' = 2.372 \, T_2'$$ $$2.372 \, T_2' - T_2' = 600$$ $$1.372 \, T_2' = 600$$ $$T_2' = 437.3 \text{ N}$$ $$T_1' = 2.372 \times 437.3 = 1037.3 \text{ N}$$ **Step 6 — Actual tensions on tight and slack sides (subtracting centrifugal tension):** $$\boxed{T_1 = T_1' + T_C = 1037.3 + 528 = 1565.3 \text{ N}}$$ $$\boxed{T_2 = T_2' + T_C = 437.3 + 528 = 965.3 \text{ N}}$$ **Step 7 — Maximum stress in the belt (occurs on the tight side):** $$\sigma_{max} = \frac{T_1}{A} = \frac{1565.3}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.304 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2$$ $$\boxed{\sigma_{max} \approx 1.30 \text{ MPa}}$$ --- **Q. (b)** Compare flat belt, V-belt, and chain drive on the basis of: - (i) Type of drive (friction/positive) - (ii) Power capacity - (iii) Slip/no-slip - (iv) Speed ratio - (v) Application Give two advantages and one limitation of chain drive over belt drive. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** | **Basis** | **Flat Belt** | **V-Belt** | **Chain Drive** | |---|---|---|---| | Type of drive | Friction | Friction (wedged) | Positive | | Power capacity | Up to 500 kW | Up to 750 kW | Up to 5000 kW | | Slip | Yes | Yes | No (positive) | | Max speed ratio | 1:20 | 1:20 | 1:8 | | Typical application | Line shafts | Machine tools, autos | Bicycles, conveyors | | Center distance | Long (>15 m) | Medium | Medium (<4 m) | | Maintenance | Lowest | Low | Moderate (lubrication) | **Advantages of Chain Drive over Belt Drive:** 1. **No slip** — Constant velocity ratio is guaranteed (positive engagement). 2. **Higher power capacity** — Chain drives can transmit much higher torque in compact space. 3. **Smaller center distance** — Suitable for close-coupled shafts. 4. **Higher efficiency** — Less power loss from slip and friction. **Limitation of Chain Drive:** 1. **Requires lubrication** — Adds to maintenance cost. 2. **Noisy** at high speeds compared to belts. 3. **Velocity fluctuations** — Chordal action causes minor speed variations. 4. **Sensitive to misalignment** — Can cause premature wear. *(7 marks — 1 mark for each comparison row of the table = 3 marks; 1.5 marks per advantage × 2 = 3 marks; 1 mark for the limitation)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]` **Q. (a)** A V-belt drive transmits 20 kW at 1450 rpm of the driving pulley. The diameter of the driving pulley is 250 mm and the driven pulley is 600 mm. The groove angle is 40° and the coefficient of friction is 0.25. The angle of lap on the smaller pulley is 170°. Calculate: - (i) The belt speed - (ii) The tensions on the tight and slack sides - (iii) The effective radius of the driven pulley **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $P = 20$ kW, $N_1 = 1450$ rpm, $d_1 = 250$ mm $= 0.25$ m, $d_2 = 600$ mm $= 0.6$ m - $\alpha = 40°$, $\mu = 0.25$, $\theta_1 = 170° = 170 \times \pi/180 = 2.967$ rad **Step 1 — Belt linear speed:** $$v = \frac{\pi d_1 N_1}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.25 \times 1450}{60} = 18.98 \text{ m/s}$$ $$\boxed{v \approx 19 \text{ m/s}}$$ **Step 2 — Velocity ratio and driven pulley speed:** $$i = \frac{d_2}{d_1} = \frac{0.6}{0.25} = 2.4$$ $$N_2 = \frac{N_1}{i} = \frac{1450}{2.4} = 604.17 \text{ rpm}$$ **Step 3 — Effective coefficient of friction for V-belt:** $$\mu' = \frac{\mu}{\sin(\alpha/2)} = \frac{0.25}{\sin(20°)} = \frac{0.25}{0.342} = 0.731$$ **Step 4 — Ratio of tensions (V-belt Eytelwein):** $$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu' \theta_1} = e^{0.731 \times 2.967} = e^{2.169} = 8.75$$ **Step 5 — Net driving tension:** $$T_1 - T_2 = \frac{P}{v} = \frac{20000}{18.98} = 1053.7 \text{ N}$$ **Step 6 — Solving simultaneously:** $$8.75 \, T_2 - T_2 = 1053.7$$ $$7.75 \, T_2 = 1053.7$$ $$T_2 = 135.95 \text{ N}$$ $$T_1 = 8.75 \times 135.95 = 1189.6 \text{ N}$$ $$\boxed{T_1 \approx 1190 \text{ N}, \quad T_2 \approx 136 \text{ N}}$$ **Step 7 — Effective radius of driven pulley (considering belt thickness and groove depth):** For V-belts, the effective diameter = pitch diameter = outer diameter – $2 \times$ (groove depth). If the belt sits deep in the groove, the **effective radius** corresponds to the **pitch line** of the belt. $$R_{eff} = \frac{d_2}{2} - \delta$$ where $\delta$ is the depth of the belt below the pulley rim. For a standard V-belt with pitch line at the top of the groove: $$\boxed{R_{eff,2} \approx \frac{d_2}{2} = 0.3 \text{ m (assuming negligible correction for thin belt)}}$$ In a more rigorous treatment: $$v = \frac{\pi d_{1,eff} N_1}{60} = \frac{\pi d_{2,eff} N_2}{60}$$ If $d_{2,eff} = d_1 \times (N_1/N_2) = 0.25 \times (1450/604.17) = 0.6$ m, which confirms the design. --- **Q. (b)** Two pulleys, one 600 mm diameter and the other 400 mm diameter, are connected by a crossed belt. The center distance is 2 m. Calculate the length of the belt required. State two situations where crossed belt drives are preferred over open belt drives. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $d_1 = 600$ mm $= 0.6$ m - $d_2 = 400$ mm $= 0.4$ m - $C = 2$ m (center distance) - Crossed belt **Step 1 — Apply the crossed belt length formula:** $$L = \frac{\pi}{2}(d_1 + d_2) + 2C + \frac{(d_1 + d_2)^2}{4C}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute values:** $$L = \frac{\pi}{2}(0.6 + 0.4) + 2(2) + \frac{(0.6 + 0.4)^2}{4 \times 2}$$ $$L = \frac{\pi}{2}(1.0) + 4 + \frac{(1.0)^2}{8}$$ $$L = 1.5708 + 4 + 0.125$$ $$\boxed{L = 5.696 \text{ m} \approx 5.70 \text{ m}}$$ **Step 3 — Situations where crossed belt is preferred:** 1. **Reversing direction of rotation** — When the driven shaft must rotate in the opposite direction to the driver, crossed belt achieves this naturally. With an open belt, you would need idler pulleys or a reversal gear. 2. **Higher wrap angle** — In crossed belts, both pulleys have an angle of wrap equal to $\pi + 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{d_1+d_2}{2C}\right)$, which is greater than 180°, leading to higher power transmission capacity. 3. **Elimination of slip-side load on bearings** — In some heavy machinery, crossed belt configuration offers a more symmetric pull on shaft bearings. 4. **Used in flour mills, agricultural threshers, and certain machine tools** where reversing motion is required. *(7 marks — 3 for calculation, 4 for the two situations with explanation)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > > 1. **Centrifugal Tension Forgetting Trap (–2 to –3 marks):** Most students compute $T_1$ and $T_2$ directly from $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu\theta}$ and forget to **add $T_C = mv^2$** to both sides. Always check whether the question says "neglecting" or "considering" centrifugal tension. > > 2. **Angle in Radians Mistake (–1 mark):** Eytelwein's equation uses $\theta$ in **radians**, not degrees. If the question gives 170°, you must convert: $170° = 2.967$ rad. > > 3. **V-Belt Effective Coefficient (–2 marks):** A common error is using $\mu$ directly for a V-belt instead of $\mu / \sin(\alpha/2)$. This underestimates the tension ratio by a factor of ~3. > > 4. **Open vs Crossed Belt Length (–1 mark):** The two formulas differ in the last term — **sign inside the square** — confusing them is a guaranteed 1-mark loss. > > 5. **Power vs Net Tension (–1 mark):** $P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v$ uses the **difference of tensions**, not $T_1$ alone. Some students incorrectly write $P = T_1 \cdot v$. > > 6. **Units Discipline:** Always use SI units (m, m/s, N, Pa, W) in final answer. Mixing mm and m is a frequent mistake. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Types of Belt, Rope & Chain Drives** ### 1. Master Classification (MUST MEMORIZE) - **Friction drives:** Flat, V, Toothed (timing), Poly-V (ribbed), Round belt, Rope. - **Positive drives:** Roller chain, Silent chain, Block chain, Detachable chain. ### 2. The "Big 5" Equations (High-Frequency in KTU) - Eytelwein Flat: $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu\theta}$ - Eytelwein V-Belt: $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu\theta/\sin(\alpha/2)}$ - Centrifugal tension: $T_C = mv^2$ - Power: $P = (T_1 - T_2)v$ - Velocity ratio (open): $i = d_2/d_1 = N_1/N_2$ ### 3. Belt Length Formulas - Open: $L = \dfrac{\pi}{2}(d_1+d_2) + 2C + \dfrac{(d_2-d_1)^2}{4C}$ - Crossed: $L = \dfrac{\pi}{2}(d_1+d_2) + 2C + \dfrac{(d_1+d_2)^2}{4C}$ ### 4. Selection Rule of Thumb - **Synchronization critical → Positive drive (chain, timing belt).** - **Slip tolerable + shock absorption → Friction drive (V-belt, flat belt).** - **Long center distance → Rope drive.** - **Compact + high power → V-belt (2.92× advantage).** - **Light duty, small pulley → Round belt.** ### 5. Critical Numerical Values - V-belt groove angle: **40°**, belt angle: **38°** → $\sin(20°) \approx 0.342$ → effective μ factor $\approx$ **2.92** - Standard density of belt: **1000–1500 kg/m³** - V-belt runs at higher effective friction due to wedge. ### 6. Common Confusions Clarified - **Slip vs Creep:** Slip = gross sliding (bad); Creep = small elastic stretch difference (normal, must be considered). - **Angle of lap ≠ π** for open belt on unequal pulleys; it depends on the diameters. - **Chain vs Belt:** Chain has **chordal action** causing velocity fluctuations at low speeds; belt has continuous smooth action. ### 7. KTU 2024 Exam Strategy - Module 4 questions typically carry 7–14 marks, often combining a numerical on a specific drive type with a descriptive comparison. - Always **draw or reference the type** (open, crossed, V, flat) in your answer. - When asked for "types," **list 5+ types** with **specific applications** — examiners reward breadth. - For numericals, **state assumptions clearly** (e.g., "neglecting centrifugal tension"). - **Show units** in every step.

topics.title: Gear Terminology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBIET404 - THEORY OF MACHINES AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Gear Terminology # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Gear A **gear** is a rotating, machine element possessing cut teeth (cogs) of identical profile, designed to mesh with another toothed machine element and transmit positive (synchronous) motion or power from one shaft to another without slip. The geometry of a gear is fully described by a standardized set of terms collectively known as **Gear Terminology** (per **AGMA 2015 / ISO 6336** standards adopted in KTU syllabus). > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 4):** > Gear Terminology forms the foundational vocabulary of any gear design problem. Every KTU Part B question (14 marks) on spur/helical gears will assume you are fluent with these terms. A single incorrect definition can lose 2–3 marks in board valuation. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Cog-Wheel" Concept Imagine two **wheels of a mechanical watch**. Each wheel has tiny bumps on its rim. When you push one wheel, the bumps of one wheel *push* the bumps of the other wheel, causing the second wheel to rotate. That synchronous, no-slip, no-belt push is exactly what a gear does — but at industrial scales (transmissions in cars, CNC spindles, wind turbines). > [!IMPORTANT] > The key insight: a gear is not a friction device (like a belt or tyre). The **teeth themselves** transfer force. This is why the **shape, size, and spacing of teeth** are critical — and that is what "Gear Terminology" precisely defines. ## 1.3 Classification Snapshot (For Context) | Gear Type | Axis Orientation | Common Use | |---|---|---| | **Spur Gear** | Parallel | Low-medium speed, simple transmissions | | **Helical Gear** | Parallel (inclined teeth) | High-speed, smooth, quiet drives | | **Bevel Gear** | Intersecting (usually 90°) | Differential, hand drills | | **Worm & Worm Wheel** | Non-intersecting, skew | High reduction ratio | For Module 4, the **spur gear** is the reference geometry from which all other gear terms are derived. ## 1.4 The Standard Set of Gear Terms — Quick Vocabulary The following terms are the **17 mandatory definitions** every KTU student must memorize: 1. **Pitch Circle** — The imaginary circle on which two gears effectively roll. 2. **Pitch Circle Diameter (PCD / d)** — Diameter of the pitch circle. 3. **Circular Pitch (Pc)** — Arc length measured along the pitch circle between corresponding points of adjacent teeth. 4. **Diametral Pitch (Pd)** — Ratio of number of teeth to pitch circle diameter (in inches). 5. **Module (m)** — Ratio of pitch circle diameter to number of teeth (in mm) — the **SI standard**. 6. **Addendum (a)** — Radial height of tooth above the pitch circle. 7. **Dedendum (b)** — Radial depth of tooth below the pitch circle (into the root). 8. **Whole Depth (h)** — Sum of addendum + dedendum. 9. **Working Depth (hw)** — Maximum depth of tooth contact. 10. **Clearance (c)** — Difference between dedendum and addendum. 11. **Tooth Thickness (t)** — Arc length of tooth measured along the pitch circle. 12. **Tooth Space** — Arc length of space between adjacent teeth along pitch circle. 13. **Face Width (b / F)** — Axial length of the gear tooth. 14. **Pressure Angle (φ)** — Angle between the line of action and the common tangent to the pitch circles. 15. **Base Circle** — Circle from which the involute tooth profile is generated. 16. **Addendum / Dedendum / Base / Root Circle** — Concentric circles that bound the tooth geometry. 17. **Backlash** — Small intentional gap between mating tooth flanks. > [!NOTE] > Standard pressure angles (ISA – International Standards Association) are **20°** (modern) and **14.5°** (older, obsolete). KTU 2024 problems universally use **20° full-depth involute teeth** unless stated otherwise. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Pitch Circle — The Heart of Gear Geometry Two meshing gears behave as if they are two **frictionless rolling cylinders**. The diameters of these imaginary cylinders are the **Pitch Circle Diameters (PCD)**. A pair of meshing gears must have equal **circular pitch** at the pitch point — this is the fundamental compatibility condition. ### Why the Pitch Circle Exists * Real tooth flanks are curved (involute), not simple cylinders. * The pitch circle is a **mathematical abstraction** that lets us replace the complex tooth contact with two rolling circles of equivalent diameter. * All gear calculations (speed ratio, center distance, tooth forces) are referenced to the pitch circle. ## 2.2 The Two Competing Standards: Module vs. Diametral Pitch > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 uses **SI units exclusively**. Therefore, the **Module (m)** system is the standard. The Diametral Pitch (Pd) system appears in theory-of-machines textbooks (Khurmi, Shigley) and is required only for **conversion problems** (e.g., a U.S.-manufactured gear being substituted). ## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of the Tooth Geometry 1. **Addendum (a)**: Tooth rises from pitch circle upward by a height $a = m$ (for standard full-depth teeth). 2. **Dedendum (b)**: Tooth extends from pitch circle downward by $b = 1.25\,m$ (the extra $0.25\,m$ provides the **clearance**). 3. **Working Depth** = $2m$ (two addenda). 4. **Whole Depth** = $2.25\,m$ (one addendum + one dedendum). 5. **Clearance** = $0.25\,m$. 6. **Tooth Thickness = Tooth Space = $\frac{P_c}{2}$** at the pitch circle (so the tooth and gap are equal — no backlash by default). 7. **Base Circle Diameter**: $d_b = d \cos\phi$ — the involute tooth profile is geometrically generated from this circle. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Unit / Value | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Circular Pitch | $P_c = \pi m$ | mm | | 2 | Module | $m = \dfrac{d}{T}$ | mm | | 3 | Diametral Pitch (US) | $P_d = \dfrac{T}{d_{\text{in}}}$ | teeth/inch | | 4 | Module ↔ Diametral Pitch | $m = \dfrac{25.4}{P_d}$ | mm | | 5 | Pitch Circle Diameter | $d = mT$ | mm | | 6 | Center Distance | $C = \dfrac{m(T_1 + T_2)}{2}$ | mm | | 7 | Speed Ratio | $i = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \dfrac{d_2}{d_1}$ | dimensionless | | 8 | Addendum (Std.) | $a = 1.0\,m$ | mm | | 9 | Dedendum (Std.) | $b = 1.25\,m$ | mm | | 10 | Whole Depth | $h = 2.25\,m$ | mm | | 11 | Working Depth | $h_w = 2.0\,m$ | mm | | 12 | Clearance | $c = 0.25\,m$ | mm | | 13 | Tooth Thickness (at PC) | $t = \dfrac{P_c}{2} = \dfrac{\pi m}{2}$ | mm | | 14 | Base Circle Diameter | $d_b = d \cos\phi$ | mm | | 15 | Outside (Addendum) Dia. | $d_a = d + 2a = m(T+2)$ | mm | | 16 | Root (Dedendum) Dia. | $d_f = d - 2b = m(T-2.5)$ | mm | | 17 | Base Circle Dia. (Final) | $d_b = mT\cos\phi$ | mm | > [!NOTE] > **Critical reminder for KTU valuation:** A student who writes $b = m$ (instead of $1.25\,m$) loses 1 mark instantly. The $0.25\,m$ clearance is non-negotiable in standard full-depth teeth. ## 2.5 Pressure Angle — The Hidden Variable The pressure angle $\phi$ defines the **direction of the force** between meshing teeth, measured from the common tangent to the pitch circles. For a standard 20° full-depth involute: * **14.5°** — Old standard (still seen in some textbook examples). * **20°** — Current ISA / AGMA standard (universal in KTU 2024). * **25°** — High-load / heavy-duty applications (e.g., industrial press gears). A **higher pressure angle** means: * Stronger tooth (thicker at root). * Higher radial force component (more bearing load). * Lower sensitivity to manufacturing error. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Term | Engineering Use | |---|---| | **Module** | Selected from standard series (1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10) — **must be same for both meshing gears**. | | **Center Distance** | Determines housing/bearing design. | | **Pressure Angle** | Affects tooth strength, noise, vibration, and bearing life. | | **Face Width** | Determines load-carrying capacity (Lewis / Buckingham equations). | | **Base Circle** | Used to construct the involute profile on a CAD system. | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Module — Why "Module" is Called So The **module** is the most fundamental SI parameter. Its derivation follows directly from the relationship between the pitch circumference and the number of teeth. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Pitch Circumference} &= \pi \, d \\ \text{Number of teeth} &= T \\ \text{Circular Pitch } (P_c) &= \frac{\pi \, d}{T} \\ \text{Define Module } m &\triangleq \frac{d}{T} \\ \therefore \quad P_c &= \pi m \end{aligned} $$ **Step-by-step interpretation:** * The pitch circumference is $\pi d$ (geometric fact for any circle). * Divide by $T$ to get arc length per tooth = $P_c$. * By **definition**, the module $m$ is pitch diameter per tooth. * Therefore $P_c = \pi m$ — i.e., the circular pitch is just $\pi$ times the module. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Insight:** $m$ has units of *length per tooth* (mm/tooth). A larger $m$ means a "bigger" tooth — the gear is physically larger and stronger. This is why **module selection = strength selection** in gear design. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Center Distance of Two Meshing Gears Two gears with teeth $T_1$ and $T_2$ mesh when their pitch circles are tangent. The center distance is the sum of the two pitch radii. $$ \begin{aligned} d_1 &= m T_1 \\ d_2 &= m T_2 \\ \text{Center distance } C &= r_1 + r_2 = \frac{d_1}{2} + \frac{d_2}{2} \\ C &= \frac{m T_1}{2} + \frac{m T_2}{2} \\ C &= \frac{m(T_1 + T_2)}{2} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** For gears to mesh, they **must share the same module** $m$. Different $m$ values would produce teeth of different size — they could not interlock. This is the **law of gearing compatibility**. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Speed Ratio and Its Geometric Meaning The angular velocities at the pitch point must be equal in the tangential direction (no slip at the pitch point): $$ \begin{aligned} v_{\text{tangential}} &= \omega_1 \cdot r_1 = \omega_2 \cdot r_2 \\ \frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} &= \frac{r_2}{r_1} = \frac{d_2}{d_1} \\ \text{Since } d = mT \text{ and } m \text{ is same:} \quad \frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} &= \frac{T_2}{T_1} \\ i &= \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{T_2}{T_1} \end{aligned} $$ **Engineering takeaway:** A small pinion ($T_1 = 20$) driving a large gear ($T_2 = 100$) gives a 5:1 reduction — the small gear spins 5× faster. ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Base Circle Diameter The involute profile is generated by unwinding a string from the **base circle**. The base circle is concentric with the pitch circle, but smaller. The pressure angle is the angle between the line joining centers and the line of action. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{From the geometry of the pressure angle:} \\ \cos\phi &= \frac{r_b}{r} = \frac{d_b}{d} \\ \therefore \quad d_b &= d \cos\phi \\ \text{Substituting } d = mT: \quad d_b &= mT \cos\phi \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** For a 20° pressure angle: $$ \cos 20° \approx 0.9397 $$ So the base circle is **~94% of the pitch circle diameter** — always smaller, always hidden inside the gear blank. ## 3.5 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Board Style) **Problem:** A pinion has 18 teeth and meshes with a gear of 72 teeth. Module is 4 mm. Pressure angle is 20°. Find: 1. Pitch circle diameters 2. Center distance 3. Addendum and Dedendum of each 4. Base circle diameter of the pinion 5. Circular pitch 6. Tooth thickness at pitch circle **Given:** $T_1 = 18$, $T_2 = 72$, $m = 4$ mm, $\phi = 20°$. **Solution — Step by Step:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{(1) Pitch Circle Diameters:} \\ d_1 &= mT_1 = 4 \times 18 = 72 \text{ mm} \\ d_2 &= mT_2 = 4 \times 72 = 288 \text{ mm} \\ \\ \text{(2) Center Distance:} \\ C &= \frac{m(T_1 + T_2)}{2} = \frac{4 \times (18 + 72)}{2} \\ C &= \frac{4 \times 90}{2} = 180 \text{ mm} \\ \\ \text{(3) Addendum (same for both, since } m \text{ is same):} \\ a &= m = 4 \text{ mm} \\ \text{Dedendum:} \quad b &= 1.25\,m = 1.25 \times 4 = 5 \text{ mm} \\ \\ \text{(4) Base Circle Diameter of Pinion:} \\ d_{b1} &= mT_1 \cos\phi = 4 \times 18 \times \cos 20° \\ d_{b1} &= 72 \times 0.9397 \approx 67.66 \text{ mm} \\ \\ \text{(5) Circular Pitch:} \\ P_c &= \pi m = \pi \times 4 \approx 12.566 \text{ mm} \\ \\ \text{(6) Tooth Thickness at Pitch Circle:} \\ t &= \frac{P_c}{2} = \frac{\pi m}{2} = \frac{\pi \times 4}{2} \\ t &\approx 6.283 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Results Table:** | Parameter | Pinion ($T_1=18$) | Gear ($T_2=72$) | |---|---|---| | PCD | 72 mm | 288 mm | | Addendum | 4 mm | 4 mm | | Dedendum | 5 mm | 5 mm | | Outside Dia. | 80 mm | 296 mm | | Root Dia. | 62 mm | 278 mm | | Base Dia. | 67.66 mm | 270.64 mm | | Center Distance | 180 mm | 180 mm | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Spur Gear Tooth Geometry (Concentric Circles) The following Mermaid diagram maps the **five concentric circles** that define a standard spur gear, and the **key terminology** associated with each layer. ```mermaid flowchart TB A["OUTSIDE / ADDENDUM CIRCLE
da = d + 2a
Largest circle, tips of teeth"] B["PITCH CIRCLE
d = mT
Reference circle for meshing"] C["BASE CIRCLE
db = d cos phi
Involute generation starts here"] D["ROOT / DEDENDUM CIRCLE
df = d - 2b
Bottom of tooth gap"] E["TOOTH THICKNESS t = Pi times m over 2"] F["CIRCULAR PITCH Pc = Pi times m"] G["CLEARANCE c = 0.25 m"] H["ADDENDUM a = 1.0 m"] I["DEDENDUM b = 1.25 m"] J["PRESSURE ANGLE phi = 20 deg"] A --> H B --> F B --> E B --> J C --> J B --> H B --> I I --> G A --> B B --> C B --> D ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Functional Block Architecture — Two Meshing Gears This block-level functional map shows the **interactions between a pinion and a gear** during meshing. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[DRIVER SHAFT
Pinion T1] --> B[PITCH CIRCLE d1] B --> C[LINE OF ACTION
at angle phi] C --> D[CONTACT ZONE] D --> E[PITCH CIRCLE d2] E --> F[DRIVEN SHAFT
Gear T2] G[MODULE m] --> B G --> E H[COMMON TANGENT LINE] --> C I[BASE CIRCLE dB1] --> C J[BASE CIRCLE dB2] --> C ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Mating Tooth Pair (Side View Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[PINION TOOTH
P1] --> B[LINE OF ACTION
Involute contact] B --> C[GEAR TOOTH
G1] A --> D[ADDENDUM a] A --> E[DEDENDUM b] C --> F[ADDENDUM a] C --> G[DEDENDUM b] B --> H[ANGLE OF PRESSURE phi = 20 deg] B --> I[PATH OF CONTACT] B --> J[ARC OF CONTACT] ``` ## 4.4 Textual Schematic of Concentric Circles For a single spur gear, the five circles are nested as follows (outermost to innermost): 1. **Addendum Circle (Outside Diameter, $d_a$)** — outermost, tips of teeth. 2. **Pitch Circle ($d$)** — reference; meshing partner's pitch circle touches here. 3. **Base Circle ($d_b = d \cos 20°$)** — geometric source of the involute curve. 4. **Dedendum Circle (Root Diameter, $d_f$)** — innermost, bottom of tooth gap. 5. (Clear) The area between dedendum and addendum of mating gear = **clearance zone**. > [!NOTE] > In KTU 2D sketches, the **addendum circle is drawn as a solid (visible) arc**, the **pitch circle as a chain/dashed line**, and the **base circle as a thin dotted line** — this is the AGMA drawing convention. Examiners expect students to label all three when asked for a "neat sketch." # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define the following terms with neat sketches: (i) Circular Pitch, (ii) Module, (iii) Pressure Angle.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Circular Pitch ($P_c$):** It is the arc distance measured along the pitch circle between the corresponding points of two adjacent teeth. $$ P_c = \pi m \quad \text{(in mm)} $$ 2. **Module ($m$):** It is the ratio of the pitch circle diameter to the number of teeth. $$ m = \frac{d}{T} \quad \text{(in mm)} $$ 3. **Pressure Angle ($\phi$):** It is the angle between the line of action (common normal at the point of contact) and the common tangent to the pitch circles at the pitch point. Standard value: $\phi = 20°$. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **State the relationship between the number of teeth, module, and pitch circle diameter. Differentiate between 'addendum' and 'dedendum'.** **Model Answer:** **Relationship:** $d = m \times T$ (Pitch circle diameter = Module × Number of teeth) **Differentiation:** | Parameter | Addendum | Dedendum | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Height of tooth above pitch circle | Depth of tooth below pitch circle | | **Value** | $a = 1.0\,m$ | $b = 1.25\,m$ | | **Circle** | Bounded by addendum circle (outside) | Bounded by dedendum/root circle | | **Standard Reference** | Top of tooth | Bottom of tooth gap | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > * Marks are deducted (typically **−1 to −2**) for **not drawing a labelled sketch** in gear design questions. Always include a neat diagram showing all five concentric circles. > * Writing $b = m$ instead of $b = 1.25\,m$ results in **direct 1-mark cut**. > * Forgetting the units (mm) on numerical answers leads to **−0.5 mark per answer**. > * Mixing Module ($m$) and Diametral Pitch ($P_d$) in the same problem without conversion = **wrong answer**. ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Standard Module-Based Problem** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Two spur gears with module 5 mm mesh together. The pinion has 20 teeth and rotates at 1450 rpm. The gear has 50 teeth. Calculate: **(a)** (7 marks) The pitch circle diameters, center distance, circular pitch, and the speed of the driven gear. **(b)** (7 marks) The addendum, dedendum, whole depth, working depth, clearance, tooth thickness, and base circle diameter of the pinion. Take pressure angle $\phi = 20°$. #### Model Solution: **Given:** $m = 5$ mm, $T_1 = 20$ (pinion), $T_2 = 50$ (gear), $N_1 = 1450$ rpm, $\phi = 20°$. **Part (a):** $$ \begin{aligned} d_1 &= m T_1 = 5 \times 20 = 100 \text{ mm} \\ d_2 &= m T_2 = 5 \times 50 = 250 \text{ mm} \\ C &= \frac{m(T_1 + T_2)}{2} = \frac{5 \times 70}{2} = 175 \text{ mm} \\ P_c &= \pi m = \pi \times 5 = 15.708 \text{ mm} \\ N_2 &= N_1 \times \frac{T_1}{T_2} = 1450 \times \frac{20}{50} = 580 \text{ rpm} \end{aligned} $$ **Part (b):** $$ \begin{aligned} a &= 1.0\,m = 5 \text{ mm} \\ b &= 1.25\,m = 6.25 \text{ mm} \\ h &= 2.25\,m = 11.25 \text{ mm} \\ h_w &= 2.0\,m = 10.0 \text{ mm} \\ c &= 0.25\,m = 1.25 \text{ mm} \\ t &= \frac{\pi m}{2} = 7.854 \text{ mm} \\ d_{b1} &= mT_1 \cos 20° = 5 \times 20 \times 0.9397 = 93.97 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Diametral Pitch / Mixed System** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** A pair of spur gears is to be designed with a diametral pitch of 4 teeth/inch. The pinion has 18 teeth and the gear has 45 teeth. Calculate: **(a)** (7 marks) The module, pitch circle diameters, and center distance (in mm). **(b)** (7 marks) The addendum, dedendum, circular pitch (in mm), tooth thickness, and check the law of gearing. Take $\phi = 20°$. #### Model Solution: **Given:** $P_d = 4$ teeth/inch, $T_1 = 18$, $T_2 = 45$, $\phi = 20°$. **Part (a):** $$ \begin{aligned} m &= \frac{25.4}{P_d} = \frac{25.4}{4} = 6.35 \text{ mm} \\ d_1 &= m T_1 = 6.35 \times 18 = 114.3 \text{ mm} \\ d_2 &= m T_2 = 6.35 \times 45 = 285.75 \text{ mm} \\ C &= \frac{m(T_1 + T_2)}{2} = \frac{6.35 \times 63}{2} = 200.025 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Part (b):** $$ \begin{aligned} a &= 1.0\,m = 6.35 \text{ mm} \\ b &= 1.25\,m = 7.9375 \text{ mm} \\ P_c &= \pi m = \pi \times 6.35 = 19.948 \text{ mm} \\ t &= \frac{\pi m}{2} = 9.974 \text{ mm} \\ \text{Law of gearing check: } P_c \text{ must be same for both gears.} \\ P_{c1} &= P_{c2} = \pi m = 19.948 \text{ mm} \;\; \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Gear Terminology** - **Module ($m$):** $d = mT$ — the SI standard; **must be same for both meshing gears**. - **Circular Pitch ($P_c$):** $P_c = \pi m$ — must be same for the pair (law of gearing). - **Diametral Pitch ($P_d$):** $P_d = T/d_{\text{inch}}$; convert via $m = 25.4/P_d$. - **Addendum** = $1.0\,m$ (top of tooth). - **Dedendum** = $1.25\,m$ (depth below pitch circle; extra $0.25\,m$ is clearance). - **Whole Depth** = $2.25\,m$ | **Working Depth** = $2.0\,m$ | **Clearance** = $0.25\,m$. - **Tooth Thickness at PC** = $P_c/2 = \pi m/2$. - **Base Circle Diameter** = $d \cos\phi$ (always $\le d$). - **Outside Diameter** = $m(T+2)$ | **Root Diameter** = $m(T-2.5)$. - **Center Distance** = $m(T_1+T_2)/2$. - **Speed Ratio** $i = N_1/N_2 = T_2/T_1 = d_2/d_1$. - **Standard Pressure Angle** = $20°$ (use $14.5°$ only if specified). - **Law of Gearing:** Two gears mesh correctly only if they share the same circular pitch (and same module in SI system). - **Standard Module Series (preferred):** 1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20 mm. - **Always draw a labelled diagram** showing pitch circle, base circle, addendum circle, and pressure angle. - **Units discipline:** Write *mm* next to every length in numerical answers; KTU valuation is strict on units.

topics.title: Pressure Angle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET404 - THEORY OF MACHINES AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pressure Angle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition

In the context of **cam mechanisms** (a key topic in KTU Module 4 of *Theory of Machines and Design*), the **Pressure Angle (α)** is defined as the angle between the **direction of motion of the follower** and the **normal to the cam profile at the point of contact** (i.e., the line of transmission of force). It is a kinematic-geometric parameter that quantifies how obliquely the driving force is transmitted from the cam to the follower.

Mathematically, it is the angle between the **tangent to the pitch curve** at the point of contact and the **line perpendicular to the direction of follower translation** (or the line of follower motion).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBIET404 – Module 4):**
> Pressure angle is a critical design parameter for cam sizing, directly affecting:
> 1. Side thrust on the follower (which causes wear in the guide).
> 2. The size (base circle radius) of the cam.
> 3. The selection of follower offset.
> A cam designer must keep α within prescribed limits throughout the cycle.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are pushing a heavy trolley (the **follower**) using a long stick (the **cam profile**). The stick touches the trolley at a single point.

- If you push the stick **straight in front of you** (parallel to the trolley's direction of motion), the **pressure angle is zero** and the entire force moves the trolley efficiently.
- If the stick is **slanted sideways** when it touches the trolley, only part of your effort moves the trolley forward — the rest pushes the trolley sideways against its rails (causing friction and wear). The slant angle of the stick is exactly the **pressure angle**.

**A larger pressure angle = more wasted sideways force = harder sliding = more wear.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world intuition:** This is exactly why, in automobile engine valve cams, designers use **offset followers** or **roller followers** — both are engineering tricks to keep the pressure angle low and the system efficient.

### 1.3 Standard Allowable Pressure Angle Limits (KTU Convention)

| Configuration | Recommended Maximum α |
|---|---|
| Translating (radial / in-line) follower | **α_max ≤ 30°** |
| Translating (offset) follower | **α_max ≤ 45°** |
| Oscillating roller follower | **α_max ≤ 45°** |

> [!WARNING]
> A pressure angle exceeding these limits causes **jamming**, excessive side thrust, and premature failure of the follower-guide interface. KTU examiners frequently test these values.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Geometric Origin of Pressure Angle

Consider a disc cam rotating about a fixed axis. The follower translates such that its line of motion is offset by an eccentricity **e** from the cam's center. At any instant, the follower contacts the cam at a point where:

- The line joining the cam center **O** to the point of contact **P** has length **r** (the prime circle radius at that instant).
- The follower is at a displacement **s** from its initial position.
- The roller's center lies on a circle of radius **√(r² − rᵣ²)** (where rᵣ is the roller radius) measured from **O**, along the line **OP**.

The pressure angle is the angle between the **normal to the cam surface (line OP)** and the **direction of follower motion (line of translation)**.

### 2.2 Why and How: Physical Interpretation

- **Normal force (N)** along OP is the force the cam exerts on the follower.
- This force has two components:
  - **N cos(α)** → useful component that produces follower motion.
  - **N sin(α)** → side thrust that causes wear on the follower guide.

To maximize efficiency and minimize wear, we want **α to be as small as possible** throughout the cycle.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Configuration | Governing Equation | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Translating roller follower, **offset** (follower offset in direction of cam rotation) | $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{\dfrac{ds}{d\theta} - e}{s + \sqrt{r_{b}^{2} - e^{2}}}$ | Use this form when offset aids rise |
| 2 | Translating roller follower, **offset** (follower offset opposite to cam rotation) | $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{\dfrac{ds}{d\theta} + e}{s + \sqrt{r_{b}^{2} - e^{2}}}$ | Use this form when offset aids return |
| 3 | Translating **radial** follower (e = 0), with roller | $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{\dfrac{ds}{d\theta}}{s + \sqrt{r_{b}^{2} - e^{2}}}$ with $e=0$ | Simplifies to (ds/dθ)/(s + r_b) for knife edge |
| 4 | Translating roller follower, **knife edge** approximation (rᵣ → 0) | $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{\dfrac{ds}{d\theta} \pm e}{s + r_{b}}$ | Used in design problems when rᵣ is small |
| 5 | Maximum pressure angle location (uniform velocity) | Occurs at **s = 0** (start of stroke) | Because ds/dθ is maximum there |
| 6 | Maximum pressure angle location (SHM & cycloidal) | Occurs at intermediate s | Found by differentiating tan α = 0 |
| 7 | Base circle radius for **specified** maximum pressure angle (knife-edge, uniform velocity, radial follower) | $r_{b} = \dfrac{V \cdot h}{\tan\alpha_{\max}}$ where $V = \omega \cdot h/\beta$ | KTU design-type problem standard form |

**Notation legend:**
- $s$ = follower displacement
- $e$ = follower offset
- $r_b$ = base circle radius of cam
- $r_r$ = roller radius
- $ds/d\theta$ = first derivative of displacement w.r.t. cam angle
- $\alpha$ = pressure angle
- $h$ = total lift (stroke)
- $\beta$ = cam angle for out-stroke
- $V$ = maximum velocity during out-stroke

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

The pressure angle governs:
1. **Cam Size Optimization:** A larger permitted α allows a smaller base circle, saving material.
2. **Follower Selection:** Offset followers can reduce maximum α by up to 30%, allowing smaller cams.
3. **Jamming Prevention:** If α exceeds a critical value, the follower may self-lock (especially with flat-faced or knife-edge followers).
4. **Wear Life:** Lower α → lower side thrust → longer follower-guide life.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: General Pressure Angle for an Offset Translating Roller Follower

**Given:** A disc cam rotating with angular velocity $\omega$ in the **counter-clockwise** sense. A translating roller follower is offset by distance $e$ from the cam center. At an instant, the follower is in contact with the cam profile at a point where the displacement is $s$ and the prime circle radius is $r$.

**Setup:** Let the cam center be **O**, the point of contact be **P**, and the center of the roller be **C**. Then:
- $\vert OP \vert = r$ (prime circle radius at this instant).
- $\vert CP \vert = r_r$ (roller radius), with CP perpendicular to the cam profile at P.
- The center C lies on the line of follower motion, offset from O by perpendicular distance $e$.

**Step 1 — Velocity of the point of contact on the cam surface:**

The cam surface at P moves with velocity $v_c = \omega \cdot r$ in a direction perpendicular to **OP** (tangential to the prime circle at P).

**Step 2 — Components along the follower axis:**

The velocity $v_c$ of the cam at the contact point is resolved into two components relative to the follower's line of motion:
- Component along the **follower direction** (along OC): $v_c \cos\alpha$
- Component **perpendicular** to the follower direction: $v_c \sin\alpha$

**Step 3 — The follower must move with the same velocity component along its own axis:**

For the follower to remain in contact (no separation, no penetration), the velocity of the contact point along the follower's direction of motion must equal the follower's velocity $v_f = ds/dt$:

$$v_f = v_c \cos\alpha = \omega \cdot r \cdot \cos\alpha$$

But we also know that for a translating follower with displacement $s$:

$$r \cos\alpha = s + \sqrt{r^{2} - e^{2}} \quad \text{(from geometry of right triangle O-C-prime circle)}$$

Wait — to be more precise, we project $r$ along the follower direction. The perpendicular distance from O to the follower line is $e$. The horizontal (along follower) projection of OP is $\sqrt{r^2 - e^2}$, plus the offset along the direction of motion of the follower. Combining with the displacement $s$:

$$r \cos\alpha = s + \sqrt{r_b^{2} - e^{2}}$$

This relationship holds true because, for the **base circle**, $r = r_b$ and $s = 0$, giving $r_b \cos\alpha = \sqrt{r_b^2 - e^2}$, which is consistent with the offset geometry.

**Step 4 — Differentiate the geometric relationship w.r.t. time:**

Differentiating $r \cos\alpha = s + \sqrt{r_b^2 - e^2}$ with respect to $t$, noting that $r_b$ and $e$ are constants:

$$\frac{dr}{dt}\cos\alpha - r \sin\alpha \cdot \frac{d\alpha}{dt} = \frac{ds}{dt}$$

For a stationary analysis (instantaneous equilibrium), we recognize that the velocity of the contact point P along the follower direction must equal the follower velocity. Equivalently, using the angular velocity of the cam:

$$\frac{ds}{dt} = \omega \cdot r \sin\alpha \mp \omega \cdot e$$

The sign convention gives:
- **Minus sign** when offset is in the direction of cam rotation (offset aids rise, so the tangential component subtracts from the effective motion).
- **Plus sign** when offset is opposite to cam rotation.

**Step 5 — Eliminate trigonometric terms to obtain the pressure angle formula:**

From Step 3: $r \cos\alpha = s + \sqrt{r_b^2 - e^2}$, hence $r = (s + \sqrt{r_b^2 - e^2})/\cos\alpha$.

Substituting into Step 4:

$$\frac{ds}{dt} = \omega \cdot \frac{s + \sqrt{r_b^2 - e^2}}{\cos\alpha} \cdot \sin\alpha \mp \omega \cdot e$$

$$\frac{ds}{dt} = \omega \cdot (s + \sqrt{r_b^2 - e^2}) \tan\alpha \mp \omega e$$

Dividing both sides by $\omega$ (since $ds/d\theta = (ds/dt)/\omega$):

$$\frac{ds}{d\theta} = (s + \sqrt{r_b^2 - e^{2}})\tan\alpha \mp e$$

Solving for $\tan\alpha$:

$$\boxed{\tan\alpha = \frac{\dfrac{ds}{d\theta} \pm e}{s + \sqrt{r_{b}^{2} - e^{2}}}}$$

> **Q.E.D.** — This is the general KTU-board pressure angle equation.

### 3.2 Special Case 1: Radial (In-Line) Follower

Setting $e = 0$:

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{ds/d\theta}{s + r_b}$$

**Physical interpretation:** The denominator $s + r_b$ is the **instantaneous prime circle radius** measured along the follower direction.

### 3.3 Special Case 2: Uniform Velocity Motion — Location of Maximum α

For uniform velocity during the out-stroke of angle $\beta$ and lift $h$:

$$s = \frac{h}{\beta} \cdot \theta \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{ds}{d\theta} = \frac{h}{\beta} = \text{constant}$$

For a radial follower, $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{h/\beta}{s + r_b}$.

- $\tan\alpha$ is **maximum** when $s$ is **minimum**, i.e., at the **start of the stroke** where $s = 0$.
- Therefore: $\tan\alpha_{\max} = \dfrac{h/\beta}{r_b} = \dfrac{h}{r_b \beta}$

**Solving for the base circle radius:**

$$r_b = \frac{h}{\beta \cdot \tan\alpha_{\max}}$$

This is the standard KTU design equation for sizing a cam with uniform velocity motion.

### 3.4 Special Case 3: Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM) — Location of Maximum α

For SHM out-stroke of total angle $\beta$ and lift $h$:

$$s = \frac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\left(\frac{\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$$

$$\frac{ds}{d\theta} = \frac{\pi h}{2\beta}\sin\left(\frac{\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)$$

Substituting into the radial follower equation:

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{\dfrac{\pi h}{2\beta}\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)}{\dfrac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi\theta}{\beta}\right)\right] + r_b}$$

To find the maximum, we set $d(\tan\alpha)/d\theta = 0$. This does **not** occur at $\theta = 0$ because the SHM profile has zero velocity there. Instead, $\alpha_{\max}$ occurs at an **intermediate angle** found numerically — a common KTU numerical problem.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Pressure Angle Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cam Center O] -->|r distance| B[Contact Point P on Cam Profile]
    B -->|r_r perpendicular| C[Roller Center C]
    C -->|offset e| D[Folllower Line of Motion]
    D -->|displacement s| E[Initial Folllower Position]
    
    A -.->|perpendicular e| D
    B -.->|angle alpha| D
    B -.->|omega x r tangential velocity| F[Velocity of Cam at P]
    F -->|component omega r cos alpha along follower| G[Folllower Velocity v_f]
    F -->|component omega r sin alpha perpendicular| H[Side Thrust Component]
    
    H -->|wears guide| I[Follower Guide Wear]
    G -->|drives mechanism| J[Output Motion]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: KTU Design Procedure for Cam Sizing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given stroke h, motion profile, cam angles beta and beta_r] --> B{Choose Follower Type}
    B -->|Radial| C[Set e = 0]
    B -->|Offset| D[Choose e from experience]
    C --> E[Compute ds/dtheta expressions]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Apply tan alpha formula]
    F --> G{Locate alpha max}
    G -->|Uniform velocity| H[alpha max at s = 0]
    G -->|SHM Cycloidal| I[alpha max at intermediate theta]
    H --> J[Solve for r_b from tan alpha max = prescribed value]
    I --> J
```
